London, Ohio

Luke Series

============================================= === SERMON: message(0).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 9/25/2021 2:14:09 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message FIRST IMPRESSIONS Luke 1:1-4 INTRODUCTION A Nation of Christians? 2 Corinthians 11:3 Galatians 1:6 few Christians realize the apostles were already dealing with groups inventing new versions of Christ in the First Century. From last week: Luke as Physician Colossians 4:14 Historian: much of what he wrote is unique to his gospel; almost half 35 miracles; 7 only in Luke About 50 parables; 19 only in Luke (depending on definition of parable) 30 events in Jesus life only in Luke Estimated 59% is peculiar to Luke Theologian: an overview of his writing Key verse: 19:10 Luke 1:1-2:52 = 30 yrs of Jesus life; Luke 3:1 - 19:28 = 3 yrs of Jesus life; Luke19:29 - 24:53 = 1 week of Jesus life. PROLOGUE to the Gospel Luke 1:1-4 Before he settles into his letter Customary to the time to explain and justify their work Assuring the reliability of what they are about to read It is also written in classical Greek; demonstrate the high level of the writer The seriousness of what is being presented Possible idea that the letter would receive broad circulation This is Luke s Utmost for the highest A form used by the greatest Greek historians Remember, this is a Greek writing to Greeks. Began in a manner of attention getter. The SOURCES for the Gospel Luke1:1-2 Previous written an oral testimonies We know he had access to Matthew s and Mark s No indication he was critical of what had been presented He was not out to correct previous accounts That is not to say that misinformation had not been circulated It does present his material as consistent with the others Luke was not satisfied with anyone else s story. He had to have his own Real religion is not second-hand stuff. It is a personal discovery to set in order compile, arrange Not a strict chronological presentation There are times Luke was present something to contribute to his theme So an event may be out of the time-line with another gospel narrative declaration literally: to lead the way through The idea is thoroughness And it is singular: there is one narrative which the many have directed their attention which have been accomplished among us fulfilled among us; KVJ believed We have full proof of; to carry out fully; completely assure Perfect tense: the things had been accomplished or fulfilled in the past and are still in existence. among us nothing took place in a corner or in secret but out in the open for all to see THOUGHT - God wants lost people to see His life giving Son among them. How can we do that? Dying daily to self; live by the Spirit John 16:14 He will glorify Me, for He will take of what is Mine and declare it to you How does the Holy Spirit glorify Jesus today? Through your life lived in the power of the Spirit for the glory of God What is the gospel according to you? Question: what was fully accomplished? God fulfilled His plan among men; sending His own Son to the Savior of sinners These fulfilled events from the past continue to color how one should see the present. The effect of Jesus life, death, and resurrection lives on Thus Luke s writing of Acts Vincent adds that "The word is chosen to indicate that these events happened in accordance with a preconceived design." MacArthur: The gospels do not relate the story of a misunderstood ethical teacher, a failed social revolutionary, a model of selfless humility, or even a heroic martyr; they reveal the Savior who is God incarnate, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world J C Ryle - Christianity is a religion built upon facts. Let us never lose sight of this. It came before mankind at first in this shape. The first preachers did not go up and down the world, proclaiming an elaborate, artificial system of abstruse doctrines and deep principles. They made it their first business to tell men great plain facts. They went about telling a sin-laden world, that the Son of God had come down to earth, and lived for us, and died for us, and risen again. The Gospel, at its first publication, was far more simple than many make it now. It was neither more nor less than the history of Christ. V. 2 just as those who from the beginning were eyewitnesses and ministers of the word delivered them to us Luke is stressing the validity of the facts handed down to him 1 Corinthians 15:3 refers to the passing on of a tradition which is based on the testimonies of those who saw and heard Jesus which substantiates the truth and trustworthiness of the message. Luke was secondary, not a primary eyewitness We recognize some traditions handed down as nothing more than old wives fables Total unreliable Not so here. Luke has access to both oral and written sources Luke s use of source material in no way negates the inspiration of the Holy Spirit MacArthur: The process of inspiration never bypassed or overrode the personalities, life experiences, vocabularies, or writing styles of the Bible s human authors; their unique traits are indelibly stamped on all the books of Scripture. The Spirit used Luke s knowledge, gave him additional information, guided his selection of material, and controlled every word so that he wrote exactly what God wanted written Luke did the research. Inspiration was not God magically transcending human minds and faculties. That s the wish of college students His resources include those who were Jesus from the beginning Setting the stage with the birth of John the Baptist Properly called An Inspired Autopsy Eyewitness: autopsy literally self seeing seeing something for oneself The Gospel of Luke is like a carefully performed "autopsy" of Jesus' life, death, burial and resurrection. We can trust what we read Luke is a physician but he knows that here he is dealing with the health of souls, not bodies, and thus he gives utmost attention to the details, even as he likely did as a physician. Ministers of the Word: under-rowers; oarsman; galley slaves (20x NT) One who is under the service of another Luke 4:20 These were the men down in the ship's, doing one thing -- rowing and with their eyes on one man, the man standing at the front of the hull, shouting "Row,Row, Row."! Why? Because if anyone gets out of synch, the whole boat is thrown off course! What a picture -- There are no big "I's" or little "u's" in the ministry! Servants of Who? Of Christ! A God-called preacher marches to the beat of a different drummer and if he doesn't he's not worth being in the pulpit. Servants of the word describes these men as focused on the word, listening and acting according to the word. "They not only had personal knowledge of the facts but also practical experience of the facts." Word the gospel Acts 6:4; 8:4; 12:24 Summary of verse 1, 2 (Darrell Bock) Luke has done his investigation He went back to the beginning He thoroughly studied everything He took great care to put it in an orderly account FIRST IMPRESSIONS Luke 1:1-4 Key verse: 19:10 Luke 1:1-2:52 = 30 years of Jesus life Luke 3:1 - 19:28 = 3 years of Jesus life Luke19:29 - 24:53 = 1 week of Jesus life PROLOGUE to the Gospel Luke 1:1-4 This is Luke s utmost for the highest The SOURCES for the Gospel Luke1:1-2 Real religion is a personal discovery to set in order arrange Not a strict chronological presentation narrative The idea is thoroughness nothing took place in a corner or in secret John 16:14 What is the gospel according to you? God fulfilled His plan among men The Gospel was neither more nor less than the history of Christ Luke is stressing the validity of the facts handed down to him Luke was secondary, not a primary eyewitness Eyewitness: autopsy Ministers of the Word: under-rowers One who is under the service of another Word the gospel Acts 6:4; 8:4; 12:24 Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(1).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 9/30/2021 8:01:29 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message THE PROLOGUE TO THE GOSPEL The Sources for the Gospel Luke 1:1, 2 INTRODUCTION Warren Wiersbe - If ever a man wrote a book filled with good news for everybody, Dr. Luke is that man. His key message is, For the Son of man is come to seek and to save that which was lost (Luke 19:10 + ). He presents Jesus Christ as the compassionate Son of man, who came to live among sinners, love them, help them, and die for them. In this Gospel you meet individuals as well as crowds, women and children as well as men, poor people as well as rich people, and sinners along with saints. It s a book with a message for everybody, because Luke s emphasis is on the universality of Jesus Christ and His salvation: good tidings of great joy, which shall be to all people (Luke 2:10 + ). (Bible Exposition Commentary) The Research for the Gospel Luke 1:3 In the Luke 1:1-2 Luke refers to writers and witnesses, but now he begins to describe his own writing. The Amplified translation emphasizes Luke's desire to pen an authoritative and historically reliable gospel "It seemed good and desirable to me, [and so I have determined] also after having searched out diligently and followed all things closely and traced accurately the course from the highest to the minutest detail from the very first, to write an orderly account for you, most excellent Theophilus." Remember: Luke is not being critical of previous accounts He is convinced he has something to add because of his research His emphasis is on historical accuracy "having had perfect understanding of all things from the very first." NKJV Perfect exactly, perfectly Understanding: to follow near; "means to follow closely, and hence to trace accurately often giving details that helped identify the historical context of the events he described (Luke 1:5 ; Lk 2:1 + , 2; Lk 3:1 , 2 + ; Lk 13:1 4 + ) what we learn? His research took him back to the beginning; the angel to Zacharias and Mary His research included all things His research was done with precision; having had perfect Luke carefully investigated the life of Christ; eyewitnesses; oral and written reports Carefully combined his research and then the Holy Spirit used his talents as an investigator to record exactly what the Holy Spirit wanted. We are not speaking of the dictation theory of inspiration It does mean the inspiration of the Holy Spirit does not preclude the use of careful scholarship on Luke s part. Note of interest to Bible students: 1:2 beginning = arche; commencement; first in time 1:3 from the very first = from above; John 3:31; 19:11 Jn. 3:3, 7 again While he used earthly resources the Holy Spirit used those resources Luke claims a heavenly origin to his gospel The orderly account as we said last week does not necessarily mean a strict chronological presentation There is a logical, systematic nature to Luke s writings to present salvation through the Lord Jesus Christ most excellent Theopolis Friend of God, beloved of God; a real person of some distinction Most excellent is missing from Acts introduction Most likely a Gentile office holder; Acts 23:26 (Felix); 26:25 (Festus) The Purpose for the Gospel Luke 1:4 that you may know the certainty of those things in which you were instructed. that introduces a purpose clause; in order that Here is amplification of what Theophilos had already been taught Luke wants to give Theophilus a bedrock certainty by supplying him with a historically verified and completely accurate account of the life of Jesus. He does not just want him to know about Jesus, but to truly and deeply and personally know Jesus To know fully, to know with certainty, to become thoroughly acquainted with Safety, security, firmness Reinforces the accuracy of what Theophilos is about to read The assurance is in the diligent way Luke sought for and handles his resources you were instructed you have been taught We do not know if Theophilos was a new convert or a God-fearing Gentile not yet converted English word catechism; literally to sound down on or into the ears This word speaks of systematic teaching which is the basis of the critical discrimination and discernment. CONCLUSION Luke 1:1-4 is the prologue, preface to his letter to Theophilos Luke the physician, historian, theologian, and pastor took great pains to research the elements of the life of Jesus to present to one man so that he may rest confidently in the faith. J. C. Philpot wrote, "Right views concerning Christ are indispensable to a right faith, and a right faith is indispensable to salvation. To stumble at the foundation, is, concerning faith, to make shipwreck altogether; for as Immanuel, God with us, is the grand Object of faith, to err in views of His eternal Deity, or to err in views of His sacred humanity, is alike destructive (cited in Free Grace Broadcaster, Fall 1997, p. 19) Here we have an inspired autopsy, the testimony of the under rowers, and the systematic teaching so that will benefit our faith in the grace and knowledge of the Lord Jesus. FIRST IMPRESSIONS THE PROLOGUE TO THE GOSPEL The Sources for the Gospel Luke 1:1, 2 The Research for the Gospel Luke 1:3 His emphasis is on historical accuracy His research took him back to the beginning His research included all things His research was done with precision 1:3 from the very first = from above; John 3:3, 7 Luke claims a heavenly origin to his gospel Most likely a Gentile office holder; Acts 23:26; 26:25 The Purpose for the Gospel Luke 1:4 To know fully, to know with certainty you were instructed catechism systematic teaching Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(2).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 10/9/2021 12:39:27 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 INTRODUCTION The announcement of the birth of John Luke 1:5-25 The parents of John Luke 1:5-7 The promise of John Luke 1:8-23 The prospect of John Luke 1:24-25 Note the time period: in the days of Herod, king of Judah It was a dark period for Israel. The northern kingdom fell in 722 B.C.; southern in 586 B.C. They were under the thumb of Babylon, Syria, Greece, Rome for all those years Not a free people; longing to be delivered; looking for a Messiah who they thought would bring a military victory Some had made peace with the opposition; used friendship with Rome to advance their place in life, e.g. tax collectors, Sadducees Not only dark politically but dark spiritually It was a time of called the 400 silent years; 400 years since a prophet in the land 400 years since thus saith the LORD Heaven was closed; God had forgotten them That understanding makes the conduct of Zachariah and Elizabeth all the more compelling. Why be upright in practice and character to a God who is silent? days of Herod This is part of Luke s historical accuracy Herod I; or Herod the Great or Herod the murderer He was an Edomite; Idumean; a natural enemy of Israel Born in 73 B.C. he became king of Judea in 40 B.C. He removed some threatening bands of robbers in Galilee to gain some Jewish support He brought Jerusalem under his control in 37 B.C. He married a girl with Maccabean blood and even returned some tax money to the people to further gain Jewish support. But he was a killer of wives, children, friends; anyone thought a threat He even ordered the death of some in prison upon his death so at least there would be some mourning when he died which was in 4 B.C. And, of course, Herod made the decree to kill all male babies under the age of 2 It was a time when hope was hard to find. What one writer described: Today looked like yesterday and tomorrow looks no different than today (Roger Helle) Now, I was not alive in 5 B.C. or about the time of Luke 1. (Herod died 4 B.C.) But I suggest we also live in a day where hope is hard to find. Our country is in a world of hurt and the present administration still has over 3 years to really mess it up. And their agenda both present and proposed is heart-breaking. Politicians commit crimes and are never punished. Media elites lie habitually with no pushback. The legal system is corrupt and used to destroy the opposition. We could spend the morning making a list of grievances. It would accomplish nothing except to makes us feel justified in our attitudes. And as Ben Shapiro wrote: the problem will not be solved by having the right person with the right principles in the White House. Such a belief says that government is not the problem but the solution. They tell us that is the darkest just before dawn. The last book of the OT as God went silent says: But unto you that fear my name shall the Sun of righteousness arise with healing in his wings Malachi 4:2 Zacharias himself said about his son, To give light to them that sit in darkness and in the shadow of death, to guide our feet into the way of peace. (1:79) What can we learn from Luke 1? The culture of Luke 1 makes the conduct of these parents all the more impressive Note the words used: 1:6 righteous, blameless Other translations: spiritually spotless, good people, upright, without reproach, irreproachable, scrupulously exact, faultless, faithful I maintain they were blameless because they had not children Nothing like children to bring out the sin nature Have a child act up in public: parents react because of pride and embarrassment how dare you make me look like a bad parent? More interest than child s behavior Zacharias: Yahweh has remembered When you thought God had forgotten; what a contrast to Herod of the course of Abijah 24 divisions within the priesthood; 1 Chron. 24; Abijah being the 8th. Does not mean that Zacharias was a direct descendent After the Babylonian captivity, only 4 divisions returned; Ezra 2:36-38 To keep the tradition they were divided up into 24 courses, divisions, daily order Each division served 2x per year and as there were about 18,000 priests; just short of 1,000 per division, the opportunity to burn incense was slim Elizabeth: God is my oath; also a descendent of Aaron A priest might marry only a woman of absolutely pure Jewish lineage. It was specially meritorious to marry a woman who was also a descendant of Aaron, as was Elizabeth, the wife of Zacharias. MacArthur - "Priests were expected to marry an Israelite woman who was a virgin (cf. Lev. 21:7 , 14 ; Ezek. 44:22 ). Zacharias went beyond that, however, and chose his wife from the daughters of Aaron. Since all qualified male descendants of Aaron were priests (Ex. 29:9 ; 40:13-15 ; Lev. 21:17-23 ; Num. 3:3 ; 18:7 ), she was familiar with the priesthood; most of the men in her family would have been priests." Here are two wonderful examples of OT saints; the godly remnant They are an example of how an OT saint was saved righteous before God in the sight of God They were declared righteous because of their faith Just like Abraham Galatians 3:6-9 Romans 3:25, 26 J. R. Miller: The "time of Herod" were not times when it was easy to be godly. The times were ungodly and the prevailing spirit was unrighteous. The holy lives which we find here in this story are like lamps shining in the darkness. Amid the almost universal corruption of the priesthood, and the hypocrisy of the Pharisees; this old priest and his wife lived in piety and godly simplicity. The lesson is, that we may be holy and may live saintly lives though everyone around us is evil. We need not be like those among whom we live. No matter how corrupt the times, or how unholy the influences, we ought always to strive to be holy and pure. Please note: this couple were righteous before God probably not before men They were old, she was barren; their lives were a reproof to their generation Walking blamelessly they walked according to their position It is not they walked and they declared They weren t perfect Ps. 19:7 the law of the LORD is perfect, converting the soul They were righteous, they were blameless There were walking; more to say of their direction than their perfection And what a joy to be walking together Ecclesiastes 4:9-12 Amos 3:3 CONCLUSION From the Like Application Bible: Barton adds that they did not "merely go through the motions in following God's laws; they backed up their outward compliance with inward obedience. Unlike the religious leaders whom Jesus called hypocrites, Zechariah and Elizabeth did not stop with the letter of the law. Their obedience was from the heart, and that is why God viewed them as righteous. Does your life reflect careful obedience and high regard for God's will? As your life shows your love for God, others will be drawn to him Paul s prayer: 1 Thessalonians 3:11-13 without blame James 5:7, 8 looking to Jesus IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 The Announcement of the Birth of John Luke 1:5-25 The Parents of John Luke 1:5-7 It was a dark period for Israel the 400 silent years Herod I; or Herod the Great Malachi 4:2 Zacharias: Yahweh has remembered 1 Chronicles 24 24 divisions within the priesthood Ezra 2:36-38 Elizabeth: God is my oath They were declared righteous because of their faith Galatians 3:6-9 Walking blamelessly They walked according to their position Ecclesiastes 4:9-12 Amos 3:3 1 Thessalonians 3:11-13 James 5:7, 8 Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(3).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 10/16/2021 11:30:04 AM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 Introduction Last week we looked at the sterling character of Zacharias and Elizabeth especially in light of the world in which they were living. 400 years of silence from God and the world and Judaism was growing dark. I spent some time speaking of the dark days in which we live. I am not assuming it has never been this bad but it has never been this bad in my lifetime in the US. Note his story from ACLJ: In one case we're fighting right now, a Christian realtor was targeted, harassed, and discriminated against by a state agency for putting a Bible verse (John 3:16) on her website and including "Jesus loves you" in her email signature. She's been forced to quit her job. In fact, her former employer was essentially ordered to blacklist her and report on her "religious" conduct to the state agency. The Announcement of the Birth of John 1:5-15 The Parents of John 1:5-7 Zacharias: Yahweh has remembered Elizabeth: God is my oath Righteous and blameless Barren and Old 1:7 Righteous and blameless but what looks like not blessed at all Barrenness was looked upon as a sign of disfavor Lev. 20:20, 21; Jer. 22:30 1:25 Elizabeth herself called it a disgrace; reproach, taunting Surgeon on barrenness - We do not, at the present time, understand the anguish which filled the heart of an Eastern woman who had no child. It was considered to be a disgrace, and many suffered very bitterly on that account; as did Hannah, and Rachel, and others besides. Plenty of OT examples: Sarah, Ge 18:11 ; Rebekah, Ge 25:21 ; Rachel, Ge 29:31 ; Samson's mother, Jug 13:2, 5; Hannah, 1 Sa 1:1--2:36 J. C. Ryle: Let us remember this, if we serve Christ, and let us count trial no strange thing. Let us rather believe that a hand of perfect wisdom is measuring out all our portion, and that when God chastises us, it is to make us partakers of his holiness. Heb. 12:10 were both stricken in years KJV NKJV: well advanced in years Wycliffe: had gone far in their days Child bearing is now impossible 1:37 quoted from Genesis 18:4 God is the director of this divine drama. He is doing something special to advance His agenda What is the chief end of man? To glorify God and enjoy Him forever That is why you are still here regardless of your age Psalm 92:12-15 the truly godly man under all adversities retains the joy of his soul, and continues to make progress in the divine life planted in the house protected, produce even in trying seasons; Spiritually as special place of communion with the Lord All depends on our abiding in Christ John 15:5 2 Cor. 4:16 Barren and Not Bitter Here is a woman who is old and barren What about the person who serves God and sees no results Zach and Liz still served faithfully, obediently; they still loved God The record states they were righteous and blameless Scripture states they prayed. Were they still praying or resigned themselves to being childless? They knew they were old. They knew the biological clock had sounded. The challenge is to be faithful to God and leave the results to Him Don t give up. It s too soon to quit. One old guy says: they say I am going downhill. That s a life. Life is always going up; that s why it s such hard work. God moves in a mysterious way His wonders to perform; He plants His footsteps in the sea, and rides upon the storm. Deep in unfathomable mines of never-failing skill, He treasures up His bright designs, and works His sovereign will. Ye fearful saints, fresh courage take, the clouds ye so much dread, are big with mercy, and shall break In blessings on your head. Judge not the Lord by feeble sense, but trust Him for His grace; Behind a frowning providence, He hides a smiling face. His purposes will ripen fast, unfolding every hour; The bud may have a bitter taste, but sweet will be the flower. Blind unbelief is sure to err, and scan his work in vain; God is His own interpreter, and He will make it plain. * William Cowper (1731-1800) TRANSITION So that is Luke s historical background record of the Parents of John The Promise of John Luke 1:8-23 it came to pass (KJV) so it was (NKJV) now it happened (WEB) Not an accident; not happen by chance; Zach just happened to be on temple duty This is a providential act of God R. C. Sproul: God does not roll the dice Providence is God s gracious oversight of the universe (Ray Pritchard) Providence is one aspect of His grace 5 statements about His Providence: He upholds all things He governs all things He directs everything to its appointed end He does this all the time and in every circumstance He does it always for His own glory. while he was serving as priest before God His disappointment did not keep him from serving He waited all his life for a child; He waited all his life to be in this position This is the blessedness of duty. You never know when God may step in and interrupt your plans, your life. This is our example of steadfast service Paul: 1 Corinthians 15:58 (WEB) Therefore, my beloved brothers, be (present tense) steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the Lord's work, because you know that your labor is not in vain in the Lord. the order of his division his order of Abijah (1:5) ((24 orders rotate service twice per year with a little less than 1K priests in each order)) 1:9 he was chosen by lot the opportunity to enter the temple was slim; once in a lifetime There was an orderly arrangement appointed by God God is behind the scenes and controls the scenes He is behind. Proverb 16:33 says "The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD." Flipping a coin, drawing a name out of a hat, drawing straws A tie in voting: At the local level, ties happen fairly frequently: In 2012, a city council election in Walton, Kentucky, that tied at 669 votes each was decided by a coin toss; in 2014, candidates in Poplarville, Mississippi, who had tied 177-177 in an alderman s race drew straws to determine the winner; and this year, candidates drew cards from a deck to pick the winner in a tied city council race (Huffington Puffington Post) God is in charge of each. We can trust Him. God does not make mistakes. This is His gracious oversight of the universe Sure it is ugly out there. But it is also an exciting time. What is God going to do next? Will there be an exposure of the lies and deceit? Will there be another awakening? Are these the final days for the US? Will the rapture of the church really happen in our lifetime? All of you can point to a time of a chance encounter; just happen to run into somebody Just happened to cross paths. There are plenty of testimonies from people who speak of a sudden change of schedule; or an unexpected delay that kept them from a terrible accident; kept them from being in the twin towers in NY on 9/11. God had 5 B.C. on His calendar when the world began. It was no accident Zacharias was in the temple. It is no accident your position in life now. Somethings can truly be your own fault. E.g. lack of spiritual disciplines But through it all, you can trust Jesus. Tears, sorrows, questions, unanswered prayers. Zacharias and Elizabeth had them. But nothing altered their position of being blameless before God. IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 The Announcement of the Birth of John 1:5-15 The Parents of John 1:5-7 Barren and Old 1:7 Leviticus 20:20, 21; Jeremiah 22:30 Genesis 18:11 Genesis 25:21 Genesis 29:31 1 Samuel 1:1--2:36 God is the director of this divine drama Psalm 92:12-15 Barren and Not Bitter The challenge is to be faithful to God and leave the results to Him God moves in a mysterious way His wonders to perform; The Promise of John Luke 1:8-23 This is a providential act of God Providence is God s gracious oversight of the universe His disappointment did not keep him from serving 1 Corinthians 15:58 God is behind the scenes and controls the scenes He is behind. Proverbs 16:33 Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(4).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 11/6/2021 7:07:44 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message If you can put a slide for the tabernacle would be great. IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS 1:5-56 The Announcement of the Birth of John 1:5-25 The Parents of John 1:5-7 THE PROMISE OF JOHN Luke 1:8-23 "God is behind the scenes and controls the scenes He is behind!" Proverb 16:33 says "The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD." Visiting Angel For Zachariah: Dumbfounded Burning Incense; twice a day, morning and evening For priest: one time in life opportunity; if at all Possibly evening or Sabbath Day because of the crowd; (temple picture) Psalm 141:1; Exodus 30:34-38; Revelation 8:3, 4 Priest: dressed in a white linen tunic and flower-shaped cap Entered barefoot into the inner court of the temple Taking burning coals from the brazen altar he spread them on the golden altar On them he places a censer of special incense made from essential oils The smoke of the incense billowed up as a symbol of the prayers of the people The priest was the representative of the people Lighfoot: When the priest came in unto the holy place to offer incense, notice was given to all, by the sound of a little bell, that the time of prayers was now. Spurgeon - While he, in the inner shrine, was burning incense, the multitudes in the outer courts were engaging in prayer. I think that is a very beautiful symbol,-the priest unseen, like the Lord Jesus Christ in the Holy of Holies above, and the mass of the people engaged in prayer while the unseen priest is offering the sacred perfume before the altar of Jehovah. Morning and Evening Prayer: Might that be a challenge for us as a church. It is the praying church that sees God s power at work Proper to note that God selected the temple, the holiest spot in Jewish worship, to begin the most significant series of events that would lead to the advent of the Messiah. Wiersbe comment: God often speaks to His people and calls them while they are busy doing their daily tasks. Both Moses and David were caring for sheep, and Gideon was threshing wheat. Peter and his partners were mending nets when Jesus called them. It is difficult to steer a car when the engine is not running 1:12 reaction to angel Angels are fascinating creatures; they easily capture the imagination Luke likes them: he refers to them 46 times in his writings; 23 in his gospel They are active in the ministry of Jesus and salvation This was a real angel; not hallucinating over incense Troubled : agitated Fear : terror shaken and overwhelmed with fear shaken and stirred Think about this: Here is an older gentleman who has waited all his life for the privilege of participating in this service. He has listened to the others tell of their experience. He has diligently prepared himself. He has pictured his movements while lying in bed. But with all his preparation, no one prepared him for this. Were his eyes closed in prayer? Did he sense a presence? Was there a glorious light? What he did know: the angel s appearance could have meant divine judgment A quick reminder we are not fit for heaven righteous and blameless but still a sinner The vision of an inhabitant of heaven reminds us forcibly of our own imperfection, and of our natural unfitness to stand before God. If angels are so great and terrible, what must the Lord of angels be? I do believe that when you are taken to heaven, there will be no fear. But I also believe that the wicked have everything to fear. Terror for the wicked with be forever. A high level of fear ultimately became the greatest blessing of his life From Angel: Direct Right side of altar: no particular meaning; only indicating where he was No further description do not be afraid stop being afraid these words breaks 400 years of silence 1 Samuel 3:1 the word of the Lord was rare in those days 400 silent years 500 years since the last heavenly visitor to the 3 Hebrew fellows in the furnace 500 years since a miracle 800 years since the last season of miracles from Elijah and Elisha your prayer not specific Probably two-fold Probably not praying for a child; ship had sailed; duty was for the nation The most common prayer was that God would visit His people with salvation through the Messiah And God had not forgotten their prayer for a son Matthew Henry Prayers are filed in heaven, & are not forgotten though the thing prayed for is not presently given us. The time as well as the thing is the answer; and God s gift always transcends the measure of the promise. The two prayers, son and Messiah, are directly related. For the Messiah was to have a fore-runner Gladness : exultation that makes one feel like jumping for joy About Child: Dedicated great in the sight of the Lord whatever men might think of him, the Lord would recognize his greatness Nazarite: ordinary folk who took this vow to express their separation from the world and their dedication to the Lord and His will Anyone could take this vow for a short time as a testimony. But is was a special mark of God s calling for anyone to be make a Nazarite for life as John was. Being filled with the Holy Spirit from birth was, at this time, most unusual The Holy Spirit filled a number of people in the OT. But these experiences were temporary; the person was filled for the specific work to which he was called. 16, 17 twofold work *sin and unbelief separated many from the Lord John s separated life would be a testimony reminding them of God s just requirements and turning many back to Him. *sin and unbelief also brought dissension and disobedience in homes and communities Ministry of Elijah: call a confused people to a decision Pharisees made impossible demands on them Sadducees denied the reality of angels, spirits, and resurrection Herodians were cozying up to the Romans Essence withdrew to develop their own doctrines IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS 1:5-56 The Announcement of the Birth of John 1:5-25 The Parents of John 1:5-7 THE PROMISE OF JOHN Luke 1:8-23 Proverb 16:33 For Zachariah: Dumbfounded Psalm 141:1; Exodus 30:34-38; Revelation 8:3, 4 Troubled : agitated Fear : terror shaken and overwhelmed with fear From Angel: Direct 400 silent years 500 years since the last heavenly visitor to the 3 Hebrew fellows in the furnace 500 years since a miracle 800 years since the last season of miracles from Elijah and Elisha The two prayers, son and Messiah, are directly related. Gladness : exultation Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(5).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 11/13/2021 11:41:16 AM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS 1:5-56 The Announcement of the Birth of John 1:5-25 The Parents of John 1:5-7 Old age is about stripping away the lies in order to teach us the truth. 1) The lie says life is a bell-curve - up, then inevitably down. 2) Scripture teaches that life is meant to be up, all the way to heaven. There is a goal, and the goal determines the process we must go through to get there. Whatever is valued in heaven grows more and more valuable on earth. Whatever doesn't matter in heaven, matters less and less on earth. The longer you live, the more it is so. THE PROMISE OF JOHN Luke 1:8-23 Proverb 16:33 Zachariah: Dumbfounded Psalm 141:1; Exodus 30:34-38; Revelation 8:3, 4 Troubled : agitated Fear : terror shaken and overwhelmed with fear Angel: Direct Answer to two prayers: Messiah and child John the Baptist Dedicated Named by the angel: The Lord is gracious John was to usher in the age of grace His parents had suffered some degree of disgrace; 1:25 1 Peter 5:10 After you have suffered for a little while , the God of all grace, who called you to His eternal glory in Christ, will Himself perfect, confirm, strengthen and establish you. To Him be dominion forever and ever. Amen. Circumstance of his birth: gladness exultation; makes one feel like jumping for joy Joy and gladness are also hallmarks of the kingdom age. Isaiah 25:9 Isaiah used us Jews; messianic talk Isaiah 34:1, 2 Many shall rejoice John s birth would draw the attention of the godly to what God was about to do; heighten the expectation of the Messiah many but not all for some the coming of Light will reveal their true darkness John 3:19, 20 Why such joy and gladness? 1:15 great in the sight of the Lord used in v. 6 of parents; before Dedicated to His walk; his walk before God Whatever men might think of him, the Lord would recognize his greatness That s what every parent wishes for the child; some level of greatness; and nothing greater than a faithful walk before God What other kind of greatness even matters? Do I value God s opinion of me more than man s? Am I a man-pleaser of God-pleaser? Colossians 1:9, 10 Scripture always links greatness with humility. Prov. 15:33 before honor is humility You can t be great with God unless you are right with God. Nazarite: drink neither wine or strong drink Leaves some question for us: was John a Nazarite? (Num. 16; Judges 13:4, 5) No mention of cutting hair or having this position of entire life; natural to assume D. L. Moody said that "God doesn't seek for golden vessels, and does not ask for silver ones, but He must have clean ones." 2 Timothy 2:21 What we can say is that John was unique and possessed characteristics of both a prophet and a Nazarite. Dedicated to his role filled with the Holy Spirit before his birth unique designation The main reason John would be great The only person in the NT so spoken of The HS filled a number of people in the OT These experiences were temporary; the person was filled for the specific work to which he was called John would be filled and stay filled from the very beginning of his life Here is our call to be Nazarite-like; Ephesians 5:18 I yield, the HS controls 1:16, 17 two-fold work beginning in Luke 3 John s separated life would be a testimony reminding them of God s just requirements and turning many back to God Religion had become an empty form to many. More focus on earthly advancement People turn back because they have turned away Again we are reminded: many but not all His own received Him not The same sin and unbelief also brought dissension and disobedience in homes and communities. People had neglected God s Word The finer points of religious ceremony were ignored Many had given up even trying to live up to the stipulations imposed by the leaders Parents had little hope that either they or their children could really please God Fathers had given up on trying to train their children John s ministry would restore country and homes It would bring the disobedient to a place where they would respond to the wisdom of the just and make them a people ready to receive the promised Messiah Dedicated to his calling John s ministry would be like Elijah s; bold and uncompromising The people of Elijah s day were confused; trying to figure out who or what to serve They had Jehovah; but they also had Jezebel and Baal worship God sent Elijah to call the people to a decision 1 Kings 18 The people of John s ministry were also confused Pharisees made impossible demands on them Sadducees denied the reality of angels, spirits, and the resurrection Herodians curried Roman favor Essenes withdrew into the desert to develop their own doctrines. John would direct them back to God in repentance Jesus summarized John s ministry this way: Luke 7:28 John was the greatest of the OT prophets Jesus went on: he that is least in the kingdom is greater than John Jesus was not diminishing John but explaining why John asked perplexing questions: vs. 19, 20 John had not yet entered the kingdom; his knowledge was still limited The average believer today is not greater than John in power or character Jesus meant that those living in the new age of the Holy Spirit have greater advantages than John possessed. The humblest believer can testify to the finished work of Jesus John s ministry was promise Jesus ministry was fulfillment Our ministry is execution; finish; completion IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 The Announcement of John 1:5-25 The Parents of John 1:5-7 THE PROMISE OF JOHN Luke 1:8-23 Zachariah: Dumbfounded Angel: Direct John the Baptist Dedicated Isaiah 25:9 Isaiah 34:1, 2 many but not all John 3:19, 20 Dedicated to His walk Colossians 1:9, 10 2 Timothy 2:21 Dedicated to his role turning many back to God John s ministry would restore country and homes Dedicated to his calling bold and uncompromising 1 Kings 18 Luke 7:28 The humblest believer can testify to the finished work of Jesus Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(6).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 11/19/2021 8:02:19 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 The Announcement of John 1:5-25 The Parents of John 1:5-7 The Promise of John 1:8-25 Characters Involved Zachariah: Dumbfounded Angel: Direct John the Baptist: Dedicated Beginning in vs. 18, we look once again at Zachariah Should you like a simple outline for him: vs. 5-25 Righteous, Responsibility, Revelation, Response, Result Questioning an Angel 1:18-25 Have you ever been in a situation where you should have just kept your mouth shut? Have you ever said something and quickly put in your place? Job 40:3-5; 42:3, 5-6 Growing up we would speak of someone as deaf and dumb. The National Association of the Deaf calls that title the granddaddy of all negative labels pinned on deaf and hard of hearing people. It would easy to apply the tag to Zachariah as he has this conversation with an angel. you aren t listening and now look what happened to you We generally only think of Zachariah as loosing his ability to speak, but look at 1:62 they made signs to his father a hearing problem also But let s face it; we have all been there. God speaks through His Word. We are not listening; we are slow to hear; we read in disbelief; we doubt the Source I wonder how many of us would be struggling today if God responded in the same way. Would you be making signs? Would we be making signs to you? I am bringing no condemnation today to Zachariah; rather, I feel for him. He presents a valid argument. His only frame of reference is what he has seen. Old people don t have babies. In our day, a pregnant woman over 40 is considered by many to be at risk I believe they are least in their 60 s, if not 70 s In Luke 1, we are looking at a couple far along in years The Objection 1:18 Just one verse is all it takes to express this man s unbelief. by what shall I know this? what s he doing? His question is really a demand for a sign Now look, here is a priest well-schooled in the Scriptures His mind should have recalled: Abraham and Sarah: Genesis 15:4; 17:5, 15-17; 21:1, 2 Isaac and Rebekah: barren for 20 years, conceived twins; Gen. 25:20-26 Manoah s wife: Judges 13:2-7 We say, but Abraham questioned God; Sarah laughed Gideon and Hezekiah both asked for a sign from God No rebuke came to any of them. It goes to the attitude behind the question; one of amazement or one of unbelief Zachariah refused point blank to believe the angel. Here is a godly man in a time of unbelief. Folks, these things are written for our example and hope. We need not fret our usefulness is gone when we react similarly. Repent, deal with any consequences and go on. The Rebuttal 1:19 I emphatic; as opposed to Zachariah s I in v. 18 I want a sign I am a priest. I am ministering in the temple. I am from a priestly family. We sometimes call that a messianic complex. Lucifer: Isaiah 14 Frank Sinatra: I did it my way Obama Angel s answer: I am Gabriel ; meaning mighty one of God I stand before the face of God same before as v. 6, 15 I am simply a messenger who has come from the presence of God glad tidiings good news later used by Luke for the gospel Zachariah s refusal to believe must be seen in the light of God s condescension in sending such a messenger with such a message. There will be consequences to reject the messenger and the message. 1:20 Zachariah asked for a sign. The sign is a rebuke in 3 ways: 1. By Word Zachariah had to be ashamed for his refusal to believe. 2. By loosing the power to speak He voice unbelief; he looses the power to speak This absence of speech would speak louder than words that all the promises of God are true. 3. By the fulfillment of God s promise Elizabeth s praise after she conceived must have made her husband feel how foolish his doubts had been. The sign is an indication of a still greater truth Zachariah s dumbness was a symbol of the condition in Israel at the time 400 years since a prophetic voice; they were loosing hope in the promise of a Messiah Like Zachariah, their unbelief and backsliding had kept God from speaking to them. But God would not be silent forever. At the birth of his son, Zachariah would be able to speak. At the birth of Jesus, God would again speak to His people. The Fulfillment 1:21-25 The people were waiting. The priest generally did not spend a long time performing their job; they were deliberate and fearful of a misstep. He was to fulfill his responsibilities, come out and bless the people. They could go home knowing the work was accomplished. Typical benediction: Numbers 6:23-26 (KJV) Speak unto Aaron and unto his sons, saying, On this wise ye shall bless the children of Israel, saying unto them, The LORD bless thee, and keep thee: The LORD make his face shine upon thee, and be gracious unto thee: The LORD lift up his countenance upon thee, and give thee peace. Upon his appearance, the people knew something was up. The priest has seen something. Zachariah s fulfills the week s duties and returns home. That conversation had to be interesting. Elizabeth may have already heard of her husband s sudden inability to speak. Did she even know in advance that her husband would be in the holy place? How did he communicate the news of Gabriel? Did he have a twinkle in his eye? How do you break news like this? 1:24, 25 is all we know. Elizabeth is pregnant Seclusion for 5 months until her pregnancy was apparent to all Used her quiet time to prepare for divine responsibilities. We will soon study her thanksgiving to God for the sending of a child. She did not view her age as a stumbling block to the power of God. She now can show God s favor on her. Others can now see their foolishness in judging her walk with God. IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 THE ANNOUCEMENT OF JOHN 1:5-25 The Parents of John 1:5-7 The Promise of John 1:8-25 Questioning an Angel 1:18-25 Luke 1:62 they made signs to his father The Objection 1:18 His question is really a demand for a sign Abraham and Sarah Isaac and Rebekah Manoah s wife Here is a godly man in a time of unbelief. The Rebuttal 1:19 I want a sign I am Gabriel - mighty one of God The sign is a rebuke in 3 ways: By Word By loosing the power to speak By the fulfillment of God s promise Zachariah s dumbness was a symbol of the condition in Israel at the time The Fulfillment 1:21-25 Numbers 6:23-26 Seclusion for 5 months until her pregnancy was apparent to all She did not view her age as a stumbling block to the power of God. She now can show God s favor on her. Others can now see their foolishness in judging her walk with God. Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(7).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 11/19/2021 8:01:51 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 The Announcement of John 1:5-25 The Parents of John 1:5-7 The Promise of John 1:8-25 Characters Involved Zachariah: Dumbfounded Angel: Direct John the Baptist: Dedicated Beginning in vs. 18, we look once again at Zachariah Should you like a simple outline for him: vs. 5-25 Righteous, Responsibility, Revelation, Response, Result Questioning an Angel 1:18-25 Have you ever been in a situation where you should have just kept your mouth shut? Have you ever said something and quickly put in your place? Job 40:3-5; 42:3, 5-6 Growing up we would speak of someone as deaf and dumb. The National Association of the Deaf calls that title the granddaddy of all negative labels pinned on deaf and hard of hearing people. It would easy to apply the tag to Zachariah as he has this conversation with an angel. you aren t listening and now look what happened to you We generally only think of Zachariah as loosing his ability to speak, but look at 1:62 they made signs to his father a hearing problem also But let s face it; we have all been there. God speaks through His Word. We are not listening; we are slow to hear; we read in disbelief; we doubt the Source I wonder how many of us would be struggling today if God responded in the same way. Would you be making signs? Would we be making signs to you? I am bringing no condemnation today to Zachariah; rather, I feel for him. He presents a valid argument. His only frame of reference is what he has seen. Old people don t have babies. In our day, a pregnant woman over 40 is considered by many to be at risk I believe they are least in their 60 s, if not 70 s In Luke 1, we are looking at a couple far along in years The Objection 1:18 Just one verse is all it takes to express this man s unbelief. by what shall I know this? what s he doing? His question is really a demand for a sign Now look, here is a priest well-schooled in the Scriptures His mind should have recalled: Abraham and Sarah: Genesis 15:4; 17:5, 15-17; 21:1, 2 Isaac and Rebekah: barren for 20 years, conceived twins; Gen. 25:20-26 Manoah s wife: Judges 13:2-7 We say, but Abraham questioned God; Sarah laughed Gideon and Hezekiah both asked for a sign from God No rebuke came to any of them. It goes to the attitude behind the question; one of amazement or one of unbelief Zachariah refused point blank to believe the angel. Here is a godly man in a time of unbelief. Folks, these things are written for our example and hope. We need not fret our usefulness is gone when we react similarly. Repent, deal with any consequences and go on. The Rebuttal 1:19 I emphatic; as opposed to Zachariah s I in v. 18 I want a sign I am a priest. I am ministering in the temple. I am from a priestly family. We sometimes call that a messianic complex. Lucifer: Isaiah 14 Frank Sinatra: I did it my way Obama Angel s answer: I am Gabriel ; meaning mighty one of God I stand before the face of God same before as v. 6, 15 I am simply a messenger who has come from the presence of God glad tidiings good news later used by Luke for the gospel Zachariah s refusal to believe must be seen in the light of God s condescension in sending such a messenger with such a message. There will be consequences to reject the messenger and the message. 1:20 Zachariah asked for a sign. The sign is a rebuke in 3 ways: 1. By Word Zachariah had to be ashamed for his refusal to believe. 2. By loosing the power to speak He voice unbelief; he looses the power to speak This absence of speech would speak louder than words that all the promises of God are true. 3. By the fulfillment of God s promise Elizabeth s praise after she conceived must have made her husband feel how foolish his doubts had been. The sign is an indication of a still greater truth Zachariah s dumbness was a symbol of the condition in Israel at the time 400 years since a prophetic voice; they were loosing hope in the promise of a Messiah Like Zachariah, their unbelief and backsliding had kept God from speaking to them. But God would not be silent forever. At the birth of his son, Zachariah would be able to speak. At the birth of Jesus, God would again speak to His people. The Fulfillment 1:21-25 The people were waiting. The priest generally did not spend a long time performing their job; they were deliberate and fearful of a misstep. He was to fulfill his responsibilities, come out and bless the people. They could go home knowing the work was accomplished. Typical benediction: Numbers 6:23-26 (KJV) Speak unto Aaron and unto his sons, saying, On this wise ye shall bless the children of Israel, saying unto them, The LORD bless thee, and keep thee: The LORD make his face shine upon thee, and be gracious unto thee: The LORD lift up his countenance upon thee, and give thee peace. Upon his appearance, the people knew something was up. The priest has seen something. Zachariah s fulfills the week s duties and returns home. That conversation had to be interesting. Elizabeth may have already heard of her husband s sudden inability to speak. Did she even know in advance that her husband would be in the holy place? How did he communicate the news of Gabriel? Did he have a twinkle in his eye? How do you break news like this? 1:24, 25 is all we know. Elizabeth is pregnant Seclusion for 5 months until her pregnancy was apparent to all Used her quiet time to prepare for divine responsibilities. We will soon study her thanksgiving to God for the sending of a child. She did not view her age as a stumbling block to the power of God. She now can show God s favor on her. Others can now see their foolishness in judging her walk with God. She now can show God s favor on her. Others can now see their foolishness in judging her walk with God. Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(8).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 11/27/2021 10:15:01 AM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 THE ANNOUNCEMENT OF JOHN 1:5-25 The Parents of John 1:5-7 The Promise of John 1:8-25 INTRODUCTION One of my favorite TV shows growing up was Mission Impossible . It has been glamorized in recent years by Tom Cruise but his movies with all the present technology could not improve on the original series. No, I never saw the movies so don t come up afterwards and defend them. Some of you will remember that when the assignment had been given you heard this, In five seconds this tape will self- destruct. In Luke 1 we have already had one episode of Mission Impossible. The announcement and pregnancy of a couple well advanced in years. Now we come to the second episode, Gabriel to a young virgin who is soon to become pregnant through the work of the Holy Spirit. Miracles are God s way of introducing and substantiating His work. Creation, the Exodus, entering the Promise Land, prophetic ministries, and later the beginning of the Church age all were accompanied by the miraculous. God is gaining the attention of man even as He will do in the future. We have every reason to expect that God coming to earth in the form of a man would also see the demonstration of some incredible events that can only be credited to the power of God. THE ANNOUNCEMENT OF JESUS 1:26-38 The Messenger 1:26 The Maiden 1:27 The Message 1:28-36 Content of Message 1:28-33 Concern of Message 1:34 Q and A Compassion of Message 1:36 The Meekness 1:38 The Explanation 1:37 The Messenger 1:26 Gabriel: mighty one of God Return trip to earth but instead of Jerusalem it is Nazareth in Galilee A small rural town about 75 miles north of Jerusalem; Greek has no word for village Suggested population about this time of 400. A region of such little regard to be called Galilee of the Gentiles Can any good thing come out of Nazareth was the common understanding Only 3 angels in Scripture are mentioned by name: Lucifer, Michael, and Gabriel Michael the arch angel. He is the warrior Gabriel the messenger We will learn this trip is about 6 months since his visit to Zachariah This announcement is far more significant The Maiden 1:27 An engaged virgin; note virgin is used twice; not by mistake but for emphasis I am choosing not to spend much time defending the understanding of virgin. Mary makes note of her purity. The Holy Spirit s work also in clear enough. Charles Ryrie writes, "There is no instance where it can be proved that ?'almah designates a young woman who is not a virgin" almah is the Hebrew word W. A. Criswell: It is clear that both Matthew and the Lxx translators understood that Isaiah was speaking of a virgin when he used `almah. And this is precisely the purity which both Matthew and Luke ascribe to Mary We will spend more time with this as we discuss the genealogy, but know this: the virgin birth makes possible Christ s true humanity without inherited sin. Engaged to Joseph Engagements among the Jewish people were publicly announced in a social ceremony The engagement was considered so binding it could be broken only by legal action considered as serious as divorce. Unfaithfulness on the part of the bride was punishable by death Interaction with the angel: no dream, no vision; Mary saw and heard The Message 1:28-36 Content: common greeting Hail good morning; joy be with you Added: especially favored For our Roman Catholic friends: Hail Mary, full of grace is accurate. But any thought of Gabriel giving worship to Mary is conspicuous by its absence Mary is to be honored, but only God is to be worshipped Mary is the recipient of divine grace but not something for her to dispense to others Word is only used twice: Ephesians 1:6; of all believers; accepted Further, is Mary the mother of God? How would you answer? Besides a quick no First of all: Scripture never used the title. Two: the title implies God had a beginning. Only accurate to state Mary was the mother of the Lord Jesus Christ during His incarnation on the earth. 1:43 mother of my Lord 1:29 Mary: perplexed (1:12), puzzled, pondered A major dialogue was going on in her mind. Mary knew her humble surroundings; she knew her engagement to a poor carpenter This greeting was naturally surprising and troubling 1:30-33 Reassuring her of God s favor (Lit. favor beside God), and more info Jesus Christ came not to make life easy but to make men great. The Facts: the pregnancy of a son His name will be Jesus; identical to Hebrews Joshua; the Lord is salvation Name points to His central role: atonement from sin; restoration in relationship Matthew 1:20, 21 He would be great: mega Same word for John (v.15) but what a contrast John would be great in the sight of the Lord Jesus would be great as the Son of the Highest John would be divinely ordained to a purpose Jesus would be the unique God-man Son used in 3 ways: Mary would bring forth a human son, Jesus Son of the Highest is the title for His deity; qualities of His Father His kingly sonship is received from his father David v. 33 revelation of Kingship the throne of David: suggest Mary a descendent of David (more on that in genology) His reign would be both temporary and eternal He would reign over the whole world: Zechariah 14:9 His kingdom would be without end Concern of Message 1:34 Q and A Q: Mary does not focus on the kind of Son she would bear nor on the nature of His life s work Her serious inquiry was how she could give birth at all since she had never known a man Mary viewed the conception as immediate Euphemism as modest reference to sexual relations A: would not be a natural conception Third member of the trinity would cover as a shadow Reminder of Shekinah glory (Exodus 40:34) and Mt of Transfiguration (Matthew 17) The mechanics of which we will never comprehend (Henry Morris) Title: Son of God His father would be God, not man Just as the body of "the first Adam" was directly formed by God (Genesis 2:7 ), with no genetic connection to either father or mother, so the body of "the second Adam" (1 Corinthians 15:45 ) was directly formed by God (Hebrews 10:5 ) with no genetic connection to either parent. there is no biblical evidence that the sin nature is passed on genetically only through the father (J. MacArthur) Compassion of Message 1:36 News of a companion miracle for Elizabeth 6 months prior Contemplate the total loneliness of Mary No one in her world could identify with her feelings I know how you feel Elizabeth had conceived through miraculous intervention but naturally Here is one person in whom Mary could find companionship The Meekness 1:38 That Mary believed for such an impossible event as the virgin birth is remarkable She accepted without reservation God s plan for her life She is a model of one as worthy as any devout young lady for such a taks Behold, the handmaid of the Lord No hired servant but a bondslave; completely surrenderd Mary gave up the last vestige of living for self in that moment She risked losing Joseph and marital happiness She risked her reputation in the community She risked the potential of stoning (Deuteronomy 22:23, 24) The Explanation 1:37 The only sufficient explanation God rules the universe He upholds all things by the Word of His power For no word from God shall be void of power (ASV) (Gen. 18:14; Jer. 32:17, 27) The incarnation of God s Son is beyond human understanding but it is not beyond His infinite power Gabriel s words to Mary are no less true in our age We observe that the statement of the angel is unmodified and absolute The words speak of God s omnipotence. And knowing that God does not change, the words are the answer to our need. Nothing is impossible when you put your trust in God; Nothing is impossible when your trusting in His Word Harken to the voice of God to thee; Is there anything to hard for Me? Then put your trust in God alone, and rest upon His Word; For ev rything, O ev rything, Yes ev rything is possible with God. Mary submitted to the plan of God Romans 12:1 I beseech you, therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God that you present yourselves as a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God which is your reasonable service. IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 THE ANNOUNCEMENT OF JESUS 1:26-38 The Messenger 1:26 Gabriel: mighty one of God The Maiden 1:27 Virgin is used twice for emphasis almah is the Hebrew word the virgin birth makes possible Christ s true humanity without inherited sin. The Message 1:28-36 Hail Mary, full of grace is accurate Mary is to be honored, but only God is to be worshipped Ephesians 1:6 accepted Mary was the mother of the Lord Jesus Christ during His incarnation on the earth His name will be Jesus He would be great: mega Jesus would be the unique God-man Mary would bring forth a human son, Jesus Son of the Highest is the title for His deity His kingly sonship is received from his father David Concern of Message 1:34 Mary viewed the conception as immediate Third member of the trinity would cover as a shadow Exodus 40:34; Matthew 17 Compassion of Message 1:36 The Meekness 1:38 She accepted without reservation God s plan for her life She risked losing Joseph and marital happiness She risked her reputation in the community She risked the potential of stoning. (Deuteronomy 22:23, 24) The Explanation 1:37 The incarnation of God s Son is beyond human understanding but it is not beyond His infinite power the statement of the angel is unmodified and absolute Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(9).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 12/3/2021 9:09:17 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 INTRODUCTION All is moving quickly for the arrival of the Lord Jesus in human form. We have had the Announcement of John the Baptist and now Mary has been notified of her role as the instrument in His birth. We have received instruction from their lives. Zachariah and Elizabeth as righteous, blameless, fulfilling responsibilities, temporary rebuke. Mary as young, humble, submissive. Elizabeth as longing to have a child; Mary as facing disgrace for becoming pregnant. What a contrast! It is indeed the Most Wonderful Story Ever Told. We now come to the part where Mary visits Elizabeth. MARY VISITS ELIZABETH 1:39-45 As noted last week, the news of Elizabeth s pregnancy was two-fold The reinforce that nothing is impossible with God To provide a note of compassion and companionship for Mary Personal Confirmation 1:39, 40 With the understanding of Mary that the conception would take place immediately, the text indicates she quickly makes the trip to see Elizabeth with haste a quick movement suggesting an eager interest Scripture is silent about the specific town where Elizabeth and her husband lived. What was her hurry? What was she expecting to see? Perhaps some confirmation Mary was overwhelmed with the news from Gabriel News of a miraculous nature; news like she had never heard before. Could it be possible? Gabriel gave her a note of compassion Mary had a relative who also experienced a miracle From Elizabeth Mary would receive confirmation of all that had been said Imagine the encouragement to one s spirit when amazing news is reinforced Here is a chance for Personal Confirmation Greetings among Hebrews was much more than a Hi , hello , Yo , what s happening Among Jews this was a blessing of peace, eloquently stated with compliments and bows. Physical Confirmation 1:41, 42 An immediate bond of sympathy Happiness communicated doubles itself J. C. Ryle: Without this visit, Elizabeth might never have been so filled with the Holy Spirit, as we are here told she was; and Mary might never have uttered that song of praise which is now known all over the Church of Christ We should always value the communion of the saints. It is a little refreshment along our journey. (Hebrews 10) Mutual confirmation and comfort; Romans 1:11, 12 The sound of a voice The HS moved on John the Baptist even before birth; 1:15 Here if the first worship of the Messiah Joyful jumping; not unusual for such movement in the womb Here is miraculous timing; Physical Confirmation Joy is an indication of the Spirit s filling Remember: what fills you controls you. Here is a Spirit-filled family: 1:41, baby and mom; 1:67 dad Joy is a gift. Joy is kingdom living; Romans 14:17; a distinction of Jesus followers filled does not mean she received a fullness similar to the day of Pentecost It means that the spirit of prophecy came upon her Elizabeth spoke as a Spirit-filled prophetess of God. Divine Unction 1:42-45 Spirit filling and spiritual proclamation go together; Ephesians 5:18, 19 she cried out loudly loud for emphasis Here is gospel song #1 Praise to Mary; called her highly blessed of God Simply noting her special place of privilege as the mother of Jesus Elizabeth was righteous and blameless not green with envy This greeting has been turned into a prayer by Roman Catholics Ave Maria or Hail Mary Blessed are thou among women, and blessed is the fruit of they womb Did not come into use until about the 11th century and was sanctioned by the church in the 12th century. The text: blessed among women not above women 43 Eulogy and Revelation Prophetic Confirmation 1:43 the mother of my Lord Mary has not yet told her of her experience with Gabriel Mary was barely pregnant; only in the first week or two The Spirit gave at least partial knowledge to Elizabeth of what had happened concerning Messiah s birth Quote of Ps. 110:1 ?? Mary was recognized as the mother of the Messiah But Elizabeth s focus was on the Messiah and not Mary She loses sight of her distinct privilege; not jealously holding on to it Elizabeth recognized Jesus as the Messiah Her son was the first to recognize Him as a man 1 Corinthians 12:3; Philippians 2:11 1:44 It is a spiritual marvel that one unborn child should bless another unborn child in such a manner We are already being told that is to be a bummer of a Christmas. Supply chain issues; empty shelves; a new covid variant. That is what makes a bad Christmas from the world s view For a Christian, how can there be a bad Christmas? How can the celebration of the coming of Jesus ever be bad? v. 44 is all about excitement; not gloom Dave Branon: every Christmas is good when you focus on the good news of Jesus 1:45 Here is the last mention of Elizabeth; not mentioned in other chapter in the Bible Elizabeth commended Mary for believing that God would fulfill His promise Faith translates into obedience Belief is associated with obedience which is associated with blessedness v. 42 blessed eulogy; v. 45 blessed happy; an inward contentedness not affected by circumstances The faithfulness and devotion of Mary was necessary for the Son of God to be born upon the earth Guzik adds that "Elizabeth recognized that Mary s faith played an active role in receiving the promise. God promises should never make us passive; they should prompt us to seize them by faith." Luke 11:27, 28 "Blessed is she who has believed that what the Lord has said to her will be accomplished!" Do we know anything of this precious faith? This is the question that concerns us. Do we know anything of the faith of God's elect, the faith which is the working of God? We do not live on explanations; we live on promises. It has often been said that the Lord is not looking for better methods but for better men and women of faith. IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 MARY VISITS ELIZABETH 1:39-45 Personal Confirmation 1:39, 40 Mary had a relative who also experienced a miracle Physical Confirmation 1:41 Happiness communicated doubles itself Mutual confirmation and comfort; Romans 1:11, 12 the first worship of the Messiah Joy is an indication of the Spirit s filling Divine Unction 1:42-45 Simply noting her special place of privilege as the mother of Jesus Prophetic Confirmation 1:43 Mary has not yet told her of her experience with Gabriel Elizabeth recognized Jesus as the Messiah Her son was the first to recognize Him as a man How can the celebration of the coming of Jesus ever be bad? Faith translates into obedience v. 42 blessed eulogy; v. 45 blessed happy Luke 11:27, 28 We do not live on explanations; we live on promises Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(10).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 12/11/2021 9:37:51 AM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message IN THE COURSE OF HUMAN EVENTS Luke 1:5-56 THE ANNOUNCEMENT OF JOHN 1:5-25 The Parents of John 1:5-7 The Promise of John 1:8-25 THE ANNOUNCMENT OF JESUS 1:26-38 MARY VISITS ELIZABETH 1:39-56 The Song of Elizabeth 1:45 The Song of Mary 1:46-56 THE SONG OF MARY Luke 1:46-56 Commonly called The Magnificat from the first word in the Latin translation It is another form of the word mega; to make great, declare great, show great Reminder of Psalm 34:1-3 It s one thing to have praise in your mind, and another to have praise in your mouth. It is a great part of worship to see how you can magnify or make God great. It is an outburst of praise with some strong OT references and language Particularly 1 Samuel 2:1-10 from Hannah s own praise song about her son A distinction can be noted between the two: (noted by Leon Morris) Hannah: a shout of triumph over her enemies Mary: a humble contemplation of the mercies of God Mary had plenty of time to mull over her circumstance, pray and meditate on Scripture during her trip south. She refers to at least 12 OT passages. PERSONAL REASONS FOR PRAISE 1:46-49 46, 47 text does not say but natural to assume the Holy Spirit s filling Result in a spontaneous inspired utterance of praise The song had two movements: Personal Reason for Praise 46-49 Israel s Reasons for Praise 50-55 The general progression of thought seems to be from the goodness of God to Mary as an individual to His consequent kindness to Israel as a nation. A couple of specifics from the Greek text: Spirit and soul should be considered poetic parallelism; rather than special distinction Psalm 103:1 suggests worship with the whole being Bless the Lord, O my soul, and all that is within me bless His holy Name Spurgeon: Soul music is the very soul of music Verb tenses: magnifies notes a continual activity rejoices is past tense; a special act of rejoicing, maybe from angel s visit Rejoiced: a soul jumping for joy Mary praised the Lord and rejoiced in Him as her Savior She exalted the Lord and glorified Him Express pure praise, worship on its highest level 48 Motivating her adoration was awareness of how greatly she had been blessed by Him he has regarded attentively looked upon; same word LXX used for Lot s wife She was careful to give all the glory to God Mary used phrases from the OT, especially the Psalms She knew Scripture She knew God s faithfulness to His Word God my Savior recognized her need, she was a sinner like other people She was living on a high spiritual level Yet she remained a humble person as she obeyed God and accepted His plan for her life low estate hand maiden (Goodspeed) he has noticed his slave in her humble station; she had no will of her own but to do His will Need a fresh motivation for the Advent season; here it is: we like Mary should realize that the mercy we received as a result of Jesus' first coming is just as undeserved, call me blessed we are still talking about her 2,000 years later J. Vernon McGee: don t play her down, but don t play her up 49 Mary s song made it clear that she deserved no credit for what God was doing in her It was all the result of who He is, not what she was. He is the Mighty One. Nor was God showing favoritism in what He was doing in and through her God was blessing her, not for her sake alone, but for the sake of His own holy name Mary seeks to magnify His greatness because He has done great things. Jeremiah 10:6 (ASV) There is none like unto thee, O Jehovah; thou art great, and thy name is great in might. He is Holy the idea of separation. The title stands for the whole person of God Not just that is name is be considered holy and used reverently, but it means His whole person is holy. "Holiness is that which makes God God." ISRAEL S REASON FOR PRAISE 1:50-55 He is Merciful Mary set forth all the glories of the God of her fathers; His holiness, His mercy, His might, His faithfulness Mary was able to envision something of the redemptive plan of God She saw that she was part of a great plan that would bring God s mercy, not only to her, but to all who would fear and serve Him. Mercy and fear Fear: a wholesome dread of offending a holy God; reverential awe 1 Peter 1:17 2 Corinthians 7:1 Puritan Charles Bridges defines fear of God - It is that affectionate reverence by which the child of God bends himself humbly and carefully to his Father's law. Mercy: outward manifestation of pity and assumes need on the part of the recipient Mercy gives attention to those in misery 51-53 Another section where some confusion could occur Mary is reciting God s actions but uses verbs in the past tense She could be looking in the past and citing some activities of God that have taken place Meaning this: only because the mighty Lord has done mighty things is there good news to tell, only because of the past tenses which speak of God s deeds is there a gospel to proclaim (Ford) Or could be speaking of activities yet future but which have begun to be realized The future is spoken of as if it had already happened. Called Prophetic Aorist Not an unusual use of grammar in Scripture. God s activities are so certain to take place that it is spoken of as already have taken place. The spirit of prophecy Mary is speaking of a Son who is growing in her belly. Mary s words clearly had in view the might results of the coming of the Messiah The strong arm which she spoke of pointed to the strength of Christ when He should come Here is a complete reversal of human values Those who prospered and gained power through the exploitation of the poor and needy God is about to overthrow all the proud, mighty, rich Daniel 2:34-35 (WEB) You saw until a stone was cut out without hands, which struck the image on its feet that were of iron and clay, and broke them in pieces. Then was the iron, the clay, the brass, the silver, and the gold, broken in pieces together, and became like the chaff of the summer threshing floors; and the wind carried them away, so that no place was found for them: and the stone that struck the image became a great mountain, and filled the whole earth. Revelation 19:15-16 (WEB) Out of his mouth proceeds a sharp, double-edged sword, that with it he should strike the nations. He will rule them with a rod of iron. He treads the winepress of the fierceness of the wrath of God, the Almighty. He has on his garment and on his thigh a name written, "KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS." The coming of Christ means the end to all human boasting. the proud the imaginations of their heart ; proud thoughts not just arrogant behavior the mighty those sitting on thrones God lowers the high and raises the low. the rich in a culture where it is assumed the rich will always be cared for; here God will fill the hungry and send the rich away empty His mercy will be to them of low degree and to those who are hungry God will turn the order of society upside down; the great reversal of fortune 54, 55 God s help for His people He has given help - means to take hold of another as by the hand and figuratively means to support them from falling. He is Faithful Her testimony of faithfulness extended to God s promise to Abraham A promise to bless all the families of the earth God used Israel as His servant in preparing for the fulfillment of His plan. But Israel failed to be the servant who would truly do God s will Now God was taking hold of Israel to give it help through the Child conceived in Mary s womb Mary is thinking of the help that will come through the Messiah Jesus is the one Seed of Abraham through whom God s promise is fulfilled. God s actions through the Messiah is not a new activity but a continuation of His mercy toward Abraham. Malachi 3:6 (ASV) For I, Jehovah, change not; therefore ye, O sons of Jacob, are not consumed. v. 56 Mary s visit continued for 3 months Probably left before all the commotion of the birth of John the Baptist THE SONG OF MARY Luke 1:46-56 The Magnificat first word in the Latin translation Psalm 34:1-3 1 Samuel 2:1-10 Hannah: a shout of triumph over her enemies Mary: a humble contemplation of the mercies of God PERSONAL REASONS FOR PRAISE 1:46-49 Psalm 103:1 Spurgeon: Soul music is the very soul of music She was careful to give all the glory to God He is the Mighty One. He is Holy "Holiness is that which makes God God."
