Think of Stephen; Acts 7 Lord, do not hold this sin against them Reinforcing a supernatural message with supernatural love We know of at least one person who was impacted He was watching over the garments Jesus calls us to a love unknown by any other religion in the world 16 year old condemned to death in Pakistan on false charges of blasphemy It is the Spirit of God that produces this love; Gal. 5 It is one thing to love thy neighbor quite another to love your enemy Jesus zeroes in on our actions and attitudes Our model is God: For God so loved the world that He gave 6:36 This is real tough love because it is tough on the believer who loves The world knows about jungle warfare and retaliation Love s Reward Confound the Enemy The world does not know how to respond to the person who repays wrong with kindness The right response helps us to Avoid bitterness and hatefulness Ann Landers said, "Hate is like acid. It can damage the vessel in which it is stored as well as destroy the object on which it is poured." Hating people is like burning down your own house to get rid of a rat Agape: a love that is volitionally driven We flesh out what the Spirit is doing in our lives Philippians 2:12, 13 6:27, 32, 35 Love s Reminders Lowell Johnson: How do we love our enemies? 1. Greet them graciously. Don t avoid, look the other way, try to hide 2. Disarm them. Do the very thing they least expect. Turn the cheek 3. Do good to them. You make the first move 4. Refuse to speak evil of them. The more you talk of hurt, the more it hurts. 5. Thank God for them. God s sovereignty says that He placed them in your life. 6. Pray for them. 7. Ask God to bless them. In The Grace of Giving, Stephen Olford tells of a Baptist pastor during the American Revolution, Peter Miller, who lived in Ephrata, Pennsylvania, and enjoyed the friendship of George Washington. In Ephrata also, lived Michael Wittman, an evil-minded man who did all he could to oppose and humiliate this dear pastor. One day Michael Wittman was arrested for treason and sentenced to die. Pastor Peter Miller traveled seventy miles on foot to Philadelphia to plead for the life of the traitor. "No, Peter," General Washington said. "I cannot grant you the life of your friend." "My friend!" exclaimed the old preacher. "He's the bitterest enemy I have." "What?" cried Washington. "You've walked seventy miles to save the life of an enemy? That puts the matter in different light. I'll grant your pardon." Washington granted the pardon and Peter Miller took Michael Wittman back home to Ephrata--no longer an enemy but a friend. His love for his enemy changed his enemy. We can easily say this kind of love is radical. It is upside down But it is really our hatred that is upside down. God is seeking to set us right side up. Isaiah 55:8 His ways are not our ways May the mind of Christ my Savior; Live in me from day to day, By His love and power controlling All I do and say. DROPPING A BOMBSHELL Luke 6:20-40 Jesus is bringing to the world a new dimension of love We must be convinced that there will come a day when God will make all things right Genesis 18:25 Romans 12:19 hear carries with it the thought of obeying what is heard PRACTICE OF LOVE 6:27-36 Love s Recipient Love s Requirement It is not truly love until it acts Stephen; Acts 7 Love s Reward Confound the Enemy Avoid bitterness and hatefulness Agape: a love that is volitionally driven Love s Reminders Greet them graciously Disarm them Do good to them Refuse to speak evil of them Thank God for them Pray for them Ask God to bless them Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(54).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 1/7/2023 3:38:05 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message DROPPING A BOMBSHELL Luke 6:20-49 CHARACTERISTICS OF THOSE IN THE KNGDOM 20-26 Beatitudes and Woes PRACTICE OF THOSE IN THE KINGDOM 27-45 Loving and Forgiving EVALUATION OF THOSE IN THE KINGDOM 46-49 Illustration LOVE YOUR ENEMY Luke 6:27-36 Just a brief review: We are just after midway of Jesus public ministry The location is a mountain Jesus has selected the 12 men who would be His close disciples, apostles Coming back down to a more level place but still on the mountain others come to meet Him Out of this group are more disciples of various levels of following Included are curious observers of Jew and Gentile background The message is commonly referred to as the Sermon on the Mount Luke abbreviated it to 29 verses; Matthew has 3 chapters The message is the ordination message for the disciples Jesus is laying out how a follower of His can be identified He totally upsets the cart as to the usual approach to life What He is presenting is radical; off the chart stuff; humanly impossible Humanly IMpossible but supernaturally HIMpossible Jesus has spoken of the Characteristics of Those in the Kingdom 20-26 The Beatitudes: Blessed are the beggarly poor, the spiritually hungry, those crushed over their sin. Blessed are those who are mistreated. hate, harp, heckle and hurt After a warning to the casual observers living life for the here and now, Jesus Drops a Bomb on the followers: Love your enemies As mentioned last week, there are a total of 9 commands in these verses v. 27 Love, do; v. 28 Bless, pray; v. 29 offer; v. 30 give, not ask back; v. 31 do; v. 35 Love, do good, lend; v. 36 Be Commands which run counter to way we would typically respond There must be dependence on a supernatural source Romans 12:14-21 (Deut. 32:35; Prov. 25:21, 22) It is not enough to say you love your enemy, but you must follow the words with do good Agape is not a word of great emotion, but a word of action, sacrifice v. 28 adds bless and pray I would like to add three subjects to the remaining verses on love RESPONSE, RULE, CAUTION, REWARD RESPONSE 6:29-31 Turn the cheek Matthew 5:39 But I say to you, do not resist an evil person; but whoever slaps you on your right cheek, turn the other to him also. There is no tit for tat in the followers of Jesus Unnatural response This is not a verse opposing self-defense Prophetic verse: Isaiah 50:6 I gave My back to those who strike Me, and My cheeks to those who pluck out the beard; I did not cover My face from humiliation and spitting. Jesus is speaking about the proper response to being unjustly humiliated Rod Mattoon: In Matthew 5:39 , Jesus mentions the strike of the right cheek which tells us He is describing a backhanded slap. Since most people are right-handed, this is surely what Jesus had in mind. In Jesus' time, if you wanted to really insult someone, you would slap them with the back of your hand. According to rabbinic law, to hit someone with the back of the hand was twice as insulting as hitting him with the flat of the hand. The back of the hand meant calculated contempt or withering disdain for a person. It meant that you were insulted, vilified, and scorned as a nobody. You were considered worthless or as nothing Vine says tupto means to strike but "usually not with the idea of giving a thrashing Zodhiates on tupto - To strike, smite with the hand, stick, or other instrument repeatedly. John 18:22 to slap with the palm Jesus did not turn His head and asked to be struck again But neither did He lash out in anger and revenge To turn the other cheek is to accept the mistreatment without retaliation Lovingly absorb the insult, hurt, pain, rejection, and abuse. Turning the cheek is more of an illustration of a principle to live by than of a literal action. It is more of an attitude of your heart than a physical posture. It shows an attitude opposite of retaliation Avoid the Court Matthew 5:40 If anyone wants to sue you and take your shirt (chiton ), let him have your coat (himation ) also. John MacArthur explains "Many people owned only one coat, or cloak, which also served as a blanket when they slept. Because of that, the Mosaic law required that any coat taken as a pledge had to be returned before sunset (Ex. 22:26 ; Deut. 24:13 ). To keep a person s cloak would constitute serious abuse. But when that happens, Christ s disciples are not to retaliate, but rather to continue to lovingly minister to those who persecute them even if that results in losing their shirt (inner garment) as well. The point is to not seek revenge. Be willing to be exposed and even take more risks. Be Generous 6:30 Everyone who asks you give Spurgeon: Be generous. A miser is no follower of Jesus Use discretion as to not promote laziness Context (6:34) suggests a legitimate need We are not required to respond to every foolish, selfish request made of us. Even Christian love must exercise discernment RULE 6:31 The negative expression was well known: It had long been said, You should not do to your neighbor what you would not want him to do to you. D A Carson adds that "The Golden Rule was not invented by Jesus; it is found in many forms in highly diverse settings. About A.D. 20, Rabbi Hillel, challenged by a Gentile to summarize the law in the short time the Gentile could stand on one leg, reportedly responded, What is hateful to you, do not do to anyone else. This is the whole law; all the rest is commentary. Go and learn it. The Golden Rule was not meant to be a prerequisite for salvation Jesus is speaking to those presumed to be believers Jesus is not seeking to control negative behavior but to encourage behavior motivated by love Jesus, in a very brief statement, condensed the OT to a singular principle. CAUTION 6:32-34 Examples of natural behavior of loving, doing good, lending Unsaved men can and will do these things Worth noting: an important word in each verse KJV: thank NKJV: credit It is the word grace Where is the grace if you do what any unsaved man will do? The behavior Jesus is calling for requires a supernatural response HIMpossible REWARD 6:35, 36 but moreover Contrasts the doing and loving like the unsaved with what is expected to the followers of Jesus 2 Peter 1:4 we are partakers of the divine nature We should bear the stamp of His moral character He is loving, gracious, generous to His enemies. We should be like Him This is the light that shines Matthew 5:16 that they may see your good works Your supernatural works; your HIMpossible works Works that in no way we can receive the credit because so unnatural to man Your life will be full of fruit and evidence of relationship to God Moral likeness proves parentage Love, do good, be generous Your pay for service will be much The believer s divine recompense; recognition for the divine quality of character Already used in 6:23 Revelation 22:12 Behold, I am coming quickly, and My reward (misthos ) is with Me, to render to every man according to what he has done." Disciples practice a lifestyle of mercy v. 35 He is kind (gracious) to give to meet a need v. 36 He is merciful to withhold judgment rightly deserved WWW: The important thing is not that we are vindicated before our enemies but that we become more like God in our character (Luke 6:35 ). This is the greatest reward anyone can receive. On the last day of the US Civil War, officer Joshua Chamberlain was in command of the Union army. His soldiers lined up on both sides of the road that the Confederate army had to march down in surrender. One wrong word or one belligerent act and the longed-for peace could be turned to slaughter. In an act as brilliant as it was moving, Chamberlain ordered his troops to salute their foe! No taunting here, no vicious words only guns in salute and swords raised to honor. LOVE YOUR ENEMY Luke 6:27-36 Humanly IMpossible but supernaturally HIMpossible RESPONSE 6:29-31 Turn the Cheek This is not a verse opposing self-defense John 18:22 To turn the other cheek is to accept the mistreatment without retaliation It is more of an attitude of your heart than a physical posture. Avoid the Court Be willing to be exposed Be Generous 6:30 Context (6:34) suggests a legitimate need RULE 6:31 The Golden Rule was not meant to be a prerequisite for salvation CAUTION 6:32-34 KJV: thank NKJV: credit grace REWARD 6:35, 36 We should bear the stamp of His moral character Your pay for service will be much Revelation 22:12 Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(55).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 1/14/2023 9:04:45 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message PRACTICE OF THOSE IN THE KINGDOM 6:27-45 Loving 6:27-36 Principle: v. 36 be merciful be compassionate We are to bear the stamp of the moral character of Jesus No retaliation Be willing to suffer loss Be generous Your compassion for others including your enemy should mirror your heavenly Father s The Lord is upright; He is my rock; there is no unrighteousness in Him God will make all things right with the wicked; they will be exposed The classified documents will be found; lies will be discovered The next section of the Practice of Those in the Kingdom is Forgiving The next four commands; two negative, two positive are governed by the last part of verse 38 For with the same measure you measure it will be measured back to you." NEGATIVE COMMANDS 6:37 You have heard the suggestion: You can t judge a book by its cover True or false? There have been the old timer walk drive into a new car dealership in his old farm truck, wearing some ragged clothes, and looking totally disheveled seeking to buy a new truck, but was turn away because he was not deemed financially equipped. He was rejected solely by his appearance. He only went to another dealership, was accepted and paid cash for a new truck. He later returned to the first dealership, spoke with the sales manager and a couple of salesmen last their jobs. You might even come across a blond who is really articulate and beats the rap of blondness. That have been many times when you quickly realized that your first impressions were wrong. You can t always judge a book by its cover. Do Not Judge A negative command in the present tense: Stop judging If there is a misunderstood, abused, misused verse in the Bible this is it Even the ungodly have memorized this one Whenever Christians warn against or condemn our society for its loss of moral moorings, it is not uncommon to have them misquote Matthew 7:1 or Luke 6:37 to counter our "judgmental attitude" Jesus is not forbidding making moral evaluations We are to continually make moral judgments about people and issues John 7:24; Galatians 6:1 In Jesus sermon: Thou shalt not commit adultery That is a moral judgment. The world wants us to call it an affair Christians are to make sound moral judgments but the attitude behind it is critical We make the judgments based on the compassion of God We maintain a loving, humble attitude You can make judgments without being judgmental, critical Do not condemn Somewhat an extension of judging when one condemns, he establishes himself as the "executioner" of the person who he has judged. Later in this section, Jesus will tell us if you want to judge someone begin by looking in the mirror You will know the tree by its fruit Everyday we make hundreds of judgments We pass judgment on appearance, behavior, speech, deportment, attitude, work ethic, productivity, keeping or breaking a promise, guilt or innocence, which person we believe and which person we do not believe. Who is hired, who is fired. Who made the grade; who failed Who is worthy, who needs to try harder The problem is not so much the act but the motive Judge righteous judgment Jesus forbids hypocrisy and a condemning spirit rising from self-righteousness Luke 18:11 the Pharisee: stood and was praying this to himself: God, I thank You that I am not like other people: swindlers, unjust, adulterers, or even like this tax collector." We are to be discerning We are commanded to be shrewd as serpents, harmless as doves We are to assess a person s spiritual condition; confront their sin (v 42-45) We are to recognize false teachers and turn away (2 Tim 3:5) Motive is the issue Jesus is addressing The standard you apply to others God applies back Don't be too hard on the person who sins, For the yardstick you lay on another May someday be used as a measure for you; Oh, be gracious and judge not, my brother! Henry Bosch Romans 2:1, 2 a warning from Paul Alexander Maclaren: (He who) chooses to fight with a tomahawk, he will be scalped some day Oswald Chambers: Criticism leaves you with the flattering unction that you are a superior person. It is impossible to develop the characteristics of a saint and maintain a critical attitude. We may be able to come to a proper level of discernment about an action but we do always have the ability to measure a person s motive PRACTICE OF THOSE IN THE KINGDOM Luke 6:27-45 NEGATIVE COMMANDS 6:37 Do Not Judge A negative command in the present tense: Stop judging Jesus is not forbidding making moral evaluations John 7:24; Galatians 6:1 We maintain a loving, humble attitude Do not condemn Jesus forbids hypocrisy and a condemning spirit rising from self-righteousness Luke 18:11 We are to be discerning The standard you apply to others God applies back Romans 2:1, 2 Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(56).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 1/21/2023 3:27:15 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: MESSAGE DROPPING A BOMBSHELL Luke 6:20-49 CHARACTERISTICS OF THOSE IN THE KNGDOM 20-26 Beatitudes and Woes PRACTICE OF THOSE IN THE KINGDOM 27-45 Loving and Forgiving Cautions Illustration PRACTICE OF THOSE IN THE KINGDOM 6:27-45 Loving 6:27-36 Principle: v. 36 be merciful be compassionate NEGATIVE COMMANDS: Do not judge; do not condemn Not saying we are to have no moral discernment John 7:24 Make righteous judgment We maintain a loving, humble attitude Jesus forbids hypocrisy and a condemning spirit rising from self-righteousness The standard you apply to others God applies back A small-town baker bought his butter from a local farmer. One day he weighed the butter and concluded that the farmer had been reducing the amount in the packages but charging the same. So the baker accused the farmer of fraud. In court the judge asked the farmer, "Do you have measuring weights?" "No sir," replied the farmer. "How then do you manage to weigh the butter that you sell?" The farmer answered, "When the baker began buying his butter from me, I thought I'd better get my bread from him. I have been using his 1-pound loaf as the weight for the butter I sell. If the weight of the butter is wrong, he has only himself to blame." Superficial Judgment is wrong: John 7:24; Prov. 18:13; Luke 7:36-50 Hypocritical Judgment is wrong: Matt. 7:3-5 Harsh, unforgiving judgment is wrong: Luke 6:37; Titus 3:2 Self-righteous judgment is wrong: James 4:6; Luke 18:9-14 Untrue judgment is wrong: Prov. 19:5; Titus 3:2 David Guzik: * We break this command when we think the worst of others. * We break this command when we only speak to others of their faults. * We break this command when we judge an entire life only by its worst moments. * We break this command when we judge the hidden motives of others. * We break this command when we judge others without considering ourselves in their same circumstances. * We break this command when we judge others without being mindful that we ourselves will be judged. POSTIVE COMMANDS 6:37, 38 FORGIVE WEB: set free ASV: release Apoluo: Strongs: free fully, pardon, forgive Used in speaking of divorce (16:18); death (2:29); send away (9:12); to be free from an infirmity (13:12); to free a prisoner (23:16) A different word from that in the disciples prayer: 11:3 In the context: vs. 32-35 it could be a releasing from a financial debt It is a pardoning, forgiving without reference to sin or crime Proverbs 17:9 He that covereth a transgression seeketh love; but he that repeateth a matter separateth very friends. 19:11 The discretion of a man deferreth his anger; and it is his glory to pass over a transgression. This is to be our habitual response: keep on forgiving, keep on releasing people from your prison and you shall be forgiven our forgiveness is dependent on our willingness to forgive Don t hold people hostage with a judgmental or condemning spirit v. 36 is still our therefore summary: Be merciful Be quick to encourage people toward reconciliation We don t gloat over a person s stumbling; or be anxious to cut them off Our goal is to help them seek repentance, restoration so that may be the type of person God wants them to be We should be discerning about the presence of sin, but not judgmental in dealing with it. We don t close our eyes to sin and wrongdoing Being merciful does not mean suspending moral judgment But don t condemn as to make restoration impossible Disciples are to bear good news; not hold grudges Do you have a condemning spirit? Pastor Ray Pritchard Blowing small things all out of proportion. Maximizing the sins of others their faults, foibles and their petty ways. Coming to quick, hasty, negative conclusions. Making mountains out of molehills. Getting involved in situations where you should not be involved. Passing along critical stories to others. Having a strong bias to find others guilty. Being too harsh even when speaking the truth. Adding aggravating remarks when telling a story. Dismissing an unkind remark by saying, I was only joking. Saying something critical and then trying to cover it up. Being unkind and then quickly changing the subject. Telling too many people about what others have done to us. Taking pleasure in condemning others. Telling the truth in order to hurt, not to help. Putting others down in order to make yourself look better. Minimizing your sins while magnifying the sins of others. May God help us to be gracious people and not fault finders Bishop Potter "was sailing for Europe on one of the great trans-Atlantic ocean liners. When he went on board, he found that another passenger was to share the cabin with him. After going to see the accommodations, he came up to the purser's desk and inquired if he could leave his gold watch and other valuables in the ship's safe. He explained that ordinarily he never availed himself of that privilege, but he had been to his cabin and had met the man who was to occupy the other berth. Judging from his appearance, he was afraid that he might not be a very trustworthy person. The purser accepted the responsibility for the valuables and remarked, `It's all right, Bishop, I'll be very glad to take care of them for you. The other man has been up here and left his for the same reason (H. A. Ironside, Illustrations of Bible Truth). GIVE 6:38 A lifestyle command; continue to give Context of social responsibility God will reward; He will not ignore selfless actions Neither will He ignore the unjust actions of others In the context of judging, condemning, freeing, and giving, God will give to us what we give out to others That should be strong motivation in how we deal with other people The image presented is from the market place Grain is measured out, the container shaken to settle what is there and more is added You ladies do the same thing in the kitchen with you measure flower You make chocolate chips cookies and you pack the brown sugar into the measuring cup Those who give generously, receive generously The more widely you spread out seed, the more you harvest Sow sparingly; reap sparingly The person s activity sets the standard for God s reaction They say you can tell the depth of a person s spiritual walk by looking at the debit side of the bank account WWW: If we live to give, God will see to it that we receive; but if we live only to get, God will see to it that we lose. This principle applies not only to our giving of money, but also to the giving of ourselves in ministry to others. Deuteronomy 15:10 You shall generously give to him, and your heart shall not be grieved when you give to him, because for this thing the LORD your God will bless you in all your work and in all your undertakings. Proverbs 19:17 One who is gracious to a poor man lends to the LORD, And He will repay him for his good deed. Proverbs 22:9 He who is generous will be blessed, For he gives some of his food to the poor. Placed in your bosom carry in their robe; Ruth 3:15 We hear the statement: Pay it forward you pay so others are blessed It happens at the fast food drive thru TRANSITION: Practical Behavior of Disciples Positive and Negative Commands CAUTIONS 6:39, 40 Perhaps a break in the message to bring a smile to the listeners Blindness A parable: lay one thing beside another for comparison Jesus knows the priests, scribes, Pharisees of the day will not subscribe to His teaching Jesus has dropped a bombshell on the apostles, disciples, and crowd He has gone against the popular message of the day He identifies them: blind Spiritually blind people cannot lead others into spiritual sight Matthew 15:14 a blind leader will lead those who follow them to disaster and death and ultimately to eternal doom ! A warning to the disciples: Know what you know before telling others what you know And to include the verses that follow: make sure you have sight before you lead anyone else The danger of listening to false teachers is still the same People get sucked into a message that is wrong but their itching ears are satisfied or they are too lazy to do their own homework. Matthew 7:15 beware of false prophets 1 John 4:1 believe not every spirit ,but test the spirits 1 Thess 5:21 prove all things False teachers: 6:39 blind 6:41, 42 hypocrites 6:43, 45 evil 6:40 if a teacher teaches error more than likely the student will also The pupil is inevitably limited by the limitations of their teacher. Hosea 4:9 like people, like priests We cannot lead others where we have not been ourselves That is why we must spend time with the Word of God 2 Cor. 3:18 But we all, with open face beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord, are changed into the same image from glory to glory, even as by the Spirit of the Lord. The goal is to be fully trained; rendered fit; complete Not like Hebrews 5:12, 13 2 Timothy 3:16, 17 thoroughly equipped (synonym of fully trained) Hypocrisy 6:41, 42 Jesus uses an absurd illustration: Speck Inspectors Speckology And Plankitis - According to Jesus, it's a bad idea to major in "speckology" while suffering from "plankitis." If speckology were listed in a university catalog, the course description might read: "The identifying and criticizing of small shortcomings in the lives of everyone around you. Very popular course; fills early." Should plankitis appear in a medical dictionary, it might be identified this way: "A disease that distorts self-perception and renders an individual incapable of recognizing personal faults. Occurs worldwide." Within the context beatitudes Be Merciful Be Loving Be Forgiving Don t be a hypocrite Yes, you need to make moral judgments but in all things be hardest on yourself Yes, you need to help restore another believer who succumbs to sin, but in a spirit of gentleness; looking to yourself so that you also aren't tempted. Counselors call this your Log List Know your own heart; keep a short list of offenses before others and God The speck is a small piece of sawdust; an irritant only to the person who has it used figuratively of faults (defects of secondary importance, slight imperfections, minor personal faults, small moral faults) we detect and pick on in others. A speck as compared to a huge plank If we fail to judge our own sins, we are like the blind leading the blind All can fall into the trap of speck inspectors The Pharisees were Inspectors par Excellence When looking for faults, use a mirror not a telescope "It is much easier to be critical than to be correct." -- Disraeli The chapter has continued to remind us to check our motivation, attitudes Many people involved in the criticism of others are guilty of the same if not more In politics: you know what one side is doing by how they criticize the other It is not easy to examine your own heart; it can be painful 2 Corinthians 13:5 Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith; prove your own selves Scrutinize your own heart; before you go picking around in someone s else life Be honest with yourself; avoid hypocrisy The critic sits on the sideline just waiting He expects to find fault, like the man who sat watching his preacher neighbor nail up a trellis in his backyard. The preacher, seeing him watching intently from his yard, asked, "Trying to pick up some pointers on carpentry?" To which his neighbor replied, "Nope. Just waiting to see what a preacher says when he hits his thumb." There are spiritual vultures: thrive on the mistakes and sins of others. Fault finding is not a spiritual gift Jesus may have presented a humorous illustration but the issue is great Throughout the sermon, Jesus is after the heart; motivation, attitude Jesus expects us to be harder on ourselves than we are on our enemies Be ever willing to release others from our prison To look into the Scriptures and allow the scalpel perform surgery Heb. 4:12 To look into the mirror of Scripture and change what needs changed To look into your bathroom mirror and see Jesus manifest in the flesh When you get what you want in your struggle for self And the world makes you king for a day, Just go to a mirror and look at yourself, And see what THAT man has to say. For it isn't your father or mother or wife Who judgment upon you must pass; The fellow whose verdict counts most in your life Is the one staring back from the glass. Some people may think you a straight-shootin' chum And call you a wonderful guy, But the man in the glass says you're only a bum If you can't look him straight in the eye. He's the fellow to please, never mind all the rest For he's with you clear up to the end, And you've passed your most dangerous, difficult test If the man in the glass is your friend. You may fool the whole world down the pathway of years And get pats on the back as you pass, But your final reward will be heartaches and tears If you've cheated the man in the glass. Author Unknown DROPPING A BOMBSHELL Luke 6:20-49 PRACTICE OF THOSE IN THE KINGDOM 6:27-45 POSITIVE COMMANDS 6:37, 38 FORGIVE WEB: set free ASV: release Strongs: free fully, pardon Proverbs 17:9; 19:11 Don t hold people hostage with a judgmental or condemning spirit Disciples are to bear good news; not hold grudges GIVE 6:38 Context of social responsibility God will give to us what we give out to others The person s activity sets the standard for God s reaction Deuteronomy 15:10 Proverbs 19:17 Proverbs 22:9 CAUTIONS 6:39, 40 Blindness Know what you know before telling others what you know We cannot lead others where we have not been ourselves Hypocrisy 6:41, 42 Speckology And Plankitis Know your own heart All can fall into the trap of speck inspectors 2 Corinthians 13:5 Fault finding is not a spiritual gift Jesus is after the heart; motivation, attitude Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(57).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 1/27/2023 11:01:42 AM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message PRACTICE OF THOSE IN THE KINGDOM ILLUSTRATIONS: Horticulture; Construction 6:43-49 Heeding the warning of sinful judging, Jesus provides some parameters for judging. Here is the necessity of making moral judgments, righteous judgments How to identify a blind leader. How to discover a hypocrite. HORTICULTURE 6:43-45 The Fruit fits the Tree 6:43-44 For the point of explanation Be self-corrective; Be careful who you follow The use of agriculture is something the people could understand The fruit that a teacher produces is at the core of being; good or bad Like teacher, like student; like tree, like fruit Matthew 7:15-20 makes it clear, Jesus is addressing false teachers There is a consistency between source and product The fruit produced by a tree gives clear evidence of the health of the tree. Fruit is always true to character Believers do sin, but the witness of their words and works is consistently good to the glory of God. In terms of ministry, servants of God who are faithful will reproduce themselves in people who are in turn true to the Lord. * WWW Fruit (2590 )(karpos ) is used of literal fruit but is intended to convey a figurative sense describing the consequence of physical, mental, or spiritual action. In the NT the figurative (metaphorical) uses predominate and this is particularly true in the Gospels, where human actions and words are viewed as fruit growing out of a person's essential being or character. "Fruit is a picture of the product in one s life. We produce according to what we are Our words reveal what is in our hearts; fruit is what you can see What is inside will come out, sooner or later One produces what one is We must continually examine our hearts, motivations, attitudes Bitter words, blame-shifting, reveal a heart not right with God You may be a new creation but old things sometimes are hard to put away Paul admonishes: put off; put on 6:45 where does the good treasure come from from meditating often on God s great mercy in Christ toward you. Questions to ask ourselves: Am I marked more by a merciful spirit or by a critical, judgmental spirit? Am I blaming God or others for my problems, or am I working on removing the log in my own eye? Am I frequently judging my own life, down to the thought level, by God s Word? Am I truly born again? Is pleasing Christ the focus of my life? We learn best about a person when we see how they react under pressure True character is revealed in unguarded moments out of the abundance of the heart his heart speaks The heart of the issue is the state of the heart We do what we do because we are what we are Ever ask the question: Where did that come from? 6:45 abundance what is left over because the container has reached its capacity If it came out of you, it was in you What fills my heart? Ephesians 5:18 command to be Spirit controlled What immediately comes next? speaking The first organ to be controlled by the Spirit of God is the tongue Ephesians 4:29, 30 there is a link between unwholesome speech and quenching the Holy Spirit of God It has been estimated that your daily conversations could fill 50-60 pages of a book. Over a year, that is 100 books containing over 200 pages each That s a lot of words. We need Spirit controlled tongues TRANSITION: you can make righteous judgment based on fruit produced You can also judge based on how they build their lives CONSTRUCTION 6:46-49 The end of the Sermon on the Mount is much like the end of James 1 James 1:22 "prove yourselves doers of the word, and not merely hearers who delude themselves. WWW: Jesus is Lord, and He wants our works, not words! A question in the form of a warning The question implies they made professions to His lordship In a word they are saying Jesus is their absolute owner! But they either lie (overtly) or are self-deceived, because their actions (lifestyle) demonstrate that they are "lord" of their life, not Jesus. Lordship demands Obedience! You profess Christ. He comes as a package: Savior and Lord Romans 10:9, 10 Ye call me the "Way" and walk me not, Ye call me the "Life" and live me not, Ye call me "Master" and obey me not, If I condemn thee, blame me not. Ye call me "Bread" and eat me not, Ye call me "Truth" and believe me not, Ye call me "Lord" and serve me not, If I condemn thee, blame me not. Geoffrey O'Hara Luke s record is part of Matthew 7 including verse 21,22 Lord, Lord An emphatic profession without action means nothing v. 46 why? If you are disobedient, why continue the profession of obedience? the Three-fold condition: G. Campbell Morgan 1. Every one that cometh to Me, surrender. 2. And heareth My words, discipleship. 3. And doeth them, obedience. J. C. Ryle: The evil exposed here is a disease which has never ceased to prevail all over Christendom. Ezekiel 33:31 They come unto thee, as the people cometh, and they sit before thee as my people, and they hear thy words, but they will not do them, for with their mouth they show much love, but their heart goeth after their covetousness. Ryle: Let it be a fixed principle in our religion, that obedience is the only sound evidence of saving faith, and that the talk of the lips is worse than useless, if it is not accompanied by sanctification of the life. The man in whose heart the Holy Ghost really dwells, will never be content to sit still, and do nothing to show his love to Christ. The Gospel is like soap: it only cleanses the ones who use it. The call of Jesus is to examine the foundation of your life Is the evidence of your salvation confirmed in obedience to His commands? We are saved by faith: Ephesians 2:8, 9 But don t forget v. 10 We are His workmanship created unto good works We quote John 3:16, but need to remember 3:36 He who believes the Son has eternal life; but he who does not obey the Son shall not see life, but the wrath of God abides on him. The new nature will bear new fruit To build on a solid foundation takes time and expense It matters not how beautiful the super structure is if the foundation is weak Storms come and go. Flash floods are common in Israel It hits everyone. We have the trials of life and the flood of future judgment Trials: disappointments, set backs, sickness, loss of loved ones, loss of job, being let down by family members; temptation; failing health as we grow older, facing coming death The flood beat vehemently at the house; some use the word torrent Jesus is calling the people to make a decision The kicker is here that both houses look the same from the outside The foundation is only evident in the storm David affirmed "The LORD is my Rock and my Fortress and my Deliverer; My God, my Rock, in Whom I take refuge; My Shield and the Horn of my salvation, my Stronghold and my Refuge; My savior, Thou dost save me from violence." 2 Samuel 22:2-3 , Psalm 18:2 CONCLUSION We have been looking at Luke s record of Jesus Sermon on the Mount The ordination message for the apostles, the call to discipleship for disicples Time of self-examination; dealing with any planks Examining our fruit Examining our foundation How is the building of our house? Is it sufficient to withstand the onslaught of spiritual wickedness in high places? Is our life marked out be obedience? Is there sufficient evidence to convict us of being a disciple of Jesus Christ? The torrent will come; now and in future judgment Will your life stand or be quickly swept away? PRACTICE OF THOSE IN THE KINGDOM Luke 6:27-45 HORTICULTURE 6:43-45 There is a consistency between source and product Fruit is always true to character "Fruit is a picture of the product in one s life. True character is revealed in unguarded moments The heart of the issue is the state of the heart The first organ to be controlled by the Spirit of God is the tongue Ephesians 4:29, 30 CONSTRUCTION 6:46-49 James 1:22 An emphatic profession without action means nothing Three-fold condition: G. Campbell Morgan 1. Every one that cometh to Me, surrender. 2. And heareth My words, discipleship. 3. And doeth them, obedience. Ezekiel 33:31 The Gospel is like soap: it only cleanses the ones who use it. To build on a solid foundation takes time and expense The foundation is only evident in the storm 2 Samuel 22:2-3 Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(58).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 2/4/2023 9:30:42 AM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message JUST SAY THE WORD Luke 7:1-10 INTRODUCTION What will we discover in Luke 7 The Healing of the Centurion s Son 1-10 The Raising of the Widow s Son 11-17 The Explanation of John the Baptist 18-35 The Gratitude of the Sinful Woman 36-50 Somewhere in your life you have been in a situation where a decision had to be made. In fact, somebody is waiting for you to make up your mind. They might just say to you, Just say the word and it we will make it happen. It is an idiom used to show that you are willing and ready to do something as soon as somebody asks. If you need help; if you need to go someplace; if you are going to Dairy Queen George Bailey: What is it you want, Mary? What do you want? You want the moon? Just say the word and I will throw a lasso around it and pull it down. There is even a game by that title where you as a group try to get somebody to say a word It is also the response of the centurion. The Setting 7:1-5 The Attitude 7:6-8 Jesus Response 7:9, 10 The Setting 7:1-5 Jesus is still highly popular with the masses but there is growing hostility He has concluded the Sermon on the Mount words rhema the focus is on the content of the words; a definite meaning that should not be lost This account is the perfect follow up to the rhema The message was about discipleship; the Characteristics and Practices Poor in spirit, hunger and thirst for righteousness Love you enemy; don t judge with a critical spirit Avoid blind and hypocritic leaders The conclusion was to hear and obey; build upon a solid foundation The centurion is the illustration and perfect example of the Sermon Though we are not told of his salvation, Jesus commended him to the Jews for his faith, so include him in the list of Gentile converts Here is one who hears and responds positively James 1:22 Location: Capernaum Jesus home base after His forced removal from Nazareth; 4:20-30 Of Jesus 33 miracles, 11 performed in Capernaum; 2 for Gentiles John 4:46-54 healing of nobleman s son Hence, Jesus warning Matthew 11:23 Character: high regard for his servant it was universally accepted that masters possessed the power of life and death over their slaves Roman writer Varro insisted that the only difference between a slave, an animal, and a cart was that the slave talked Abuse was common; death was not unusual Here is a man with a tender heart He did not come for himself or a family member; he came for a slave Matthew 8:6 Lying paralyzed at home, dreadfully tormented Most English texts miss the compassion of the Centurion Vs. 2, 3, 7, 10 servant But v. 7 is a different Greek word: pais instead of doulos Pais: sometimes translated child 2:43 child Jesus ; 8:54 maid ; 9:42 child A more endearing term Note: only twice in the gospels does Jesus commend someone for their great faith; this centurion and the Syrophoenician woman (Matthew 15:28 ) Both are Gentiles The way of faith is open: all nationalities; man or woman Every tribe, tongue, people, nation Centurion: leader of about 100 soldiers; noted for leadership qualities Not the one to advance an army but one who will hold his position until death Every centurion mentioned in Scripture is done in a favorable light 1. There was the centurion who had great faith in the power of Jesus (Mt. 8:5 ). 2. There was the centurion who recognized Jesus hanging on the cross as the Son of God (Mt. 27:54 ). 3. There was the centurion, Cornelius, who was the first Gentile convert to the Christian church (Acts 10:22 ). 4. There was the centurion who recognized that Paul was a Roman citizen and rescued him from the rioting mob (Acts 23:17 23 ). 5. There was the centurion who took steps to deliver Paul from being murdered after being informed of the Jews plan (Acts 24:23 ). 6. There was the centurion whom Felix ordered to escort and look after Paul (Acts 24:23 ). 7. There was the centurion who escorted Paul on his last journey to Rome. He treated Paul with great courtesy and accepted him as the leader when the storm struck the ship (Acts 27:43 ). Steven Cole about the centurion He possesses three qualifications that every servant of Jesus Christ must seek to develop in his or her life: The Qualities of an Effective Servant an exalted view of Jesus, a lowly view of himself, and a caring view of others Here is a Roman soldier in charge of about 100 other soldiers He could have been a Samaritan; his primary role was to see taxes gathered Yet he was tinder toward the Jews; built a synagogue Here is a man who became a servant to his own servant Calling on Jesus to heal someone who was lowly and despised by others Here is a man of faith in a city of people with no faith Be like this centurion You can serve Christ in any ungodly environment How did the Sermon on the Mount begin: Blessed are the poor in spirit The centurion fleshed out the Sermon on the Mount And not only that He becomes for us a picture of the Lord Jesus Isaiah 52:13 My Servant A word used almost 800x in the OT; translated either slave or servant Used 31x in Isaiah 40-53; a section on the Messiah Often linked with the Lord Servant of the Lord Moses 17x, Joshua, David, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob The Servant of the Lord is an individual who carries out the will of God and is totally subservient to his divine master. We tend to admire leaders who work diligently for the benefit of their coworkers and subordinates Isaiah details the Servant s willingness to sacrifice Himself for the betterment of those He cared for and served. 53:2 it is not about His charisma, good looks or popularity 53:3 He was marginalized by society 53:4-6 willingly suffers The Servant of the Lord bears all the problems and pain we bring upon ourselves that result from living according to our own principles He was rejected, we are accepted He was alienated from God, we are drawn near He endured the guilt of moral failure, we are forgiven He experienced intolerable pain, we can be healed He bore our punishment, we will not be judged 53:8-10 The Servant s death on behalf of the sins of others His Motivation: Love John 15:13 (WEB) Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends. JUST SAY THE WORD Luke 7:1-10 The Setting 7:1-5 This account is the perfect follow up to the rhema Location: Capernaum Character: high regard for his servant Only twice in the gospels does Jesus commend someone for their great faith Matthew 15:28 The Qualities of an Effective Servant an exalted view of Jesus, a lowly view of himself, a caring view of others Here is a man who became a servant to his own servant Here is a man of faith in a city of people with no faith Isaiah 52:13 My Servant The Servant of the Lord is an individual who carries out the will of God and is totally subservient to his divine master. The Servant of the Lord bears all the problems and pain we bring upon ourselves that result from living according to our own principles His Motivation: John 15:13 JUST SAY THE WORD Luke 7:1-10 The Setting 7:1-5 This account is the perfect follow up to the rhema Location: Capernaum Character: high regard for his servant Only twice in the gospels does Jesus commend someone for their great faith Matthew 15:28 The Qualities of an Effective Servant An exalted view of Jesus A lowly view of himself A caring view of others Here is a man who became a servant to his own servant Here is a man of faith in a city of people with no faith Isaiah 52:13 My Servant The Servant of the Lord is an individual who carries out the will of God and is totally subservient to his divine master. The Servant of the Lord bears all the problems and pain we bring upon ourselves that result from living according to our own principles His Motivation: John 15:13 Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(59).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 2/11/2023 1:05:28 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message JUST SAY THE WORD Luke 7:1-10 INTRODUCTION If you remember from last week, we spent our time looking at the servant s heart of the centurion toward his own servant who was deathly ill. The timing of this is of the utmost importance Just after the Sermon on the Mount: Jesus description of a disciple We noticed this from our text: Most English texts miss the compassion of the Centurion Vs. 2, 3, 7, 10 servant But v. 7 is a different Greek word: pais instead of doulos If you were watching The Chosen last Sunday, you saw how in their presentation the servant was the real son of the centurion, born to a slave woman. Interesting thought, especially with the word usage. But remember, Scripture makes no such statement The word pais gives us insight into the centurion s compassion We also gave you G. Campbell Morgan s outline: The Qualities of an Effective Servant An exalted view of Jesus A lowly view of himself A caring view of others THE SETTING 7:1-5 The Location of the Centurion The Character of the Centurion We can use the word amaze to describe much of his character The Request of the Centurion Comparing texts: Matthew 8:5 says the centurion himself came Luke says he sent others Contradiction? Of course not Anyone coming from the centurion is as if he came himself They came with the instructions, orders, desires All done with the authority of the centurion As a Roman official, the centurion knew who Jesus was He had heard reports of His healings He probably knew first hand the healing of the nobleman s son, John 4:46-50 He knew Jesus commanded demons to come out Background for his words: Just say the word Sent apostello; sent one; to carry out a task with the authority of the one who sent him First miracle of the text: Jewish leaders interceding for a Roman Clearly, this fellow was different heal many other translations use the word save d?as???: to save through, to cure, preserve, rescue s??? is the common word for being saved: Luke 7:50 to bring safely to the other side; to bring safely through danger, sickness Acts 23:24 safe passage for Paul to see Felix Acts 27:43, 44 safely on land after ship wreck Here is a man well respected; a lover of Israel; personally responsible for building a synagogue; literally: he himself built for us compassionate toward a young slave Here is a man also, drawn to Jesus by God John 6:44 Also a man deeply sensing his own unworthiness Neither was he too proud to ask for help; intermediaries The Jews coming to Jesus were focused on works; what the centurion did They did not understand Grace; where Jesus was coming from THE CENTURION S ATTITUDE 7:6-8 Amazing Humility I am not worthy The Jews say he is worth ; he says, he is not competent; fit (John the Baptist: I am not worthy to tie His shoes ) How? By honoring the Levitical law Totally improper for any Jew to enter the house of a Gentile Associated ceremonial uncleanness As measured against Jesus Augustine - "By saying that he was unworthy, he showed himself worthy of Christ's entering, not within his walls, but within his heart." Here is a tender heart toward the Lord As compared to so many who think they deserve heaven No one is good enough or worthy or fit there are none righteous God sends none away empty but those who are full of themselves! Here is a reminder of the Jews approach to God worthy And so much of the approach of many today. True humility comes from looking at my insufficiency and God s sufficiency Here is a child facing certain death A picture of the human race; impotent to deal with sin and separation from God The Qualities of an Effective Servant An exalted view of Jesus A lowly view of himself A caring view of others Great faith reveals itself in great humility Concrete Confidence The centurion understood the principle of authority He knew what it meant to speak and have his words obeyed Go Come Do If that can happen in his world, he was convinced it could happen in Jesus world He knew his servant s condition was beyond any authority he might have He needed to go the One in authority over all creation He even knew that the One who spoke the world into existence only had to speak the Word and his servant would be healed. The centurion was not looking for a sign, a touch or even a voice No need to just touch the fringe of Jesus garment, or ask for a handkerchief that He had touched Just a word would be enough And we are not even told what Jesus said The word the Master would speak would be enough. He totally delegated the problem and the need of his servant to the Lord Jesus and was confident that Christ would take care of the matter. Do you have that kind of confidence? Where did the centurion develop this character? A tender heart toward those who are sick A tender heart toward the Jewish people Liberal support in building a synagogue A heathen by birth; a soldier by profession He was what he was by the grace of God His knowledge was limited; his religious views were imperfect But he responded to whatever light he had and God gave more light Let us strive to have an eye ready to see, and a hand ready to help, and a heart ready to feel, and a will ready to do good to all. Kindness is a grace that all can understand JESUS RESPONSE 7:9, 10 Luke s focus is not on the healing but the faith of the centurion A faith characterized by humility Many of the Jews recognized Jesus ability, the centurion saw His authority 7:9 not even no, not an absolute, objective negation Jesus' point is that in Israel, that is, among the ethnic Jews who even had access to the OT prophecies concerning the Messiah, Jesus could find no Jew with faith like this centurion. The special privileges of the Jews sadly did not result in a greater percent believing in Jesus Marveled 46x in the NT Only 2x Jesus marveled This is the only occasion Jesus was amazed at someone s faith (2x He commented on great faith Matt. 15:28) Most occasions the people were amazed by Jesus Mark 6:6 Jesus was amazed at unbelief Luke 13x 2:18, 33; 4:22; 8:25; 9:43 Does your faith amaze anyone? Is your faith great or small? How confident are you God is willing and can meet your needs? Every crisis you face is an opportunity to trust in God JUST SAY THE WORD Luke 7:1-10 THE SETTING 7:1-5 The Location of the Centurion The Character of the Centurion The Request of the Centurion Sent to carry out a task with the authority of the one who sent him d?as???: to save through, to cure, preserve, rescue Acts 23:24 Acts 27:43, 44 THE CENTURION S ATTITUDE 7:6-8 Amazing Humility I am not worthy By honoring the Levitical law As measured against Jesus The Qualities of an Effective Servant An exalted view of Jesus A lowly view of himself A caring view of others Concrete Confidence JESUS RESPONSE 7:9, 10 Luke s focus is not on the healing but the faith of the centurion Marveled 46x in the NT This is the only occasion Jesus was amazed at someone s faith Mark 6:6 Jesus was amazed at unbelief Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(60).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 2/17/2023 10:06:57 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message FUNERAL CANCELLED Luke 7:11,17 INTRODUCTION A man fell into a pit and couldn't get himself out. A Christian Scientist came along and said: "You only think that you are in a pit." A Pharisee said: "Only bad people fall into a pit." A Fundamentalist said: "You deserve your pit." An IRS man asked if he was paying taxes on the pit. A Charismatic said: "Just confess that you're not in a pit." An Optimist said: "Things could be worse." A Pessimist said: "Things will get worse!" Jesus, seeing the man, took him by the hand and lifted him out of the pit. Funerals are a familiar scene for us today You have buried relatives and friends; attended many others With two funeral homes in town, we often see the procession to the cemetery The story is told of a young preacher who was to do his first funeral So he looked into his Bible to see how Jesus handled funerals He could use the words of Jesus But Jesus never spoke at a funeral; He broke them up We have 3 occasions where Jesus went to a funeral but the dead were brought back to life The world seems to be uncaring and unsympathetic People are often cruel and indifferent We easily get wrapped up in our own lives Jesus responds to those who are suffering THE SETTING 7:11, 12 A Beautiful Village: Nain About 25 miles southwest of Capernaum; considered a days journey Only about 6 miles from Nazareth The only mention of the town in Scripture There is a village presently in this area but of a different name A Divine Appointment Still accompanied by a large group disciples a larger number than just the 12 Title makes no suggestion as to whether or not they are believers The distance would suggest Jesus headed this way before the man died as He approached the city gate no accidental occurrence; perfect providence Do you look at your chance meetings as divine appointments? Burials usually took place the day of death Tombs were in caves for the rich; graves for the poorer Cremation was reserved for notorious criminals or evil-doers An Afflicted Woman Sorrowful Past: widow; had travelled this road before Maybe time had healed some wounds but now fresh once again Bitter Present: her son was being carried out Hopeless Future: the only son of his mother Her total support system was now gone Her only source of comfort had died Emotionally and economically she is devasted God has always cared for the widows: Exodus 22:22; Deut 27:19 James 1:27 THE MIRACLE 7:13-15 Interesting to note: the closer she got to the cemetery, the closer she came to hope We are told the darkest hour is the hour before daybreak weeping may endure for a night, but joy comes in the morning (Ps. 30:5) Jesus meets the funeral; life and death come into contact Earthly sorrow is met with heavenly consolation Earthly weakness is met with heavenly strength *Heart of Sympathy: compassion Jesus knows her sorrow; she is about to see His love Deuteronomy 4:31 for the Lord your God is a compassionate God Lamentations 3:22 His compassions fail not Compassion: deep sympathy for the sorrows of others Splagna: bowels of mercies; deep feelings Compassion: your pain in my heart Note the progression: see, felt, spoke At times we see but we don t feel anything Fallen flesh is selfish and self-focused What filled His heart came out of His mouth Jesus is focused on others; we must learn that He is the Man of Sorrows Compassion is active 7:13 interesting Luke uses the title Lord at this time The Lord of Life confronts death The Lord saw - to see with the idea of responding No one interceded on behalf of the woman; she did not request Jesus help Jesus was moved from within His healings thus far: the person involved (5:12), the request of others (7:8), His own free notion *Word of Comfort: weep not To a grieving mother, the words could come across as callous Did she know who was speaking? Did He not understand? She could stop weeping because He can do something about it Jesus knew what He was going to do Timely Arrival: a short time later and burial would have been completed He touched the bier we would use the word coffin A body was wrapped and placed on a pallet for carrying Not a closed box Strange Command: Arise aorist imperative: Do it now Speaks as one having authority His Word implies the ability to respond Wondrous Change: dead sat up and began to speak One moment he is cold, helpless, silent, corrupting The next the warmth of new life and talking Happy Reunion: delivered him to his mother What comfort to a broken-hearted widow A foretaste of heaven s reunited fellowship What joy! Death had been conquered THE RESPONSE 7:16, 17 fear came on all they glorified God They were so close but yet so far They knew there was something special about Jesus but they came short of recognizing Him as God That is important because of what happens next in the text This was a sizeable crowd; those traveling with Jesus Those part of the funeral procession including professional mourners All funerals are mournful things and this one had all the markings of most sad But this funeral in Luke 7 is teachable No one said to Jesus this woman is worthy of your attention There is no evidence of faith or expectation from her There was nothing in the dead person to merit this miracle Jesus did not say, what a fine looking corpse! I think I will raise him up This miracle came totally from Christ s great compassion and love. Each time Jesus raised the dead, He did it the same way By speaking to the dead person and calling them back to life Luke 8:49-56; John 11:20-44 There is power in the spoken word of God He spoke creation into existence The centurion: just say the word Gaylord Kambarami was the General Secretary of the Bible Society of Zimbabwe. Once when he offered a man a New Testament, the man responded, If you give me that Bible, I will roll the pages and use them to make cigarettes! Gaylord replied, At least promise me that you will read the page before you smoke it. The man agreed, so he gave him the New Testament and went his way. Fifteen years later, Gaylord was attending a convention when the speaker on the platform suddenly spotted him, pointed him out to the audience, and said, This man doesn t remember me, but 15 years ago he tried to sell me a New Testament. When I refused to buy it he gave it to me, even though I told him I would use the pages to roll cigarettes. He made me promise to read the pages before I smoked them. Well, I smoked Matthew and I smoked Mark and I smoked Luke. But when I got to John 3:16 , I couldn t smoke any more. My life was changed from that moment. He had become a full-time evangelist, pointing others to the powerful message of God s Word. This is a wonderful picture of your salvation All this misery was brought to the world because of sin Romans 5:12 We are dead because of sin Ephesians 2:1 We are on our way to the cemetery of the second death Rev. 20:6 Dead people can do nothing to help themselves He did not earn a second chance of life We can only accept God s gift of life to us When Jesus imparts new life, a transformation takes place Eph 2:4-7 Old things pass away; all becomes new Those raised from the dead in Scripture 1 Kings 17:17-24 widow of Zarephath s son; Elijah 2 Kings 4:18-37 Shunammite woman s son; Elisha 2 Kings 13:20, 21 man in Elisha s grave Luke 7:11-17 Nain Luke 8:40-56 Jairus daughter John 11 Lazarus Matthew 27:50-53 various saints in Jerusalem Acts 9:36-43 Tabitha Acts 20:7-12 Eutychus Mark 16:1-8 Jesus Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(61).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 2/25/2023 12:13:52 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message I M CONFUSED Luke 7:18-35 INTRODUCTION Have you ever doubted God? Most rare is the person who had never questioned, wondered, or doubted whether God heard their prayer, whether God was interested, whether God really loved them, whether we are really saved. Doubt - The state of being uncertain about the truth or reliability of something. The etymology of doubt is interesting for it derives from Latin dubitare "to doubt, question, hesitate, waver in opinion" from duo "two" with a sense of "of two minds, undecided between two things." Synonyms: skeptical, unconvinced, unsure, puzzled, suspicious, leery Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is called the doubting disorder Can make you question things you were sure of 5 minutes ago We doubt because our faith is not perfect One writer said, I like to think of doubt as the gap between our current faith and perfect faith. Our study in Luke brings us to a study about doubting. The big questions are who is doubting and what does Jesus say about it Is our doubting acceptable to Jesus? Or to put it another way: Is our lack of faith acceptable to Jesus? Jesus is still in the first half of His public ministry He is establishing who He is in the minds of the people Through His teaching ministry and miracles, the people are required to make a decision as to His claim to Messiahship We know the people of Nazareth made their decision: 4:29 We have seen the growing hostility of scribes and Pharisees But we have also received testimony of the amazing faith of the centurion 7:9 That although Jesus commended the great faith of only two (Matthew 15:28 and Luke 7), combining amazing and faith in only for the centurion. That makes the account of 7:18-35 the more intriguing We go from a Roman soldier to the greatest prophet the world has known We move from amazing faith to questioning who Jesus really is Please note: Jesus does not rebuke John s inquiry But four times in the gospels Jesus does say, o ye of little faith Matthew 6:30 when it comes to being clothed (Luke 12:28) Matthew 8:26 when it comes to a storm on the lake Matthew 14:31 when it comes to sinking in the water Matthew 16:8 when it comes to having food So before we justify our lack of faith by pointing to John the Baptist, remember Jesus is interested in the confidence of our faith James 1:5-6 (WEB) But if any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask of God, who gives to all liberally and without reproach; and it will be given to him. But let him ask in faith, without any doubting, for he who doubts is like a wave of the sea, driven by the wind and tossed. THE COMPANY OF DOUBTERS Eve: doubt was a tool of Satan for us to question God s Word Abraham: Genesis 17:17 I m too old to have a child Sarah: Genesis 18:12 I m too old to have a child Moses: Exodus 3:10-15 Who am I that I should go to Pharaoh Gideon: Judges 6:13-23 I am least in my father s house Elijah: 1 Kings 19:1-14 Let me die Zechariah: (Luke 1) displayed his unbelief with too many obstacles and became mute Thomas: the most famous doubter Several: Matthew 28:17 after the resurrection some were doubtful A healthy skepticism is beneficial: Acts 17:11 Bereans searching THE QUESTION OF JOHN There are some who believer John was not asking for himself but for his disciples who had not yet followed Jesus He wanted them to be convinced knowing that his own death was imminent Possible but the text clearly indicates the inquiry comes from John himself John s Testimony His disciples were reporting to him the workings of Jesus v. 18 John s own testimony: Not the sort of man who vacillated: 7:24 Filled with the Spirit: 1:15 John 1:32-34 received direct revelation as to Christ s coming John 1:29 behold, the Lamb of God John 1:35-37 urged his own disciples to follow Jesus John 3:30 He must increase, I must decrease John was imprisoned for speaking directly in confronting sin of adultery John s Confusion Doubt is not necessarily the same as unbelief Sometimes doubt could be described as confusion Luke 3:7-9; 16, 17 John clearly saw the coming of Jesus as bringing judgment All the reports were not about judgment but about healing And not in Jerusalem, the city of the king, but far away in Galillee All there was minimal reception among the people John wondered if he had misunderstood his role and Jesus agenda John s confusion does not necessarily indicate a lack of faith John s confusion is the same reason we battle with doubt Primary reason for doubts: Expectations 7:20 are you the one who comes or do we seek another? Not that he got his revelation wrong but maybe there will be another one who will bring judgment The proposed judgment was not happening The elevation of Jesus as King was not taking place, in fact, John was already sensing this was not going to end well This was not the scenario John pictured in his mind Our doubting usually centers around unfulfilled expectations We had it all figured out. We would pray and Jesus would answer as we predicted. We would obey and Jesus would bring about our prosperity We would read our Bible devotedly for a week and Jesus would open the windows of heaven and shower the blessings our way It was all about our expectations and not God s Psalm 62:5 (WEB) My soul, wait in silence for God alone, For my expectation is from him. We presumed God would cater to our wishes We assumed God would see it the way we see it John presented the message given to him but even though he was the greatest prophet, he only saw judgment and not salvation John s Confinement The man who spent his life outdoors in the wild, now was place in a prison cell The unpleasant conditions, the prospect of coming death, only added to his inquiry of Jesus I am sure that John expected King Jesus to come and release him from his captivity His present condition only added to his confusion Let s face it, there are times we question our lot in life We ask why. We grumble. We believe we deserve better than we have. We see confinement as limiting instead of a time to worship Think Paul and Silas; Acts 16 This is what makes doubters different than the centurion The centurion s faith was pure and not mingled with doubt because he did not have the same expectations as the Jews The Jews saw their coming King in things He should do and would do and when He did not do them, it led to their doubting. A Gentile did not have the OT and Jewish traditions and culture And here then we find doubt on the part of John the Baptist, the greatest man who ever lived. Doubt is very real for people who believe. It is reality but it is not an acceptable reality and it needs to be addressed. Moses doubted God. Gideon doubted God. Elijah doubted God. Jeremiah even expressed doubt. The apostles doubted and here John the Baptist doubts. We understand that. Coming face-to-face with doubt is coming face-to-face with ourselves. - MacArthur We see here that even great men can have a season when the cloud over them hinders their spiritual vision Like Abraham, Moses, Elijah, doubt, confusion, uncertainty settled in Romans 15:4 these things are written for our instruction CONCLUSION We will return to this passage next week, but something to consider for today Yes, John expressed some confusion, doubt But what did he do? John brought his doubts to Jesus When we are imprisoned by our own doubts, go to Jesus When we are confined by confusion, go to Jesus And what did Jesus do? Right back to the Scriptures: Isaiah 61 Thy Word is a light to my path; a lamp to my feet Bring your doubts into the light of God s Word I M CONFUSED Luke 7:18-35 Doubt - The state of being uncertain about the truth or reliability of something We doubt because our faith is not perfect Is our lack of faith acceptable to Jesus? Jesus does not rebuke John s inquiry James 1:5-6 THE COMPANY OF DOUBTERS Genesis 17:17 Genesis 18:12 Exodus 3:10-15 Judges 6:13-23 1 Kings 19:1-14 Matthew 28:17 A healthy skepticism is beneficial: Acts 17:11 THE QUESTION OF JOHN John s Testimony Not the sort of man who vacillated: 7:24 John s Confusion Doubt is not necessarily the same as unbelief Primary reason for doubts: Expectations Psalm 62:5 John s Confinement His present condition only added to his confusion The centurion s faith was pure and not mingled with doubt We see here that even great men can have a season when the cloud over them hinders their spiritual vision John brought his doubts to Jesus Bring your doubts into the light of God s Word Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(62).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 3/3/2023 8:33:09 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message GETTING IT RIGHT Luke 7:24-35 RIGHT BY JOHN Explaining John s Disposition 7:24 Jesus did not want to leave the wrong impression to the observers So He defends John s reputation Jesus will provide a strong and positive affirmation John had godly convictions; would not bow to outside pressure To compare a person to a reed was to say that the person was without moral fiber or courage, easily tossed about by various opinions, never taking a stand on anything John was not some namby, pamby, spineless preacher bowing before every breath of popular favor or opposition John stood for truth and refused to compromise with evil That is why he was in prison Explaining John s Dress 7:25 dressed in silks and satins ? behold could substitute Hardly! A worldly, self-indulgent person would not deny himself the finest clothes or the best food Soft raiment: soft to the touch; effeminate John was a rugged man of the wilderness His diet was the food of the poor Wild hone and locusts; kosher (Ex. 3:8; Lev. 11:22) Poor people wove camel s hair into a course cloth As is their clothing, so is their doctrine. Those dressed in silks and satins don t preach repentance They present a royal road to heaven; smooth and easy Explaining John s Doctrine 7:26, 27 John was a prophet who spoke the message God gave him But he was more than a prophet He himself was the subject of prophecy John was not only a prophet but a messenger who introduced Jesus Other prophets could say, the Messiah is coming John had the privilege of saying, The Messiah is here! Quoting Malachi 3:1; confirmed by John s father (1:76) The prophecies also affirmed the deity of Jesus as both Lord and Messiah Since John announced the coming of Jesus, then Jesus must be God s chosen Messiah It is written written down in the past; it stands written, it is permanent Explaining John s Dispensation 7:28-30 John was the last of the OT prophets; still part of the Old Convenant His ministry ushered in the new dispensation Jesus was not depreciating John; he fulfilled his stewardship well He was explaining why John had asked such perplexing questions Great prophet though John was, he had not yet entered the Kingdom His knowledge was limited How are believers today greater than John? Those living in the new age of the Holy Spirit have greater advantages than John possessed John could only predict the saving and baptizing work of Christ Matt. 3:11 The humblest believer can testify to His finished work Also believers are indwelt by the Spirit John s ministry ended the time of promise Jesus ministry began the time of fulfillment Those who come into the kingdom are greater than John Not because they are more important but because they are recipients of God s blessings found in Jesus Christ Believers are God s spiritual children and enjoy a relationship that John never experienced John never experienced the benefits of the New Covenant To enjoy the blessings of the kingdom is greater than to be the forerunner of the King. J. C. Ryle: Such an expression as this should teach all Christians to be deeply thankful for Christianity. We have probably very little idea of the wide difference between the religious knowledge of the best-instructed Old Testament believer and the knowledge of one familiar with the New Testament. We little know how many blessed truths of the Gospel were at one time seen through a glass darkly, which now appear to us plain as noon-day. Our very familiarity with the Gospel makes us blind to the extent of our privileges. We can hardly realize at this time how many glorious verities of our faith were brought out in their full proportions by Christ s death on the cross, and were never unveiled and understood till His blood was shed. The hopes of John the Baptist and St. Paul were undoubtedly one and the same. Both were led by one Spirit. Both knew their sinfulness. Both trusted in the Lamb of God. But we cannot suppose that John could have given as full an account of the way of salvation as St. Paul. Both looked at the same object of faith. But one saw it afar off, and could only describe it generally. The other saw it close at hand, and could describe the reason of his hope particularly. Let us learn to be more thankful. The child who knows the story of the cross, possesses a key to religious knowledge which patriarchs and prophets never enjoyed. Explaining John s Discourse 7:29, 30 NIV (All the people, even the tax collectors, when they heard [Jesus' words], acknowledged that God's way was right, because they had been baptized by John. The common people, including publicans, recognized John as God s man with God s message for them Justified God means they declared their confidence in God by accepting John s baptism They admitted God was right ; honored God; vindicated the righteous requirements of God They considered God was right in making these demands upon them "Being baptized by John demonstrated a recognition of one s sin, a desire for spiritual cleansing, and a commitment to follow God s law in anticipation of the Messiah s arrival....John prepared the way for Christ by calling people to acknowledge their sin and their need for salvation. His baptism was a purification ceremony meant to ready the peoples hearts to receive their Savior. " (GotQuestions.org) Acts 19:4 (WEB) Paul said, "John indeed baptized with the baptism of repentance, saying to the people that they should believe in the one who would come after him, that is, in Jesus." It was a humble recognition on one s position before God In accepting John as a true prophet, the people were also preparing their hearts to accept Jesus as Messiah 30 The ruling classes; Pharisees and lawyers rejected Jesus and His claim to be Messiah The religious leaders were going to do things their way My religion is fine! I am comfortable the way it is. They made the forms of the Law and their own traditions central to religion When the Pharisees and lawyers despised John and treated him with contempt, they were actually rejecting the wise will of God and making it of no effect in their lives they rejected what God had planned for them repudiate the counsel spurned God s plan rejected God s will No different than the response to the gospel message today All who refuse declare that God is right, they have no need of a Savior, they will live life as they well please. I don t think most people are actively saying God is wrong But their refusal to submit to His Lordship is doing the same thing We declare God to be right the when we accept Jesus as our Savior and Lord GETTING IT RIGHT Luke 7:24-35 RIGHT BY JOHN Explaining John s Disposition 7:24 John had godly convictions John stood for truth and refused to compromise with evil Explaining John s Dress 7:25 John was a rugged man of the wilderness His diet was the food of the poor Explaining John s Doctrine 7:26, 27 John was not only a prophet but a messenger It is written it stands written, it is permanent Explaining John s Dispensation 7:28-30 The humblest believer can testify to His finished work John s ministry ended the time of promise Jesus ministry began the time of fulfillment John never experienced the benefits of the New Covenant Explaining John s Discourse 7:29, 30 They considered God was right in making these demands upon them Acts 19:4 The religious leaders were going to do things their way They were actually rejecting the wise will of God and making it of no effect in their lives We declare God to be right when we accept Jesus as our Savior and Lord Dwain A Hill ============================================= === SERMON: message(63).txt ============================================= From: Sent: 3/8/2023 12:38:42 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message GETTING IT RIGHT Luke 7:24-35 RIGHT BY JESUS 7:30-35 31 Some of the people who followed John at the beginning seemed to have forgotten him Many others had not gone on to follow Jesus People of that day were no different from people of today People tend to mock and deride what they do not like or understand The people of Jesus day were so wrapped up in their own traditions and manmade doctrines, they could not see truth when they faced Him John 14:6 So Jesus was looking for an analogy to what do I compare this generation?
