The host and his guests wanted to catch Jesus in a violation of the Sabbath watched watched like a hawk; scrutinizing Paul says you are like a letter to be read by those around you 2 Corinthians 3:2,3 Being under so close a watch, we will eventually disappoint Never so with Jesus; had to be exhausting to watch and never find a fault But remember the true test as to a person s character is their behavior when no one (humanly speaking) is watching The enemies of Jesus felt He was most vulnerable to a charge of breaking the Law But like other times the trap snaps on the trappers Spiritual blindness is an amazing thing Legalism blinds one to grace behold - Luke loves that word; look! See! Highlight it; pay attention Did the man with dropsy just happen to drop in Some believe the sick fellow was uninvited; not unusual for visitors (7:36) others believe he was a plant It was a purposeful setup to put Jesus to the test right in front of Him Knowing the aversion the Pharisees have toward the outcast, this was setup Jesus did not shy away from doing good simply because somebody would be offended Dropsy is not a disease but the sign of an underlying problem The Pharisees identified it as an immoral or physical problem It is the accumulation of excess water in parts of the body. The word means to look watery ; Since they wore robes, it was probably swelling in the face The word used today is edema; fluid retention SABBATH SILENCE 14:3, 4 Jesus answered a question that wasn t asked audibly He read the hypocritical hearts of the lawyers and Pharisees His question put them on the defensive The Law did not forbid healing on the Sabbath It was their traditions and not the writings of Moses that forbade such activities They would not acknowledge that the Law did not forbid the healing They were between a rock and hard place of their own making To do so would be to remove the one thing they had planned to use against the Lord Their refusal had the same effect If they could not point out the illegality in healing before it was done, they would not be able to do so after the event either They became silent; no whispering; no grumbling According to the traditions of the scribes, the only time for healing on the Sabbath was when the life of the person being healed was in jeopardy. Obviously, Jesus did not agree with that interpretation Jesus believed the law of love took precedence Jesus had used a similar argument just a few days earlier 13:15, 16 The same argument was used in Capernaum; Matt. 12:11, 12 The logic of the argument now, as on the other occasions, left His enemies speechless Jesus could have avoided any argument and told the fellow to come back in a few hours; the Sabbath would be over and avoid a confrontation Jesus healed him and sent him on his way; no reaction recorded The focus of Jesus was on the spiritually sick Pharisees; an illness far worse than edema Jesus is confrontational; He will confront your sin He does it out of love for a good reason; so you will inherit rewards SABBATH SHAME 14:5, 6 14:6 and just in case the Pharisees begin to gloat over their gotcha The logic of the question is indisputable Some translation use son instead of donkey, but Love becomes the issue Love of family and love of money; animal or kinfolk you save them Interesting Jesus says they fell into a well One might tread water for a while but eventually grow tired and drown Here was a man drowning in his own fluids and they were unwilling to see him rescued They cared more for their animals than this man Jesus exposed their lack of love and religious hypocrisy In the last chapter Jesus accused them of violating their own rules by giving drink to their animals on the Sabbath He is exposing their inconsistencies They did not respond out of compassion for their animals but because of monetary purposes; the animals made them money They had greater care for monetary gain than a suffering man When you are trouble by overlapping or conflicting regulations, choose love There was no such overlapping here; simply Pharisaical abuse Jesus healed him Jesus was not out to shame the Pharisees but to save them call them to repentance by exposing their wicked hearts They were silent; did not have the strength to respond 14:4 they were unwilling to answer; 14:6 they were unable to answer Just like selfish children; backed into a corner and unwilling to admit their guilt Why? Pride Have you ever faced that situation? Backed into a corner but your pride refuses to admit your guilt? You play the blame game, point the finger at others. Refuse to be humbled and accept the discipline It is painful to root hypocrisy out of our souls. We must dig deep. It is easy to build a case to defend your point of view and avoid overwhelming biblical evidence that convicts you of your sin. This incident at the Sabbath meal sets the table for the words of Jesus yet to come in this chapter. HUMILITY, HOSPITALITY, HESISTANTCY DEALING WITH THE LOFTY Luke 14:1-12 SABBATH SETUP 14:1, 2 Nothing just happens in God s creation Luke records 5 of the 7 Sabbath healings It was a popular meal in the life of a Jewish family. watched watched like a hawk; scrutinizing 2 Corinthians 3:2,3 Legalism blinds one to grace Jesus did not shy away from doing good simply because somebody would be offended Dropsy to look watery SABBATH SILENCE 14:3, 4 The Law did not forbid healing on the Sabbath If they could not point out the illegality in healing before it was done, they would not be able to do so after the event either Jesus believed the law of love took precedence Jesus is confrontational; He will confront your sin SABBATH SHAME 14:5, 6 The logic of the question is indisputable They had greater care for monetary gain than a suffering man 14:4 they were unwilling to answer; 14:6 they were unable to answer It is painful to root hypocrisy out of our souls DEALING WITH THE LOFTY Luke 14:1-12 SABBATH SETUP 14:1, 2 Nothing just ______________ in God s creation Luke records _____ of the 7 Sabbath healings It was a ______________ meal in the life of a Jewish family. watched watched like a hawk; ______________ 2 Corinthians 3:2,3 Legalism ____________ one to grace Jesus did not shy away from doing good simply because somebody would be ____________ Dropsy - to look ____________ SABBATH SILENCE 14:3, 4 The Law did not forbid _____________ on the Sabbath If they could not point out the illegality in healing ___________ it was done, they would not be able to do so ____________ the event Jesus believed the law of _____________ took precedence Jesus is confrontational; He will confront ___________ sin SABBATH SHAME 14:5, 6 The ____________ of the question is indisputable They had greater care for monetary gain than a _____________ man 14:4 they were ____________ to answer; 14:6 they were _____________ to answer It is ______________ to root hypocrisy out of our souls ============================================= === SERMON: message(128).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 10/11/2024 11:14:49 AM To: "Hill Jonathan" Subject: message SHOTGUN LUKE 14:7-11 INTRODUCTION ME FIRST COMMOTION 14:7-11 Target audience: Pharisees; lawyers Jesus has observed how the invited guests arrived for the meal Their character could best be described as self-centered First, by their lack of concern for the man with the dropsy Second, by their attempts to seek the seats of prestige at the banquet table noted , noticed, - marked, literally hold upon ; pay attention Chose shotgun speak out best places first recline at the dinner bed; place of prominence Interesting point: v. 1 they were watching Jesus; v. 7 He was watching them He was not watching to trip them up but to confront them with their sin And hypocrisy Would be good for us to remember Jesus notices everything 1 Corinthians 4:5; Ecc. 12:14 No one likes to be taken for granted; we like to feel important Becomes a problem when our status in the eyes of others takes precedence We will do things to get noticed; remind people we are still here Reputation can become an idol; people crave to be recognized What we drive; where we eat; what we wear; where we live Matthew 23:2-7 (Luke 11:43) What not to do: don t choose or seek a place of honor The hassle was over who would sit in the honored position The sin Jesus is describing is pride These people were invited guests It was not up to them to decide where they would sit Give preference to other people Were you ever put out by the location of your assigned table at a wedding reception? Your first thought was it will take forever to get to the buffet well, at least we are close to the bathrooms! Every child knows how to play, Look at me! Embarrassing when still played by adults It is good to remember that some may become too big for God to use, You will never become to small for God to use 1 Peter 5:6 Proverbs 25:6, 7 As hard as it might be for a Pharisee to admit it, there just could be someone at the feast who would be considered greater than himself DEMOTION 14:9 If one had already seated himself in the place of honor, he probably had already assumed the false air on an honored guest to those sitting around him. To be moved to a lower position would result in a humiliating loss of face before the others The shame of the reluctant move The only seats left by this time would be the very lowest seats; the ones farthest from the host Such a blow to his pride would be devastating to a very egotistical individual The people they were trying to impress were not impressed at all Jesus emphasized they had no right to seek out the chief seats They were invited Because of their pride they even failed to exercise the common courtesies expected of guests. Romans 12:9, 10 let love be without dissimulation . . . Be kindly affectioned one to another with brotherly love; in honor preferring one another Guzik - The Bible reminds us that we should not play the self-promotion game. We should do our work hard and unto the Lord, and let God raise us up. For exaltation comes neither from the east nor from the west nor from the south, but God is the Judge: He puts down one, and exalts another. (Psalm 75:6 7 ) Humiliation is not the same as humility The first is the rotten fruit that pride reaps The second is the antitheses of pride Humiliation is the result of external circumstances, whereas the humility is a reflection of the internal condition of one's heart. Stated another way humiliation is often a reflection of the absence of humility Pride brought the whole human race to destruction: be as God A haughty spirit looks up and is not watching the steps take hold down the farthest spot 4:42; 8:15 PROMOTION 14:10 Jesus was not saying that we should be clever and deliberately take the lower position with the hope that in doing so we will be invited to move up to a higher place If a person is really one of esteem, he will not have to force it on others By showing his humility others will see to it that he receives whatever is his due The main thing, however, is to live the truly humble life whether one s greatness is recognized or not Everyone before God ought to feel the lowest place is the proper place for him - Plummer Jesus is not promoting a false humility which is nothing more than pride It is better for others to acknowledge you than for you to suggest your proper place Let another man praise thee, and not thine own mouth; a stranger, and not thine own lips. Proverbs 27:2 It is an act of grace on the part of the host. worship means honor Honor will be given to the person who does not seize if for himself This honor will come from an outside source Look at the heart of the apostle Paul 1 Corinthians 15:9 For I am the least of the apostle Ephesians 3:8 less than the least of the saints 1 Timothy 1:15 I am the chief of sinners Solomon Proverbs 25:6, 7 Put not thyself forward in the presence of the king, And stand not in the place of great men: For better is it that it be said unto thee, Come up hither, Than that thou shouldest be put lower in the presence of the prince, Whom thine eyes have seen. 14:11 the spiritual axiom for a term of explanation; in this case application Whosoever exalteth himself shall be abased This principle was not new to the time of Jesus Proverbs 16:18 Pride goeth before destruction and a haughty spirit before a fall The applies whether we are dealing with people or God James 4:6-10 And you can identify many who have yet to be humbled We will get to chapter 16:22-23a v. 11 will be humbled will be exalted future passive God will bring the ruin or the reward Mattoon: Humility is a realistic assessment and commitment to serve the Lord Jesus Christ and others. Humility is also a fundamental grace in the Christian life, and yet it is elusive. If you know you have it, you have lost it! It has well been said that humility is not thinking meanly of ourselves. It is simply not thinking of ourselves at all. Jesus is the greatest example of humility, and we would do well to ask the Holy Spirit to enable us to imitate His example. The most lofty became the most lowly Philippians 2:8 Crawford: The desire for prominence, greatness, eminence, renown, prestige, honour, place, or whatever we may call it, is one of the most pronounced features of fallen humanity. It is a chief cause of assembly (church) problems. This desire to put forward self can assume many disguises and take many forms, but human pride is hateful to God in whatever form it takes (Pr 16:5 ). None is immune from this fearful disease of selfish pride, but let us ever judge it as sin, and never adopt the spirit of this age that glorifies it. Let s deal with some psyco-babble for a second You hear people talk about low self-esteem; or you hear I hate myself Low self-esteem can be nothing more than pity for our own inadequacy We are told to think more highly of ourselves The same goes for I just hate myself Biblical balance: No man ever hated his own flesh (Eph. 5:29) Romans 12:3 sober assessment (5 Relationships: God: sacrifice; World: separation; Believers: serve in love; Unbelievers: supernatural response; Self: sober assessment God s response is not unlike that of men when it comes to making a judgment of a person s character based on that person s willingness to humble himself or his unwillingness to do so The humble individual in the kingdom of God understands his position He knows he is a child of God and that is honor enough for any person Since God is the One who ultimately humbles and exalts, the Christian will humble himself both before God and man Humility: realizing that God and others are responsible for the achievements in my life. 1 Corinthians 4:7 The pursuit of a humble attitude is our continual practice It is a choice of our will; we willingly accept humility The way into God s kingdom is first down Matthew 5:6 blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of God Spurgeon: Let us be (be willing to be) humble that we may not need to be humbled, but may be exalted by the grace of God. Acknowledge your inability to earn God s favor, we do fall short; yet we reach out for saving grace By the Word of God Alone By Grace Alone By Faith Alone By Christ Alone To the Glory of God Alone Two things about pride: It is hard to see in ourselves There is a test called the Pride Quotient: the P.Q. 1. I enjoy being the center of attention. 2. I think I deserve the best. 3. Much of my conversation is filled with I . 4. I find it difficult to admit that I'm wrong. 5. I seldom pass a mirror without looking at myself. 6. I'm stubborn I don't like to be corrected or challenged. 7. My feelings are easily hurt. 8. I am impatient with other people's mistakes. 9. I don't get enough appreciation for all I do. 10. I'm offended if I render a service and don't receive a Thank You. 11. I seldom ask for help, because I can do the job better myself. It leads to ruin: Proverbs 16:18 Prov. 6 what the Lord hates a proud look Pride makes you run to get the best seat Pride makes sure you eat before anyone else Pride makes you believe people need to hear what you have to say Pride makes you compare yourself with others when the real comparison is with Jesus. Pride cause Commotion Jesus rewards with Promotion SHOTGUN LUKE 14:7-11 COMMOTION 14:7-11 Their character could best be described as self-centered v. 1 they were watching Jesus; v. 7 He was watching them Reputation can become an idol Matthew 23:2-7 The sin Jesus is describing is pride You will never become to small for God to use 1 Peter 5:6 Proverbs 25:6, 7 DEMOTION 14:9 The shame of the reluctant move Jesus emphasized they had no right to seek out the chief seats Romans 12:9, 10 Humiliation is the result of external circumstances, whereas the humility is a reflection of the internal condition of one's heart. A haughty spirit looks up and is not watching the steps PROMOTION 14:10 The main thing, however, is to live the truly humble life whether one s greatness is recognized or not Jesus is not promoting a false humility Proverbs 16:18 James 4:6-10 God will bring the ruin or the reward The most lofty became the most lowly Philippians 2:8 The humble individual in the kingdom of God understands his position Humility: realizing that God and others are responsible for the achievements in my life. 1 Corinthians 4:7 ============================================= === SERMON: message(129).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 10/18/2024 12:01:41 PM To: "Hill Jonathan" Subject: message R.O.I. Luke 14:12-14 INTRODUCTION Matthew 25:31-40 Chapter of dealing with the hypocrisy of the religious leaders Specifically calling out the Pharisees 14:1-6 Hypocrisy 14:7-11 Humility The lesson on humility continues in these 3 verses and could have been easily included in the message from last week, but that would have made a long message even longer. In Verses 7-11, Jesus seized upon the arrival of guests for a Sabbath meal as a teaching tool. Now He turns to the host of the meal for further instruction REPAYMENT 14:12 The topic is humiity The problem is the lack of humility The common practice is to invite only those who would return the favor with an invitation of their own Illustration: have you ever been invited someplace where your first reaction is I don t want to go because that means we need to invite them over OR Since they invited us to their kids wedding, we should invite them to ours Reciprocity: exchanging things for mutual benefit Governments do that It is the old you scratch my back and I will scratch yours The verse mentions dinner and supper as the normal meals of the day Dinner means breakfast or brunch and supper in the evening main meal The feast was more of a banquet or formal reception where guests would be in attendance. Here Jesus is including everyday meals as times when sincere hospitality may be shown Jesus knew this host had invited people for two reasons as repayment for a previous invitation To put them under debt for a return engagement It was not an expression of love or grace but pride and selfishness The host was buying recognition (WWW) call not do not invite imperative; command Stop inviting only friends to the exclusion of others Break up the mutual admiration society Give yourself to those in need, not just those of the proper social standing Luke 6:34, 35 Darrell Bock: The major point is that customary pay back hospitality is of no great merit to God. Fellowship should not have social limits. The best hospitality is that which is given, not exchanged. REQUIREMENT 14:13 but alla; a major contrast is presented the way of the Pharisees who sought to be exalted among men, and the way of God, which is the humble path that leads to true blessing. The focus should be on those who have needs and are unable to return the invitation We have to understand this suggestion would have been totally absurd to a Pharisee You don t rub shoulders with those of a lower class Having those folks present will not elevate you in the eyes of the people These people will not help in the prestige quotient. The OT clearly taught concern for the poor and powerless Dt. 14:28 29 ; 16:11 14 ; 26:11 13 It is a blessed ministry to give gifts when they cannot be repaid It is more blessed to give than to receive Acts 20:35 Never quoted in the gospels; definitely the sentiment of Jesus and evidently He had said it or Paul would not have quoted Him The challenge of Jesus is to give without manipulation Serving for what you can get out of it We serve loving God and loving others Steven Cole: Often there are many blessings that come back on you in this life when you serve the Lord. But, often there are not any visible rewards here and now. You serve and no one notices. You give to help a needy person and you get ripped off, and the person never even says, Thanks. The Pride Quotient from last week: 9. I don't get enough appreciation for all I do. 10. I'm offended if I render a service and don't receive a Thank You. What are my motives in my service? Do you use people or love them? REWARD 14:14 The point is you give expecting nothing in return That is why you never loan money; just give it If it comes back, there is greater joy because you were not expecting it Check your motives: compassion, love, selfless, graciousness When the motive is right We are laying up treasure in heaven (12:33) We are becoming rich toward God (12:21) We can easily get sucked into a world system where profit and loss are more important than sacrifice and service blessed an inward contentedness not affected by circumstances A wonderful state of joy and well-being How will you be blessed? For here is the explanation you will be repaid passive; God Himself; not those invited Is He not a rewarder of those who diligently seek Him? That God would save us is "reward" enough, but that He will reward us in eternity future is nothing short of indescribably amazing grace! Compare that with the repayment of one man to another God in infinitely rich and generous Proverbs 19:17 The distributions of resources will take place at the resurrection Do you have anything laid up to receive? Here is your return on investment Daniel 12:2, 3 And many of them that sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt. And they that are wise shall shine as the brightness of the firmament; and they that turn many to righteousness as the stars for ever and ever. John 5:28, 29 Marvel not at this: for the hour cometh, in which all that are in the tombs shall hear his voice, and shall come forth; they that have done good, unto the resurrection of life; and they that have done evil, unto the resurrection of judgment. Revelation 22:12 Behold, I come quickly; and my reward is with me, to render to each man according as his work is. John Piper: his review of our text This is the way Jesus said the hope of the resurrection is supposed to change our behavior. For example, he told us to invite to our homes people who cannot pay us back in this life. How are we to be motivated to do this? You will be repaid at the resurrection of the just (Luke 14:14 ). This is a radical call for us to look hard at our present lives to see if they are shaped by the hope of the resurrection. The Return On your Investment 6:22, 23 when you are persecuted for the name of Christ 14:12-14 when you love and serve those who can t pay you back The wonderful things He has planned for us in the future Just a "cup of cold water" was given in His name, But the soul of the giver was never the same! For he found that when giving was done with a zest, Both the heart of the giver and taker were blest. Anon. R.O.I. Luke 14:12-14 REPAYMENT 14:12 The topic is humiity The host was buying recognition Stop inviting only friends to the exclusion of others Darrell Bock: The best hospitality is that which is given, not exchanged REQUIREMENT 14:13 The focus should be on those who have needs and are unable to return the invitation It is a blessed ministry to give gifts when they cannot be repaid Acts 20:35 The challenge of Jesus is to give without manipulation REWARD 14:14 The point is you give expecting nothing in return When the motive is right We are laying up treasure in heaven (12:33) We are becoming rich toward God (12:21) blessed an inward contentedness not affected by circumstances God in infinitely rich and generous Proverbs 19:17 Daniel 12:2, 3 John 5:28, 29 Revelation 22:12 The Return On your Investment ============================================= === SERMON: message(130).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 10/25/2024 8:14:42 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message CUT ME SOME SLACK Luke 14:15-24 INTRODUCTION Luke 14 HYPOCRISY, HUMILITY, HOSPITALITY Just wait a minute. Now, hold on. Cool your jets. Making excuses. Definition of Excuse: to make an apology; to try to remove blame release from a duty or requirement An explanation to defend or justify a fault or offense To make an allowance for Lame excuse: reasoning that is considered pathetically unconvincing or inadequate. I read of one father who declined an invitation for the reason he had to change the furnace filter. THE INVITATIONS 14:15, 16 Someone at the meal, probably a Pharisee speaks up Might have heard Jesus mention the resurrection that prompted his remark Basically ignoring everything that had been said about humilty Now assuming a superior position based on his status Taking for granted his place in the eternal kingdom The OT prophets had warned that selfish, luxury-loving lives and religion devoid of real love will bring darkness and not light Amos 5:18-24; 6:1-6 Here is a fellow, representative of many, who was depending on their status as a child of Abraham for ready admittance into glory He is toasting himself and his fellow Pharisees Those who keep the Law, follow the traditions, maintain ceremonial purity They saw themselves as a few notches above the rest There is no way God would have such a banquet with them not present The parable presented by Jesus speaks to his assumption An assumption resulting in individual and national pride Pride and doctrinal error are not exposed The parable was given to show how God s offer of salvation is rejected by many who are invited Luke s account is similar to the wedding feast in Matthew 22:1-14 The certain man refers to God The feast is the great blessing of salvation The Pharisee was about to learn that admittance to the banquet is only of those who respond to the invitation. The custom was to send out two invitations: one announcing the coming banquet and the second to say the banquet is ready; come now This was a mega supper; big, huge, grand; a celebration of such magnitude that anyone would gratefully attend This is no peanut butter and jelly sandwich affair; this is beyond all tables This is where you would want to be seen This is your red-carpet moment; the time to shine This is salvation; this is the banquet table with Christ This is the fountain of living water; this is the indwelling Christ; this is rich daily fellowship with a gracious Savior; this is eternal life THE EXCLUSION 14:17-20 The OT prophets had given the first invitation They told about the coming King and kingdom John the Baptist and Jesus began to give the second call As the word servant is singular, it would point to Christ in the fullness of time (Galatians 4:4) The preparations of previous ages Roman Roads: means of travel Greek language: one of the most precise languages for communicating exact meaning All arrangements are now complete This is what Jesus told us in John 14; I go to prepare a place for you Preparations had to be made; Hebrews 9:11, 12, 23, 24; 10:12 And they all began with one consent in italics; not in original But does carry the sense; implies a conspiracy in rejecting the invitation with one mind without exception unanimously WEB: they all began as one all means all The word excuse is used 9x in the NT; this is the first; note the last Hebrews 12:25 None of the guests refused to come because they had forgotten They had either changed their minds concerning the host or They had come to believe the invitation was not important John 8:19 the Jews did not know God and rejected His Son Note the repetition of I in all of the excuses All excuses are extremely weak The people cared only about their own selfish desires and plans They really considered God s kingdom as something trivial, unimportant The kingdom would only be considered if it did not interfere with their own desires and ambitions At this stage, the listeners are right with Jesus. They would never consider turning down such an invitation. You don t say no to a big event. You don t turn down an extravagant supper from a wealthy host. They may even be laughing at such a notion. It would be preposterous. They would even find the excuses most humorous bought a field agros; field, land Two questions immediately come to mind: Who buys a field without seeing it first? Where is it going to go? five yoke of oxen this guy has money Only the rich could afford one ox; this guy has 10 Would you buy a car without a test drive? Owning the land and the oxen, signs of wealth I go worded to say he is already headed that direction Besides that, it is supper; it is night; not the time for ox tryouts Much more important the responding to God Either of these men could have gone after the feast to do as they wanted The real problem was that the feast was not important to them Demonstrates the absurdity of the excuses I got married Does not even ask to be excused; he simply says he is not coming He puts family before the invitation to salvation The OT excuses for a married man: Deut. 24:5 This is the only excuse that is plausible The wife said, NO. Has nothing to wear Happy Wife, Happy Life MacArthur: Given their low view of women, the Pharisees would have found this excuse the most laughable of all. In first-century Jewish society, women did not dictate to their husbands what they could do What keeps folks from following Jesus? Possessions, People None of these reasons are sinful: nothing wrong with having stuff or loving family But the point is, things that are legitimate in their rightful place can be wrong if they hinder us from getting right with God. (Cole) Good things keep people out of the kingdom of God THE INCLUSION 14:21-24 The report is presented The master of the house being angry Literally: the being angry master of the house God is not wishy-washy in dealing with the rejectors Note also: notice the Servant (Jesus) did not plead mercy for them being angry the Master of the house did not get angry It was His nature to be angry with such an obvious rejection of His invitation This is His response for rejecting His Son God does not get angry with sin; It is His nature to abhor sin Romans 1:18; John 3:36 The feast is now ready The banquet must be served; the celebration will take place God will not be thwarted in His plan to provide salvation for mankind The invitation must be honored now; today is the day of salvation The rejection of the Jews did not hinder God s plan Go out bring sense of urgency; do it now Those in the streets and lanes of the city represented the despised classes among the Jews People whom the Pharisees considered to be already under the judgment of God The poor had neither time nor money to fulfill the demands of the religious leaders They were made to feel they could not be restored to fellowship with God Those with physical defects were cut off from temple worship Jesus touched all of these people He showed that God would reach out to them highways the main roads coming into town Reach out from one s own town into areas beyond hedges the place of the homeless; vagrants The invitation is presented to the whole world compel not drag but constrain; express the necessity of coming v. 18 need 21:23 distress 23:17 necessity this group would need extra convincing as they would be reluctant to come on their own. They did not have the means to return the favor They have spent their lives believing they were of a lower class Surely had no business at such a glorious gathering just the offer of food should bring in the hungry it is on thing to find people in town, another to look out in the country require a more diligent search an illusion to the inclusion of Gentiles 2 Corinthians 5:20 There is no doubt that these people also had legitimate excuses They accepted the offer because the servant convinced them they were welcome Cole: The invitation was not based on anything in the recipients; it was based totally on the goodness and bounty of the host. All that these people had to do was recognize their hunger, believe that the offer was true, and say, Yes, I ll come. v. 24 not to say that no Jews will be saved the early church was composed exclusively of Jews in context, those who would not taste of the supper were those Jewish leaders and their disciples who turned down the offer by rejecting Christ the invitation is still open to whosoever will today CONCLUSION He who hesitates is lost Mattoon illustrates the urgency of taking advantage of the opportunity - In 1973, Gary Kildall wrote the first popular operating system for personal computers, named CP/M. According to writer Phillip Fiorini, IBM approached Gary in 1980 about developing the operating system for IBM personal computers. But Kildall snubbed IBM officials at a crucial meeting, according to another author, Paul Carroll. The day IBM came calling, he chose to fly his new airplane. The frustrated IBM executives turned instead to a man named Bill Gates, founder of a small software company called Microsoft, and his operating system named MS-DOS. The rest is history. In 2010, Bill Gates was worth more than 53 billion dollars. Of Mr. Kildall, who has since died, author Paul Carroll says, "He was a smart guy who didn't realize how big the operating system market would become." He missed a huge opportunity. In a similar way, people often don't realize how big God's kingdom will become someday. God is calling with the offer of a lifetime Manson says, The two essential points in His teaching are that no man can enter the Kingdom without the invitation of God, and that no man can remain outside it but by his own deliberate choice. Man cannot save himself; but he can damn himself. And it is this latter fact that makes the preaching of Jesus so urgent. CUT ME SOME SLACK Luke 14:15-24 THE INVITATIONS 14:15, 16 Taking for granted his place in the eternal kingdom They saw themselves as a few notches above the rest The Pharisee was about to learn that admittance to the banquet is only of those who respond to the invitation. The custom was to send out two invitations THE EXCLUSION 14:17-20 The OT prophets had given the first invitation John the Baptist and Jesus began to give the second call And they all began with one implies a conspiracy in rejecting the invitation They had come to believe the invitation was not important All excuses are extremely weak What keeps folks from following Jesus? Possessions, People Good things keep people out of the kingdom of God THE INCLUSION 14:21-24 The master of the house being angry God does not get angry with sin; It is His nature to abhor sin The rejection of the Jews did not hinder God s plan compel this group would need extra convincing 2 Corinthians 5:20 They accepted the offer because the servant convinced them they were welcome CUT ME SOME SLACK Luke 14:15-24 THE INVITATIONS 14:15, 16 Taking for granted his place in the eternal kingdom They saw themselves as a few notches above the rest The Pharisee was about to learn that admittance to the banquet is only of those who respond to the invitation. The custom was to send out two invitations THE EXCLUSION 14:17-20 The OT prophets had given the first invitation John the Baptist and Jesus began to give the second call And they all began with one implies a conspiracy in rejecting the invitation They had come to believe the invitation was not important All excuses are extremely weak What keeps folks from following Jesus? Possessions, People Good things keep people out of the kingdom of God THE INCLUSION 14:21-24 The master of the house being angry God does not get angry with sin; It is His nature to abhor sin The rejection of the Jews did not hinder God s plan compel this group would need extra convincing 2 Corinthians 5:20 They accepted the offer because the servant convinced them they were welcome ============================================= === SERMON: message(131).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 11/1/2024 7:59:05 PM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message SO, YOU WANT TO BE A CHRISTIAN? Luke 14:25-35 INTRODUCTION Getting into something over your head Getting involved with something that becomes overwhelming; beyond the point of being able to deal with it To be involved in a situation you cannot get out of Above your abilities; more than one could handle; distressed; can t deal with it; in a tight corner; in desperate straits; Folks can get over the head in debt Have you ever had someone hold it over your head that you got in over your head? Listen to this story of Charlie Riggs. Many years ago, he came to Christ and was discipled by a young man named Lorne Sanny, who himself was being discipled by Dawson Trotman, founder of the Navigators. Charlie was willing to grow in Christ, but he was a bit rough around the edges and didn t seem very promising as a Christian leader. When Lorne wrote to Dawson, he told him that Charlie Riggs was the only man he was working with and he felt discouraged by the prospects. Trotman wrote back and said, Stay with your man. You never know what God will do with him. So, Lorne Sanny continued to work with Charlie Riggs. A few years passed and a young man named Billy Graham came on the scene. In 1952, the Navigators loaned Charlie Riggs to the Graham team to handle the follow-up in their early crusades. He planned to return to the Navigators eventually. However, he worked out so well that he stayed with Billy Graham. In 1957, on the eve of the famous New York City crusade at Madison Square Garden, the crusade director suddenly had to be replaced. Who could they get? The lay chairman suggested Charlie Riggs, but Billy Graham wasn t sure if he could handle the job. All he does is pray and quote Scripture. The layman insisted, Charlie Riggs got the job and the rest is history. The New York campaign became a model for the many crusades that would follow in later years. Billy Graham said, I didn t think he could do it. But I had this peace that Charlie so depended on the Holy Spirit that I knew the Lord could do it through Charlie. Charlie Riggs retired after many years of effective service to the Lord. What was his secret? How could a man with little formal training rise to such a high position and hold it for so long' He says, I always asked the Lord to put me in over my head . That way, when I had a job to do, either the Lord had to help me or I was sunk. God was delighted to answer this prayer time after time. He put Charlie Riggs in over his head and then bailed him out. Jesus is still headed to Jerusalem His popularity among the masses is seen in the great number of people who continue to follow There is a huge difference between following and being a disciple There is a big difference between being interested and being committed There is a difference between counting noses and counting hearts He has this crowd behind him and does an abrupt about face and speaks one of the most sobering lessons in the NT He again teaches the need to count the cost of following Him There is a repetition to the message: he cannot be my disciple 3x vs. 26, 27, 33 Jesus seems to be effectively discouraging followers This is not the first time He has taught the same lesson Matt 10:37, 38 J. C. Ryle: The conduct of our Lord on this occasion stands out in strong contrast to that of many ministers of the Gospel, in the present day. The temptation to admit people to full communion, and endorse and approve them as true Christians, before they have given evidence of decided grace, is very strong. The inclination to set before young enquirers the joys and comforts of the Gospel, without any proportionate exhibition of the cross and the fight, requires constant watching against. The close imitation of our Lord s conduct in this passage would probably greatly lessen the number of our communicants. But it may be doubted whether we should not gain in quality what we lost in quantity, and whether we should not be freed from many of those disgraceful backslidings, and gross inconsistencies, which so often now-a-days bring discredit on religion. It may be laid down as a general rule that communicants cheaply admitted are worth little, and that to call people Christians upon lower terms than those which our Lord sets forth, in the long run does more harm than good. The previous account in the chapter makes clear that salvation is all of grace It is for all who will accept the invitation It is not about meeting certain conditions However, There are people whose relationship to Jesus is one of attachment without commitment. (Gary Inrig) But Jesus was not a false recruiter. He wanted to weed out those who followed Him for superficial reasons, because when the battle heated up, He knew that they would fall away and cause damage for His cause. So He turned to the great multitude and laid out these demands of discipleship." - Steven Cole There is no false advertising here; no bait and switch Whoever was not willing to pay the price could not be His disciple cannot - no able; no possible; no power (dunamis) It is not that they would find it difficult; or that they would be second-rate disciples. They cannot be a disciple at all. If there is a problem, it is in the follower, not Jesus He welcomes all who come to Him; will in no wise cast out There is a price; there is no cheap grace; no discipleship-lite Christian martyr Dietrich Bonhoffer once affirmed; "When Christ calls a man, he bids him come and die.".. Jesus was the latest fad; it was cool to be a follower There were miracles; there was food; they loved the gifts; they loved healings They did not love the life He was calling them to William MacDonald: At times the Lord Jesus wooed men to Himself, but after they began to follow Him, He winnowed them. This appears to be a great contradiction How can something be free (salvation) yet cost so much (discipleship) ILL> I had a cousin who is a pilot and teaches flying lessons in Xenia He was a member of the Civil Air Patrol while in high school. He offered to give a short ride one day. The ride was free. But I was totally committed to his ability to fly. If we crashed, I die. I (and my folks) trusted him with my life. When we receive the free offer of salvation by grace, we are no longer our own. We have been bought with a price. We are not our own. There is a cost to follow Jesus. R C Sproul - The corrupting influences of pragmatism are seen even in the church. Seeker-sensitive worship can increase attendance without ever seeing the congregation grow to maturity. Churches targeting specific ages or lifestyles might attract a lot of people from these groups and not minister to those who do not fit certain classifications. Beware of any ministry that emphasizes what works and do what you can to help your church avoid slipping into pragmatism. Gallop says that fewer than 10% of evangelical Christians can be considered fully committed. There can be no doubt about the power of commitment. People loyal to a leader or a principle and willing to pay the price required will always overwhelm people who value comfort and convenience more than a cause. Our society generally does not breed strong commitment. The self-fulfillment ethic seduces us to believe that we have no higher loyalty than one to ourselves. The tragedy is that self-centered values eat away at commitment not only at commitment to leaders and causes, but at commitments to marriage, family, and friends. Believers are not immune to the world s value system. One missionary statesman has observed an irony of church life in the 1990s. While Third-World Christians are information-poor, they are commitment-rich. Sadly, Western Christians are information-rich, but they are commitment-poor. All generalizations are suspect, but that one seems to be uncomfortably true. Yet this condition collides with the plain teaching of Jesus. The demands of our master are incompatible with partial commitment or casual discipleship. The Lord s call to Himself requires intelligent, unconditional allegiance to His person and work. Jesus was always intensely realistic about what it meant to be His follower. He was never a huckster pushing a product while hiding the real costs in the fine print. Instead, He realistically spelled out His requirements, nowhere more clearly than in the passage of Luke 14. Luke takes the word disciple through a major transition in his gospel By definition it means learner By application it covered a wide spectrum of people: from the casual observer to a committed follower. It is a good word but rather broad in identifying folks When you get to the book of Acts, also written by Luke, the title is crystallized. A disciple is a committed follower of Jesus. It was disciples that selected the first deacons A disciple was the first church martyr The disciples were first called Christians in Antioch A Christian was a committed follower of Jesus. Jesus is laying the ground work in Luke 14 3x Jesus says, He cannot be my disciple ; the power is not there But notice the word my Many other teachers at the time had disciples These are disciples who truly belong to Jesus MY Throughout His earthly ministry, there were many following Jesus 12:1 innumerable multitude; 10k plus As the teaching got hard and miracles decreased, many turned away John 6:66 Luke 14 is an evangelistic message; Jesus is extending an invitation Jesus has repeatedly addressed the hypocrisy of those thinking they will gain admittance into the kingdom based on genetics: child of Abraham 14:15 So as Luke puts this together for Theophilus; after stating who will not be in the kingdom, he quotes Jesus as saying who will gain entrance As you talk with people today, you will receive a wide variety of answers You pray a prayer, you give a gift, you get baptized, you join a church You try harder, reform your behavior, turn over a new leaf MacArthur put it this way: Jesus is not calling for makeover but a takeover By Luke 14:25, many in Israel had already decided against Jesus They are tried to throw Him off a cliff, try to trap Him in His teaching, they have attributed His miracles to the power of Satan If you remember the parable of the 4 soils, some folks readily received the Word and had all the trimmings of a disciple for a time but ultimately walked away Some are like the kamikaze pilot who returned from his mission; they are involved but not committed. In chapter 13, we identified the Road to Hell is for the Lazy, the Late, and the Lofty The gate is narrow, the road is rough The Bible rejects easy-believe-ism, superficial Christianity There is no Reformation Theology in our passage. No where does it say that salvation is by Scripture Alone, Faith Alone, Grace Alone, Christ Alone, To the Glory of God Alone But you can have all the facts right and still not know Jesus You can know about His virgin birth, His ministry, the Cross, the Resurrection, the Ascension You can teach children, count money, cook food, mow grass, preach All that and still only be a tare among the wheat Are you attached to Jesus or committed to Jesus ? 12:51 think not that I came to bring peace to earth. I came to bring division It has been so easy to be identified as a Christ follower in our country. But we are only 1 election away from the cost quickly rising. Not the cost of goods and services related to monetary inflation but the cost of your commitment to Jesus as Lord That is a long introduction. But that is the lesson. The remaining verses are the illustration of the lesson Family, builder, king. The Cost of Discipleship Are we in over our head ? no doubt Corrie TenBoom, a survivor of German concentration camps says * When a train goes through a tunnel and it gets dark, you don't throw away the ticket and jump off. You sit still and trust the engineer. You can never learn that Christ is all you need, until Christ is all you have. Our salvation cost Jesus His life We are about to use the Communion Table as our reminder. SO, YOU WANT TO BE A CHRISTIAN? Luke 14:25-35 There is a huge difference between following and being a disciple Jesus seems to be effectively discouraging followers There are people whose relationship to Jesus is one of attachment without commitment. Whoever was not willing to pay the price could not be His disciple Dietrich Bonhoffer once affirmed; "When Christ calls a man, he bids him come and die." How can something be free (salvation) yet cost so much (discipleship) There is a cost to follow Jesus. Our society generally does not breed strong commitment. Western Christians are information-rich, but they are commitment-poor. Jesus was always intensely realistic about what it meant to be His follower. A Christian was a committed follower of Jesus. Luke 14 is an evangelistic message MacArthur: Jesus is not calling for makeover but a takeover The Bible rejects superficial Christianity You can never learn that Christ is all you need, until Christ is all you have. ============================================= === SERMON: message(132).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 11/9/2024 11:19:04 AM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message SO, YOU WANT TO BE A CHRISTIAN? Luke 14:25-35 Part 2 INTRODUCTION The original plan for preaching through this chapter has been thrown out. This section has slowed me down. It seems to demand more attention as it speaks clearly to who we are as believers. The call to follow Christ is a call to discipleship. The call to salvation includes the principles addressed here. We are called to examine ourselves Psalm 26:2 Examine me, O LORD, and prove me; try my reins and my heart 1 Corinthians 11:28 But let a man examine himself 2 Corinthians 13:5 Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith We sing the hymn based on Psalm 139:23, 24 Search me, O God, and know my heart: try me, and know my thoughts: And see if there be any wicked way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting What is your standard for such an examination? There has to be some sort of measuring tool to see if you are on the level The 2 Corinthians verse concludes with prove your own selves. Know ye not your own selves, how that Jesus Christ is in you, except ye be reprobates? There is a wrong standard for measuring: 2 Corinthians 10:12 For we are not bold to number or compare ourselves with some of those who commend themselves. But they themselves, measuring themselves by themselves, and comparing themselves with themselves, are without understanding There is a right standard: Our Standard 1 John 3:2 Beloved, now we are children of God, and it is not yet revealed what we will be. But we know that, when he is revealed, we will be like him; for we will see him just as he is. 2 Corinthians 3:18 But we all, with open face beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord, are changed into the same image from glory to glory, even as by the Spirit of the Lord. Can I say with the Psalmist: Psalm 73:25, 26 Who do I have in heaven? There is no one on earth who I desire besides you. My flesh and my heart fails, But God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever. Or say with Paul Philippians 3:8 Yes most assuredly, and I count all things to be loss for the excellency of the knowledge of Christ Jesus, my Lord, for whom I suffered the loss of all things, and count them nothing but refuse, that I may gain Christ Jesus directly confronted the large following by alerting them once again that it would cost them to be a committed follower. Family 14:26, 27 Why the family illustration? Family was just used as excuse in refusing the invitation married Matthew 10:37 key in understanding anyone no exception clause Hate often is used in the Bible to mean love less Hate is not absolute but relative Genesis 29:31 Jacob s love for Leah in comparison to Rachel The problem is one of priority more than feeling Don t think of love and hate as emotions, but an act of the will To love is to choose or submit to; To hate is to set aside or refuse to submit to For some their greatest foe is those of their own household Christ must come first If you are devoted to anyone more than Christ; Complete commitment You cannot be My disciple The gift of eternal life is free to anyone who believes Believing involves a transfer of ownership Romans 6:23 The gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord There is a new master, authority, boss You may come across someone who teaches you can be a Christian without being a disciple. They define discipleship as a higher level of commitment To them: first you have a convert and then you become a disciple A few decades ago there was a lot of commotion caused by two books; one from Charles Ryrie and the other by John MacArthur. Most of the attacks were directed toward MacArthur whose book was titled Lordship Salvation . Many believed he was adding to the message of salvation as a gift. What did we just read in Romans 6:23 Jesus as Lord Romans 10:9 if you confess with your mouth the Lord Jesus Romans 10:13 whoever calls upon the Lord shall be saved In the early church in the book of Acts, disciple occurs some 30 x Christian is used 2x. The early church considered genuine believers to be disciples There was two-tiered Christianity A disciple is first someone who comes to Him in faith and trust v. 26 A Disciple is someone who follows Jesus Discipleship is a call to fellowship with Jesus and to obedient service to Him and for Him. Christian: first used Acts 11:26; but most scholars believe it was used by the town people as a derogatory term 1 Peter 4:16 suffer as a Christian The early church used other terms: disciple, saints, brothers Please permit me to add another term to our list: Carnal Christian The idea is that one can come to Christ and yet live the rest of their life in a carnal, fleshly, manner. Can a Christian act carnally? YES 1 Cor. 3:1-3 Will a true Christian remain carnal? NO There is no such thing as a carnal Christian but there is such a thing as a Christian who can be fleshly The question is not whether a person who claims to be a Christian but lives carnally has lost his salvation, but whether that person was truly saved in the first place (1 John 2:19 ) Counting the cost means recognizing and agreeing to terms Following Jesus may mean I lost relationships, dreams, material things, or even my life There is a whole new set of priorities 2 Corinthians 4:16-18 People can easily distract you from serving Christ Parents can hinder a child from going into missions Almost everyone wants the kingdom of God but few seek it first Discipleship is fundamentally a call to allegiance - Bock It is not just the role that others have but note even his own life also The late theologian/philosopher, Francis Schaeffer, whose life and books have impacted thousands for Christ, was raised in a non-Christian home. After he became a Christian, his father did not want him to go to college and did not want him to become a minister, which young Fran felt called to be. When the moment finally came where he had to make the decision to go with what he thought God wanted or to submit to his father s wishes, Fran asked in a strained voice, Pop, give me a few minutes to go down in the cellar and pray. In fear and uncertainty, he went down there and wept hot tears of sorrow for his father. Then, in an act of desperate and simple faith, he did something that he would never advise anyone else to do, but what he felt was right for him at the time: he prayed, Oh, God, please show me. Then he took out a coin and said, Heads, I ll go in spite of dad s desires. It was heads. Still weeping, he cried out, God, be patient with me. If it s tails this time, I ll go. Tails. The third time he pleaded, Once, more, God. I don t want to make a mistake with Dad upstairs. Please now, let it be heads again. It was heads. So he went upstairs and told his dad that he had to go. His dad looked hard at him, then went out to slam the door. But just before the door hit the frame, his voice came through, I ll pay for the first half year. It was many years later that Fran s dad became a Christian, but Fran thinks that this moment was the basis of his salvation, when Fran in effect declared, I must follow the Lord. (Told by Edith Schaeffer, in The Tapestry [Word], pp. 60-62). How do we know whether we love Jesus more than our human relations? That s perhaps difficult to gauge. But ask yourself, If God asked me to, could I leave my parents and go to the mission field or could I accept having my child go? If God were to take my wife or child in death, would I get bitter and quit God? If my family were to threaten to disown me because of my faith in Christ, would I renounce my faith? Are you a disciple of Jesus Christ? It is no wonder the section finishes with v. 35 Jesus lays it out for us right up front: the best will follow the worst There is suffering and then a great banquet Satan shows the best and hides his worst After the fleeting pleasures there will be suffering in hell forever. Discipleship is about devotion to a cause, but to a Person David Wilkerson: If we had that red-hot, all-consuming, intense and joyous love for Christ, we would not need outlines, diagrams and instructions telling us how to pray; we would pray because our hearts would be on fire with love for Him. . . . Reading His Word would never be a burden; we wouldn't need formulas on how to finish the Bible in a year. SO, YOU WANT TO BE A CHRISTIAN? Luke 14:25-35 Psalm 26:2 1 Corinthians 11:28 2 Corinthians 13:5 Our Standard 1 John 3:2 2 Corinthians 3:18 Psalm 73:25, 26 Philippians 3:8 Family 14:26, 27 Hate is not absolute but relative Don t think of love and hate as emotions, but an act of the will Believing involves a transfer of ownership Romans 6:23 Discipleship is a call to fellowship with Jesus and to obedient service to Him and for Him. Carnal Christian: The idea is that one can come to Christ and yet live the rest of their life in a carnal, fleshly, manner. There is no such thing as a carnal Christian but there is such a thing as a Christian who can be fleshly Counting the cost means recognizing and agreeing to terms 2 Corinthians 4:16-18 Discipleship is fundamentally a call to allegiance - Bock Discipleship is about devotion to a cause, but to a Person ============================================= === SERMON: message(133).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 11/15/2024 7:20:52 PM To: "Hill Jonathan" Subject: message SO, YOU WANT TO BE A CHRISTIAN Luke 14:25-35 Part 3 INTRODUCTION Some of you will remember in our study of Luke s gospel when we worked our way through chapter 9. In that chapter we looked at some verses we labeled the most unpopular verses in the Bible. 9:23, 24 Rather than the elevation of self-love Jesus called for the crucifixion of self Jesus established death to self as the standard for discipleship Last week: v. 26 Putting behind family and self CROSS 14:27 Cf. Matt. 16:24-26; Mark 8:34-37 THE PRINCIPLE OF CROSS BEARING Let s get something out of the way early: The cross is not a symbol of our problems the cross you must bear is not your spouse, boss, roommate It is not some sickness of difficulty The cross is a cruel instrument of death and the symbol for the Christian faith (9:23) These words of Jesus may have come as a shock to those following Jesus They knew what the cross identified: the scourge of society was to die Why would Jesus want to mess up a good thing? He has a large following Remember: the disciples had a strong reaction when Jesus spoke of His cross Matthew 16:21-25 Jesus was about separating the superficial from the committed; the wheat from the tares. Jesus was about avoiding the plea Lord, Lord followed by I never knew you Those who Jesus told to depart A sin-oriented, hell-bent lifestyle as described in the false teaching of Carnal Christianity And from two weeks ago: Jesus was not interested in a makeover but a takeover. The audience Jesus was addressing knew full well what He was talking about The Cross was an instrument of torturous death So torturous that Romans would not do it to a Roman citizen To carry a cross was to declare death to self The heart of Jesus' teaching on discipleship is the issue of our heart -- are we willing to deny self (even to the point of death) "The believer's own cross is whatever it costs him in self-denial and opposition from others to follow the Lord Jesus." v. 26 including parents, children, siblings, and self When you take up a cross, you are separated from all you have The very garments of Jesus were taken THE EXPECTATION OF CROSS BEARING The cross brings shame and reproach Hebrews 12:2 Jesus endured the cross, despising the shame We sing it s shame and reproach gladly bear Hebrews 13:13 we are to be bearing His reproach The cross brings pain and torture Cruel and horrid suffering When Ignatius was going to be exposed to the fury of wild beasts for the name of Christ, he cries, "Now I begin to be a disciple." We need to take A W Tozer's words to heart when he said that The man on the cross is facing in only one direction. He is not going back, and he has no further plans of his own. Our cross is not some inconvenience or irritation that crossed our path Gregg Allen: Our culture considers that there is no greater right than your right to 'self'. Isn't that our unalienable right by law? - the right to life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness? Aren't we told in all the popular self-help literature that we can't really love other people until we first love our selves? Jesus is saying to forsake that right to self It is one thing to think of physical death and quite another to die daily James Denney wrote , The man who has nothing to die for has nothing to live for; he does not know what life is. Colossians 1:27 Christ is our life Here is a challenge that I am working on. When you awake of a morning this question: Ok, Lord, how might I serve you today? What do I need to die to today? THE LIFE OF CROSS BEARING This death represents a point in time: Galatians 2:20 Romans 6:4-6 This death is a process: Philippians 3:10 Discipleship is a series of deaths I am perpetually dying Consider it a part of life; each day Why did Jesus draw such a hard line when it came to cross-bearing? Joe Stowell: Maybe because He knew that living to please a Father in heaven would be a tough assignment in a world under the control of Satan. Tozer: The old cross slew men; the new cross entertains them. The old cross condemned; the new cross amuses. The old cross destroyed confidence in the flesh; the new cross encourages it. F. F. Bruce: Denying oneself is not a matter of giving up something, whether for Lent or for the whole of life: it is a decisive saying No to oneself, to one s hopes and plans and ambitions, to one s likes and dislikes, to one s nearest and dearest, for the sake of Christ. The Manner of Cross Bearing Patiently It may be oppressive outwardly but inwardly it is a quiet submission to the hand of God Whatever or whoever the instrument might be No murmuring, no despondency Cheerfully It is a voluntary act Simon of Cyrene was compelled to carry the cross of Jesus Fruitfully Hebrews 12:11 It is seeking holiness in the midst of terror Get spiritual gain the midst of loss To grow richer by losing all To know true love in the midst of a world s hatred To make sin more hateful and grace more active Practical Questions: Are you willing to follow Jesus if it means losing some of your closest friends? Are you willing to follow Jesus if it means alienation from your family? Are you willing to follow Jesus if it means the loss of your reputation? Are you willing to follow Jesus if it means losing your job? Are you willing to follow Jesus if it means losing your life? Life is not a cafeteria where we choose what we like In his book The Empty Church, historian Thomas C. Reeves says: Christianity in modern America . . . tends to be easy, upbeat, convenient, and compatible. It does not require self-sacrifice, discipline, humility, an otherworldly outlook, a zeal for souls, a fear as well as love of God. There is little guilt and no punishment, and the payoff in heaven is virtually certain. What we now have might best be labeled Consumer Christianity. The cost is low and customer satisfaction seems guaranteed. J. C. Ryle: It costs something to be a true Christian. Let that never be forgotten. To be a mere nominal Christian, and go to church, is cheap and easy work. But to hear Christ's voice, and follow Christ, and believe in Christ, and confess Christ, requires much self-denial. It will cost us our sins, and our self-righteousness, and our ease, and our worldliness. All all must be given up. We live in a world where the customer is always right Following Jesus is not always easy; but it is always right We hold the cross before the world It is the preaching of the cross that saves people It is surrendering to the cross that models Christ before the world "Take up thy cross and follow Me," I heard my Master say; "I gave My life to ransom thee Surrender your all today." SO, YOU WANT TO BE A CHRISTIAN Luke 14:25-35 Part 3 CROSS BEARING 14:27 The Principle of Cross Bearing The cross is not a symbol of our problems The cross is a cruel instrument of death To carry a cross was to declare death to self The Expectation of Cross Bearing The cross brings shame and reproach Hebrews 12:2 Hebrews 13:13 The cross brings pain and torture The Life of Cross Bearing This death represents a point in time: Galatians 2:20 Romans 6:4-6 This death is a process: Philippians 3:10 The Manner of Cross Bearing Patiently Cheerfully Fruitfully Hebrews 12:11 Life is not a cafeteria where we choose what we like Following Jesus is not always easy; but it is always right ============================================= === SERMON: message(134).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 11/29/2024 2:24:09 PM To: "Hill Jonathan" Subject: message SO, YOU WANT TO BE A CHRISTIAN Luke 14:25-35 Part 5 INTRODUCTION HATER, BEARER, BUILDER, FIGHTER Jesus said if you do not possess these qualities, you cannot be His disciple PRESERVER 14:34, 35 Our only reference to salt is what we see for cooking or on the table The salt of Jesus day was much different; not all salt is the same Most of the salt in Jesus day came from the marshes southwest of the Dead Sea It is only 12-18% sodium chloride; our table salt is 97% sodium chloride The point is that the impure salt derived from the Dead Sea was susceptible to deterioration and could lose its flavor, leaving useless crystals fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile. Useless salt was simply thrown away. Salt is only useful when it has the qualities of salt A Christian is only useful when he has the character of Christ Jesus told the disciples they were the salt of the earth Matt. 5:13 Salt was so valued in that day that soldiers were paid with salt [Latin for salt = sal which is the root of "salary ] God made a Covenant of Salt with David: 2 Chronicles 13:5 Speaking of its enduring character Salt was used in the sacrifices of the Levitical system: Lev. 2:12 Speaking to the eternal value of the sacrifice of Christ Bible Dictionary: It signifies the imperishableness of Jehovah's love for His people; as an antiseptic salt implies durability, fidelity, purity. The opposite of leaven, the symbol of corruption. Covenants were cemented by feasts and hospitality, the viands of which were seasoned, as all foods, with salt. Hence, "a covenant of salt for ever before the Lord" is an indissoluble covenant (Nu 18:19; 2Ch 13:5; Ezr 4:14, margin). Genuine discipleship will never be temporary; it will be lasting Jesus is looking for life-long loyalty to Him We will not do it perfectly but momentary failures do not invalidate the intentions of the heart Citizens of the Kingdom influence society because they are different When salt tries to accommodate to the world s system, it loses its distinctiveness The Christian life is to be lived in the world where it can bring seasoning to those who are dead in trespasses and sins. Jesus shows how the Christian is to use his influence to improve the quality of living for all men; just as salt draws out the best in food When Christians fail to influence others to live a more dedicated life, they fail in one of their greatest opportunities and responsibilities Ephesians 5:15 walk circumspectly exactly; 5x Matt. 2:8 search diligently Luke 1:3 having had perfect understanding No Christian should allow careless or worldly living to lesson his witness for the Lord There is nothing that can make up for the Christian s losing his influence on others There is nothing greater than salt to which the salt itself could be subjected for seasoning effect. Like the 3rd grade boy who was asked to describe salt. He stumbled through a couple of attempts and then said, Salt is what makes French Fries taste bad if you don t sprinkle it on There is nothing greater than Christianity to which the Christian can turn to have his withered witness renewed. Worthless salt has no use The Christian who rejects the lordship of Jesus puts himself in a position where he is good for nothing spiritually This is the miserable condition of backsliders and apostates The is an illustration of the consequences of failure to count the cost The professor who has walked away is the salt that has lost its savor There is no more dangerous position to be in than one who has once known the truth, professed to love it, and then falls away He has heard; he has known; he is not living in ignorance He has sinned against a known, not an unknown God Hebrews 6:4-6 Temporary disciples are useless to Jesus Those contaminated by worldliness are thrown out into eternal judgment To be cast out means to be given over to eternal loss in the final judgment This is the lot of those who are given over to a reprobate mind Salt has a wide range of uses: adds flavor, promotes healing, preserves from spoiling If it becomes flat, flavorless; it is absolutely useless This is the picture of those who love family more than Christ, who place their concerns over His, who cling to their things more than clinging to Him He that hath ears to hear, let him hear Only used 4x by Jesus To emphasize the importance of His listeners not only hearing what He said, but also understanding and responding to it It describes those with an inner spiritual reception to the truth Used in Mark and Luke as part of the Parable of the Soils We know that not everyone with physical ears can hear Hearing spiritual truth is a choice one must make Jesus is calling for a faith response to the invitation to discipleship Spiritual hearing is a reflection of his openness to God Don t be one of those who are guilty of sin of sermon listening Not in one ear and out the other The Word must be received into the heart There must be a total commitment to Him and obedience to His Word. The one who builds his house upon the rock: Matthew 7:24-27 The one who receives the seed with good soil; Luke 8:15 1 Thessalonians 2:13 James 1:21,22 Each use of the phrase is in the present tense meaning to keep on hearing and heeding to hear is used 7x in Revelation All in the aorist tense meaning to give urgent heed to the message 2:7, 11, 17, 29 3:6, 13, 32 We will not do this perfectly but is there evidence that we are honestly seeking to apply this truth to our hearts ILLUSTRATION - Here is a sad example of tasteless salt Gandhi says in his autobiography that in his student days he was truly interested in the Bible. Deeply touched by reading the Gospels, he seriously considered becoming a convert, since Christianity seemed to offer the real solution to the caste system that was dividing the people of India. One Sunday he went to a nearby church. He decided to see the minister and ask for instruction in the way of salvation and enlightenment on other doctrines. But when he entered the sanctuary, the ushers refused to give him a seat and suggested that he go worship with his own people. Gandhi left and never came back. He reasoned that "If Christians have caste differences also, I might as well remain a Hindu." The partiality showed by those Christians had a devastating effect on India and the world. As we have studied, they failed to manifest the sweet aroma and saltiness of the fifth beatitude, demonstrating mercy (Mt 5:7 -note ) Mahatma Gandhi was also quoted as answering a missionary's question "What is the greatest hindrance to Christianity in India?" with the trite reply "Christians"! ________________________________ ILLUSTRATION - Salty Salt. The story has often been told about Dr. Will H. Houghton, who pastored Calvary Baptist Church in New York City and later served as president of Chicago s Moody Bible Institute till his death in 1946. When Dr. Houghton became pastor of the Baptist Tabernacle in Atlanta, a man in that city hired a private detective to follow Dr. Houghton and report on his conduct. After a few weeks, the detective was able to report to the man that Dr. Houghton s life matched his preaching. As a result, that man became a Christian! Now that's salty salt! A college professor was asked once about one of his students. He replied, he might have attended my lectures, but he was not one of my students. Will God say, they might have attended my church, but they were not my children. When the pastor says to the young couple at the marriage ceremony, It is not by any to be entered into unadvisedly or lightly, but reverently, discreetly, advisedly, soberly, and in the fear of God, he is not trying to discourage them from getting married. He s just challenging them to face marriage realistically. And Jesus is just challenging us to face discipleship realistically. While salvation is free, discipleship is very costly. He s reminding us that the Christian life is not a 100 yard dash it s a marathon race. But it s worth it! SO, YOU WANT TO BE A CHRISTIAN Luke 14:25-35 Part 5 PRESERVER 14:34, 35 The salt of Jesus day was much different Salt is only useful when it has the qualities of salt A Christian is only useful when he has the character of Christ Genuine discipleship will never be temporaryCitizens of the Kingdom influence society There is nothing that can make up for the Christian s losing his influence on others There is nothing greater than Christianity to which the Christian can turn to have his withered witness renewed. The Christian who rejects the lordship of Jesus puts himself in a position where he is good for nothing spiritually Those contaminated by worldliness are thrown out into eternal judgment Hearing spiritual truth is a choice one must make Matthew 7:24-27 Luke 8:15 1 Thessalonians 2:13 James 1:21,22 ============================================= === SERMON: message(135).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 12/7/2024 9:57:51 AM To: "Hill Jonathan" Subject: message A CHRISTMAS DOXOLOGY 1 Timothy 6:13-16 INTRODUCTION I decided to take a break from our extended study in Luke. Most of the time, I plow right through a book even during the holiday seasons. Even this time, Luke 15 could easily be discussed in the context of the birth of Christ as He came to seek and save those who are lost. Chapter 15 speaks to the precious nature of salvation: The Lost Sheep, The Lost Coin, The Lost Son. It is also challenging to present something fresh for Christmas. You have heard and read a variety of aspects about the angels, stable, Joseph and Mary, and shepherds. You have been around long enough to know the wise men came to a house and did not arrive at the stable. You know about the hasty trip to Egypt, the slaughter of infants by Herod. You have heard about the fulness of time . In fact, we began a study this past Wednesday on the blank page found in most Bibles between the Old and New Testament. That page represents 400 years of what has been mistakenly called 400 Silent Years. Although there may have not been a prophet spokesman, God was not silent at all but used that time to prepare for the arrival of the Messiah. We can talk about Gabriel, Simeon, Anna. Years ago we looked at the songs of Christmas from Elizabeth and Mary. We properly translated Luke 2:14, Glory to God in the highest, And on earth peace among men in whom he is well pleased. We recognize there were more than three wise men from the east. I guess you get the picture by now. So, what to do for Christmas is a challenge, but I have decided on a path to take. I want us to talk about the birth of Jesus not as Savior but as King. Matthew 2:2 Where is he that is born King of the Jews? for we have seen his star in the east, and are come to worship him. He did not become a King, He was born a King. As part of His trial before crucifixion Pilate asked the question: On the day of the Triumphal Entry the crowd shouted: John 12:13 Blessed is the King of Israel that cometh in the name of the Lord. Quoting the OT: Fear not, daughter of Sion: behold, thy King cometh, sitting on an ass's colt. (Zechariah 9:9) Matthew 27:11 Art thou the King of the Jews? And Jesus said unto him, Thou sayest. Jesus said, you nailed it You speak truth Amen And it is this time of year when you hear the Hallelujah Chorus from Handel s Messiah King of Kings and Lord of Lords The Introduction to our Introduction was presented by Dr. S. M. Lockridge. His birth name is Shadrach Meshach Lockridge. From 1953 to 1993 he was the pastor of Calvary Baptist Church in San Diego, CA. He died in 2000 at the age of 87. The full sermon which was preached in Detroit in 1976 is over an hour long. What we heard is from the end of the message. Listen again: "The Bible says my King is a seven-way king.. He's the King of the Jews; that's a racial king.. He's the King of Israel; that's a national King.. He's the King of Righteousness. He's the King of the Ages.. He's the King of Heaven. He's the King of Glory. He's the King of Kings, and He's the Lord of Lords. So I went looking to see how many times the exact phrase King of Kings and Lord of Lords is in the Scriptures. Any guesses (except those from Wednesday): 3x All in the NT There are similar verses in the OT Deuteronomy 10:17 For the LORD your God is God of gods, and Lord of lords, a great God, a mighty, and a terrible, which regardeth not persons, nor taketh reward: Psalm 136:3 Oh give thanks unto the Lord of lords; For his lovingkindness endureth for ever: Daniel 2:47 The king (Nebuchadnezzar) answered unto Daniel, and said, Of a truth your God is the God of gods, and the Lord of kings, and a revealer of secrets, seeing thou hast been able to reveal this secret. Daniel 11:36 And the king shall do according to his will; and he shall exalt himself, and magnify himself above every god, and shall speak marvellous things against the God of gods; and he shall prosper till the indignation be accomplished; for that which is determined shall be done.
