They make their phylacteries broad, enlarge the fringes of their garments Titles of Dignity: respectful greetings Salutations: Rabbi , leader, exalted one Front Row Seats: seats of preeminence Seats that faced the people Place of honor: seated beside the host Devour the Defenseless: figurative language of depravity Should have been assisting instead of bilking them Pretense Prayers: pretending with the intention to deceive Praying just to be seen and heard Matthew 6:5 they have their reward Brian Bell: God is seeking servants, not celebrities The Awful Conclusion Condemnation: judicial sentence Greater because of their position Those who had greater light and made no proper use of it Those who professed religion but clung to their sins They hypocrite will have the lowest place in hell Luke 12:48 To whoever much is given, of him will much be required; and to whom much was entrusted, of him more will be asked. Scripture is clear: God hates pride But it is also very clear that God hates hypocrisy in worshippers We have Malachi 1 where God says don t bring me your blind goats We have Luke 20 where God says don t put on a show We are to worship in spirit and in truth THE SCRIBE SLAM Luke 20:45-47 The Attention of the Crowd They were in one ear and out the other listeners The Attack on the Scribes Matthew 23 is the long version; 8 Woes Hypocrisy: to wear a mask practice of deception Using religion to advance their personal ambitions Acts 20:28-31 Paul issues a similar warning to the church Hypocrisy is the one sin on which Jesus comes down the hardest The Attention on the Scribes Long flowing robe with special markings of identification Titles of Dignity: respectful greetings Front Row Seats: seats of preeminence Place of honor: seated beside the host Devour the Defenseless: figurative language of depravity Pretense Prayers: pretending with the intention to deceive The Awful Conclusion They hypocrite will have the lowest place in hell Luke 12:48 ============================================= === SERMON: message(181).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 1/23/2026 10:51:56 AM To: Subject: MESSAGE THE TEMPLE PICTURE WILL COME AT "CONTEXT CHANGE"; JESUS MOVING THROUGHT THE TEMPLE. WE CAN VIDEO ANYTIME TODAY OR TOMORROW EXAMPLE OR CONDEMNATION LUKE 21:1-4 INTRODUCTION Corporate worship is meant to remind us that the One who sits on the throne is the definition of power, glory, holiness, wisdom and love. Tripp The story of the Widow s Mite is a very familiar one It is often used as an example of giving and stewardship It seems no series of messages on giving is complete without including her BUT Some of you will think I jumped the rails, but please hear me out Here are a few questions which must be considered: Is she to be considered as the prime example of giving? Be like the widow woman! She gave everything to Jesus Is she a faithful woman or a subject of an oppressive religious system? Was she a true God worshipper or seeking to earn her salvation? Did she know Jesus or simply ignore His presence? Is God glorified if you give away all your possessions? Is the widow an example of giving for us to follow or a further example of an empty religious system? Did Jesus use her as an example for the disciples who will soon be on their own? There you go That s what I m talking about Is she an example of it s not how much you give but how much you keep for yourself? Is she an illustration of giving or an illustration for the coming judgment on the nation of Israel? CONTEXT Luke 16:14 the Pharisees are lovers of money; cannot serve two masters Luke 19:46 Jesus makes a point they have turned the Temple into a robber s den Luke 20:47, 48 Jesus condemns the hypocrisy of the religious leaders pronouncing judgment upon them. (Expanded text in Matthew 23) Sermon on the Mt. when you give alms don t sound a trumpet Ka-ching; Ka-ching; Ka-ching He specifically points out the robbing of widows; devour = eat down Eat out of house and home; used by Matthew and Mark, same account Luke 15:30 prodigal son; ate up his inheritance Rev. 20:9 fire devoured those attacking Jerusalem Luke 21:5-36 Jesus continues the words of judgment upon the nation So, how do verses 1-4 fit into the context Are we reading into the text or drawing out of the text? Here are some popular thoughts from respected sources John Calvin: claims this account provides a highly useful doctrine, that whatever men offer to God ought to be estimated not by its apparent value, but only by the feeling of the heart, and that the holy affection of him who, according to his small means, offers to God the little that he has, is more worthy of esteem than that of him who offers a hundred times more out of his abundance. John Stott - Her total devotion, which no-one but Jesus would have realized, is the exact opposite of the Jewish leaders religion, all show and no heart. Life Application Commentary - She gave everything and trusted God to care for her. Jesus wanted the disciples to see this lesson in total surrender of self, commitment to God, and willingness to trust in God s provision. Warren Wiersbe - When it comes to our giving, God sees more than the portion; He also sees the proportion. Men see what is given, but God sees what is left, and by that He measures the gift and the condition of our hearts. Winston Churchill said, We make a living by what we get, but we make a life by what we give. I argue not with the principles shared by those individuals My question is this: is that the lesson from this widow? Now, one must be careful to argue against years of interpretation But an exhaustive study of the text does reveal some interesting interpretations that run contrary to how these verses are typically taught One writer began by saying, Let me say at the outset that Luke 21:1-4 is a VERY DIFFICULT passage for me to interpret. We always caution about eisegesis rather than exegesis Reading into the text rather than drawing out of the text This widow has been used in countless series of messages on stewardship I am guessing somewhere in my files, I did the same thing It is easy to do and would make for a great sermon Appeal to the emotions, BE LIKE THE WIDOW But the question remains: how does this account fit into the CONTEXT of the passage I am asking you to lay aside any preconceived impressions of the passage Many comments about the passage sound good and are definitely supported by other passages of Scripture But I am convinced that many of the commentators seem to draw conclusions that are not based on a literal, normative reading of the text Context Continuity The text reads smoothly from 11:47 through 21:5 Exposing hypocrisy and coming judgment Condemning the men who ran a corrupt religious system to the complete destruction of the religious facility 21:5 begins with and , clearly linking the condemnation of the Temple with His description of the widow in the Temple Court Context Change (Movement) (Temple Picture) Jesus is moving through the Temple complex 19:45, 46 Court of the Gentiles, size of 10 football fields; cleaning house 21:1-4 Now in the Court of the Women, location of the money receptors 21:5-36 Innermost Court, Holy Temple; judgment decreed CONTEXT IS KING RELIGIOUS SYSTEM The Jewish system has long lost the concept of Abraham s justification by faith; Romans 4 It has become a system of rules and traditions to earn one s salvation Easy for a widow to get caught up in a false system; everybody else has The nation has fallen into step through the arm twisting of its leaders Multitudes participate even though completely enamored by Jesus He taught as one having authority; the people were attentive (19:48); marveled at the gracious words (4:22) Matthew 23:13 "What sorrow awaits you teachers of religious law and you Pharisees. Hypocrites! For you shut the door of the Kingdom of Heaven in people's faces. You won't go in yourselves, and you don't let others enter either." The Pharisees led the nation in rejecting Jesus; accusations, etc Easy for this woman to feel compelled to give The NT provides plenty of teaching on how to give: 2 Cor. 8:5 God wants us to first give ourselves to Jesus 2 Cor. 8:3 God desires generous hearts, joyful giving 2 Cor. 9:7 give as you purposed; not grudgingly or under compulsion; God loves a cheerful giver Jesus had just condemned hypocritical behavior devour widow s houses make long prayers Is Jesus not lamenting over a system that would lead her to give away her security? (Do not televangelists do that today?) Where does Scripture require us to give up all we have? One may give sacrificially; it does not take much for some people to give until it hurts But it also says that he who does not take care of his own is worse than an infidel (1 Tim. 5:8) TRANSITION: CONTEXT, RELIGIOUS SYSTEM TEXTUAL QUESTIONS The only thing we know from the text is the comment from Jesus Jesus only said she gave everything. There is no other commentary from Jesus There are some questions that must be asked Where does the text describe the widow s spirit? Where does the text say anything about her motive for giving? Did she give of her free-will or was she shamed into giving It could be self-denial or foolishness There is no evidence of a commendation from Jesus Jesus does not tell the disciples, go and do likewise It is true she gave more than the rich guys No where does it say she was seeking the kingdom of God No where does it say she was doing it out of love for God She was living in a works-based salvation The same religious leaders who would reduce widows to poverty also encourage them to make donations beyond their means. Ka-ching vs. clink, clink CONCLUSION The Pharisees are still among us; enticing the vulnerable, the lonely Still devouring the bank accounts of widows They are being scammed out of their money. It may come from a phone call, snail mail, or televangelist It is wrong to go beyond the principles of giving plainly provided for us in the Bible There is a difference between emotional appeals and the moving of emotions I believe Jesus is using the contribution of her two cents as further proof of a corrupt religious system which is soon to be judged No where does He condemn the woman; only the system Pure and undefiled religion is this: take care of widows and orphans EXAMPLE OR CONDEMNATION LUKE 21:1-4 Is she a faithful woman or a subject of an oppressive religious system? CONTEXT Luke 16:14 Luke 19:46 Luke 20:47, 48 devour = eat down Luke 15:30 Revelation 20:9 We always caution about eisegesis rather than exegesis Context Continuity 21:5 begins with and , clearly linking the condemnation of the Temple with His description of the widow in the Temple Court Context Change Jesus is moving through the Temple complex RELIGIOUS SYSTEM The Jewish system has become a system of rules and traditions to earn one s salvation Matthew 23:13 Where does Scripture require us to give up all we have? TEXTUAL QUESTIONS There is no other commentary from Jesus Did she give of her free-will or was she shamed into giving? There is no evidence of a commendation from Jesus No where does He condemn the woman; only the system EXAMPLE OR CONDEMNATION LUKE 21:1-4 Is she a _____________ woman or a subject of an ______________ religious system? CONTEXT Luke ______ Luke ______ Luke 20:_________ devour = eat down Luke _______ Revelation _______ We always caution about _____egesis rather than _____egesis Context Continuity 21:5 begins with and , clearly linking the _________________ of the Temple with His description of the widow in the Temple Court Context Change Jesus is _____________ through the Temple complex RELIGIOUS SYSTEM The Jewish system has become a system of _________ and _____________ to earn one s salvation Matthew ________ Where does Scripture require us to give up _______ we have? TEXTUAL QUESTIONS There is _______ other commentary from Jesus Did she give of her ___________ or was she shamed into giving? There is ______ evidence of a commendation from Jesus _______ where does He condemn the woman; only the system ============================================= === SERMON: message(182).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 1/23/2026 10:49:26 AM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message THE TEMPLE PICTURE WILL FIT IN AT "CONTEXT CHANGE". JESUS MOVING THROUGH THE TEMPLE WE CAN VIDEO TODAY OR TOMORROW. NOT SURE YET WHEN THE SNOW IS TO BEGIN EXAMPLE OR CONDEMNATION LUKE 21:1-4 INTRODUCTION Corporate worship is meant to remind us that the One who sits on the throne is the definition of power, glory, holiness, wisdom and love. Tripp The story of the Widow s Mite is a very familiar one It is often used as an example of giving and stewardship It seems no series of messages on giving is complete without including her BUT Some of you will think I jumped the rails, but please hear me out Here are a few questions which must be considered: Is she to be considered as the prime example of giving? Be like the widow woman! She gave everything to Jesus Is she a faithful woman or a subject of an oppressive religious system? Was she a true God worshipper or seeking to earn her salvation? Did she know Jesus or simply ignore His presence? Is God glorified if you give away all your possessions? Is the widow an example of giving for us to follow or a further example of an empty religious system? Did Jesus use her as an example for the disciples who will soon be on their own? There you go That s what I m talking about Is she an example of it s not how much you give but how much you keep for yourself? Is she an illustration of giving or an illustration for the coming judgment on the nation of Israel? CONTEXT Luke 16:14 the Pharisees are lovers of money; cannot serve two masters Luke 19:46 Jesus makes a point they have turned the Temple into a robber s den Luke 20:47, 48 Jesus condemns the hypocrisy of the religious leaders pronouncing judgment upon them. (Expanded text in Matthew 23) Sermon on the Mt. when you give alms don t sound a trumpet Ka-ching; Ka-ching; Ka-ching He specifically points out the robbing of widows; devour = eat down Eat out of house and home; used by Matthew and Mark, same account Luke 15:30 prodigal son; ate up his inheritance Rev. 20:9 fire devoured those attacking Jerusalem Luke 21:5-36 Jesus continues the words of judgment upon the nation So, how do verses 1-4 fit into the context Are we reading into the text or drawing out of the text? Here are some popular thoughts from respected sources John Calvin: claims this account provides a highly useful doctrine, that whatever men offer to God ought to be estimated not by its apparent value, but only by the feeling of the heart, and that the holy affection of him who, according to his small means, offers to God the little that he has, is more worthy of esteem than that of him who offers a hundred times more out of his abundance. John Stott - Her total devotion, which no-one but Jesus would have realized, is the exact opposite of the Jewish leaders religion, all show and no heart. Life Application Commentary - She gave everything and trusted God to care for her. Jesus wanted the disciples to see this lesson in total surrender of self, commitment to God, and willingness to trust in God s provision. Warren Wiersbe - When it comes to our giving, God sees more than the portion; He also sees the proportion. Men see what is given, but God sees what is left, and by that He measures the gift and the condition of our hearts. Winston Churchill said, We make a living by what we get, but we make a life by what we give. I argue not with the principles shared by those individuals My question is this: is that the lesson from this widow? Now, one must be careful to argue against years of interpretation But an exhaustive study of the text does reveal some interesting interpretations that run contrary to how these verses are typically taught One writer began by saying, Let me say at the outset that Luke 21:1-4 is a VERY DIFFICULT passage for me to interpret. We always caution about eisegesis rather than exegesis Reading into the text rather than drawing out of the text This widow has been used in countless series of messages on stewardship I am guessing somewhere in my files, I did the same thing It is easy to do and would make for a great sermon Appeal to the emotions, BE LIKE THE WIDOW But the question remains: how does this account fit into the CONTEXT of the passage I am asking you to lay aside any preconceived impressions of the passage Many comments about the passage sound good and are definitely supported by other passages of Scripture But I am convinced that many of the commentators seem to draw conclusions that are not based on a literal, normative reading of the text Context Continuity The text reads smoothly from 11:47 through 21:5 Exposing hypocrisy and coming judgment Condemning the men who ran a corrupt religious system to the complete destruction of the religious facility 21:5 begins with and , clearly linking the condemnation of the Temple with His description of the widow in the Temple Court Context Change (Movement) (Temple Picture) Jesus is moving through the Temple complex 19:45, 46 Court of the Gentiles, size of 10 football fields; cleaning house 21:1-4 Now in the Court of the Women, location of the money receptors 21:5-36 Innermost Court, Holy Temple; judgment decreed CONTEXT IS KING RELIGIOUS SYSTEM The Jewish system has long lost the concept of Abraham s justification by faith; Romans 4 It has become a system of rules and traditions to earn one s salvation Easy for a widow to get caught up in a false system; everybody else has The nation has fallen into step through the arm twisting of its leaders Multitudes participate even though completely enamored by Jesus He taught as one having authority; the people were attentive (19:48); marveled at the gracious words (4:22) Matthew 23:13 "What sorrow awaits you teachers of religious law and you Pharisees. Hypocrites! For you shut the door of the Kingdom of Heaven in people's faces. You won't go in yourselves, and you don't let others enter either." The Pharisees led the nation in rejecting Jesus; accusations, etc Easy for this woman to feel compelled to give The NT provides plenty of teaching on how to give: 2 Cor. 8:5 God wants us to first give ourselves to Jesus 2 Cor. 8:3 God desires generous hearts, joyful giving 2 Cor. 9:7 give as you purposed; not grudgingly or under compulsion; God loves a cheerful giver Jesus had just condemned hypocritical behavior devour widow s houses make long prayers Is Jesus not lamenting over a system that would lead her to give away her security? (Do not televangelists do that today?) Where does Scripture require us to give up all we have? One may give sacrificially; it does not take much for some people to give until it hurts But it also says that he who does not take care of his own is worse than an infidel (1 Tim. 5:8) TRANSITION: CONTEXT, RELIGIOUS SYSTEM TEXTUAL QUESTIONS The only thing we know from the text is the comment from Jesus Jesus only said she gave everything. There is no other commentary from Jesus There are some questions that must be asked Where does the text describe the widow s spirit? Where does the text say anything about her motive for giving? Did she give of her free-will or was she shamed into giving It could be self-denial or foolishness There is no evidence of a commendation from Jesus Jesus does not tell the disciples, go and do likewise It is true she gave more than the rich guys No where does it say she was seeking the kingdom of God No where does it say she was doing it out of love for God She was living in a works-based salvation The same religious leaders who would reduce widows to poverty also encourage them to make donations beyond their means. Ka-ching vs. clink, clink CONCLUSION The Pharisees are still among us; enticing the vulnerable, the lonely Still devouring the bank accounts of widows They are being scammed out of their money. It may come from a phone call, snail mail, or televangelist It is wrong to go beyond the principles of giving plainly provided for us in the Bible There is a difference between emotional appeals and the moving of emotions I believe Jesus is using the contribution of her two cents as further proof of a corrupt religious system which is soon to be judged No where does He condemn the woman; only the system Pure and undefiled religion is this: take care of widows and orphans EXAMPLE OR CONDEMNATION LUKE 21:1-4 Is she a faithful woman or a subject of an oppressive religious system? The wise man is content to get his head inside the heavens. Luke 19:11 know your audience William Hendricksen: "Jesus now proceeds to correct their mistaken inference. He shows them that not everything that seems to be a sign of the end of the world is in reality such a sign. Some signs should be seen as general and not specific Many moons ago, in 1972 the 7 member European Union was planning on adding 3 more members to give it a total of 10. Right away, there were many messages from the book of Daniel of a 10 nation confederacy; (7:24) It was looked upon as a sign of the times It is frightfully easy to misinterpret and be misled Don t get caught up in a particular event I still love the comment from Earl Radmacher: this is not a sign of the times because it is not the time for signs What I do hope to do is use the same process as our study in Revelation Take the plain reading of the text as presented, read it in the order it is presented, and as long as common sense makes perfect sense consider any other sense as nonsense. Luke 21:7 the disciples asked two questions: when and what Jesus ignored the when but gave an overview of the what They had long believed that Jesus was the Messiah and that John the Baptist was His prophesied forerunner. The acclaim of the crowds at Jesus triumphal entry, at His cleansing the Temple, at His rebuking the religious leaders, as well as at His predicting the destruction of the Temple all combined to make them think He would soon manifest His messianic glory, subdue the nations that would rise up against Him, and establish His eternal kingdom. They saw all these things happening at the same general time in history Remember the OT writers only saw one coming as they wrote e.g. Isaiah 61:1-11 A Survey of Various Scenarios 1. A description of the general events of the Church Age from Jesus' day leading up to the last seven year period (Tribulation Period) (Walvoord, MacArthur, Stedman) 2. A description of general events of the Church Age and describing the 7 years of Tribulation (Ironside, L. S. Chafer, J. Vernon McGee) 3. A description of events in the first half of Daniel's seventieth week (D. Pentecost) 4. All of the events occurring in the seven year tribulation. (Wiersbe, Wycliffe Bible Commentary) 5. All of the events as fulfilled in the past the end is the destruction of Jerusalem, not the end of the age (D. A. Carson, David Platt, Albert Barnes These are the birth pains; Matt. 24:8; Mark 13:8 the beginning of sorrows Pang = pain associated with child birth going on for over 2,000 years Matthew and Mark both have Jesus leaving the temple when this conversation takes place on Mt. Olivet THE BIG POINT OF THE PLAN 21:8 Don t be Fooled Keep the main thing the main thing 21:8 take heed that you are not led astray (Matthew 24:4) ??p? bl po: behold, beward, look present imperative: a continuous command It is apparent the listeners to Jesus were already confused They are expecting things to happen soon They are soon to learn there will be a long, indeterminate period before the end of the age Jesus is tracing the age; not giving the signs of the times Perhaps this is a good time to look at the conclusion in 21:34-36 take heed watch p??s??? pros cho: to hold the mind, be cautious Evaluate. Test. Matthew 24:42 watch ??????e?? gregore o: to keep awake That is all Jesus is after A word about Eschatology: The Doctrine of Last Things The concern is ethics, behavior, how we are to live right now It is always practical, how we are to live and serve Even if it is to persevere unto death THE UNTHINKABLE OF THE PLAN 21:5, 6 William Lane: "This complex of stone was one of the most impressive sights in the ancient world, and was regarded as an architectural wonder. As a mountain of white marble decorated with gold it dominated the Kidron gorge as an object of dazzling beauty." The temple mount was 36 acres Josephus: "built of stones that were white and strong: and each of their length was twenty-five cubits; their height was eight, and their breadth about twelve." 450 in x 144 x 215 37.5 ft x 12 x 18 In a word stunning Built over 80 years and only completed 8 years before its destruction A solemn declaration: what you are staring at will be torn apart stone by stone Imminent destruction; history, 70 A.D. by Titus Mark 13:2 double negative, the strongest way to state a negative None left standing and none that are not torn down Not the first warning to the nation: 13:34, 35; 19:43, 44; 20:16; 20:47 This warning only to the disciples; Jesus is done with the nation It is easy to get caught up in the beauty of something and forget the evil within Crystal Cathedral; Morman Tabernacle THE OVERVIEW OF THE PLAN 21:8-11 Matthew 24:8 and Mark 13:8 these are the birth pangs (Interesting that Dr. Luke did not) Simply saying things will intensify as the world gets closer to the delivery date: the end of the age, the arrival of Messiah, the birth of His Kingdom The pangs simply tell us a birth is coming but not when There is a connection but it does not mean the end is near Pangs indicate it will be painful for all humanity; Revelation 6-19 The birth will bring about something precious, beautiful, and highly valued I love this from John Philiips: "Matthew bracketed the Lord's public ministry between two sermons: the sermon on the mount, in which the emphasis was practical; and the Olivet discourse, in which the emphasis was prophetical. In one sermon we read of the rules of the kingdom; in the other we read of the return of the King. Before giving the sermon on the mount, the Lord was baptized; after giving the Olivet discourse, the Lord was buried. Prior to the first sermon He proved that the tempter could not conquer Him; after the last sermon He proved that the tomb could not conquer Him." On January 27, 2026, the Doomsday Clock was set at 85 seconds to midnight , the closest the Clock has ever been to midnight in its history. It moved 3 seconds closer to the world s destruction: the estimated likelihood of a human-made global catastrophe RELIGIOUS SIGNS 21:8 False Messiahs: not just a few, but many The first one to claim the title was in 132 A.D. Most recent in my lifetime: Sun Myung Moon: Unification Church Jim Jones; claimed to be the reincarnation of Jesus, Buddha, Vladimir Lenin, and Father Divine prior to leading a mass suicide of his followers Marshall Applewhite: Heaven s Gate, rendezvous with a space ship hiding behind the comet Hale-Bopp. David Koresh: Branch Davidian religious sect in Waco, Texas. The list is long: Joseph Smith, Latter Day Saints; millions of followers William Miller: Jesus return, 1843; 7th Day Adventist All claiming to possess and be using Christ s authority in My name Or actually claim to be Christ What is fascinating is that people will be more attracted to a false Christ than the One who came saying, before Abraham was, I Am John 8:59 They are spiritual shysters False Christ: Rev. 13:5 the antichrist, the greatest deceiver given authority for 42 months He will begin with a peace covenant; will be welcomed as a great benefactor until unleashing his fury on the very same people who welcomed him 2 Thessalonians 2:3, 4 the time is near what time? The end (v.9 telos) Not only claim to be the Messiah but claim to know the time is at hand Some believe it refers to the Temple (70 A.D.) but I believe it refers to the end of the age Telos is used 3x in Matthew 24: 6, 13, 14; all context end of the age Have you ever succumbed to a smooth-talking salesman? Skilled in the art of persuasion and manipulation; charm, flattery Most smooth talkers are Smooth Stalkers NATIONAL SIGNS 21:9, 10 Warfare and disorders; confusion, instability, revolutions, tumults Social and political chaos I can easily see the political turmoil we are going through to continue until the dark side wins do not be frightened how can that be? By believing in Jesus, we choose not to be terrified By controlled by the Holy Spirit We hold to the truth knowing it will also hold us when all around us is falling apart Isaiah 26:3; Psalm 46:1, 10 Wars: Rev. 16:12-14 The end is not yet telos Jesus is trying to instill faith and assurance in His disciples to hold to when the world around them is chaotic and seemingly out of control NATURAL SIGNS 21:11 A Whole Lot of Shaking Going On (Jerry Lee Lewis) Earthquakes, plagues and famines Things that have been around for ages; birth pangs will only bring increase Famine: Rev. 6:8 kill with hunger Plagues: Rev. 9:18 1/3 of mankind; fire, smoke, brimstone Earthquakes: Rev. 16:18 a mighty and great earthquake such as not occurred since men were on the earth Signs from heaven: weather can be a blessing or a curse Revelation 6:12-17 Revelation 8:3-13 The tides are thrown off, day and night is thrown off; crops are thrown off Luke 12:24 There will be no guessing when Jesus returns THE PLAN OF THE AGES Luke 21:5-28 THE BIG POINT OF THE PLAN 21:8 Don t be Fooled Jesus is tracing the age; not giving the signs of the times Eschatology: how we are to live right now THE UNTHINKABLE OF THE PLAN 21:5, 6 Imminent destruction; 70 A.D. by Titus THE OVERVIEW OF THE PLAN 21:8-11 The pangs simply tell us a birth is coming but not when The birth will bring about something precious RELIGIOUS SIGNS 21:8 All claiming to possess and be using Christ s authority or actually claim to be Christ Rev. 13:5 the antichrist, the greatest deceiver 2 Thessalonians 2:3, 4 NATIONAL SIGNS 21:9, 10 Isaiah 26:3; Psalm 46:1, 10 NATURAL SIGNS 21:11 Famine: Rev. 6:8 Plagues: Rev. 9:18 Earthquakes: Rev. 16:18 Heaven: Revelation 6:12-17 Revelation 8:3-13 ============================================= === SERMON: message(184).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 2/7/2026 10:00:11 AM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message I WILL USE THIS QUOTE AT THE BEGINNING WORSHIP IS NO LONGER WORSHIP WHEN IT REFLECTS THE CULTURE AROUND US MORE THAN THE CHRIST WITHIN US * A. W. TOZER THE PLAN OF THE AGES Luke 21:5-28 INTRODUCTION In the midst of all we are talking about in Luke 21, I think it needful to be reminded this is the last week of Jesus life on earth It could be Tuesday or Wednesday depending on who is doing the interpreting Jesus has concluded His comments to the nation and now is focusing entirely on teaching the disciples and preparing them for what lies ahead THE BIG POINT OF THE PLAN 21:8 THE UNTHINKABLE OF THE PLAN 21:5, 6 THE OVERVIEW OF THE PLAN RELIGIOUS SIGNS 21:8 NATIONAL SIGNS 21:9, 10 NATURAL SIGNS 21:11 PERSECUTION SIGNS 21:12-19 I can only present what the passage teaches I have absolutely no personal experience with anything that comes close to what Jesus describes here. I have had my little feelings hurt, but that is nothing compared to being hauled into court I have had unkind things said and people with personal agendas depart but no one has laid hands on me My siblings have made fun of me, in fact some of you have done that, but it does not reach the level of suffering for Jesus Jesus is now addressing something that will arise before the events in verses 5-11. But before in whatever period of time the events of the previous verses address, the disciples could expect persecution In the short term, this is what life holds for you So, forget about the end of the age for a minute, focus on this Do you think Jesus had their undivided attention by now? However awesome the preceding events will be, and they will be incredible, there is nothing like words of suffering that will grab someone s mind Now, I also admit that receiving difficult news can cause one to shut their mind off to whatever else is said I have been asked on occasion to sit in on a meeting where there was the anticipation of some difficult news They knew it was possible for them to become so fixated on something difficult to hear that they would miss the full discussion It can happen in the legal arena or the world of medicine In the interest of full disclosure, there are records of event described in verses 5-11 taking place before the temples destruction in 70 A.D. Wars: Rome had no problem getting involved in conflicts Earthquakes: 62 A.D. near Pompeii; seen as a precursor to the eruption of Mt. Vesuvius in 79 A.D. which buried the city Famines: Acts 11:28 Fearful sights: Signs in the heavens: I read of a comet that looked like a sword hung over the city by night for a year. (Could find no verification) All those events are described as birth pangs They will increase and grow incredibly more severe as we approach the end of the age Seal, Trumpet, and Bowel Judgments; Rev. 6-10 DEROGOTORY ACCUSATIONS What Jesus is describing is recorded in the book of Acts Who are they ? Context: Jewish religious leaders persecuting Jews Jesus has already told them, if they do it to Me they will do it to you John 15:20 Acts 4:3-7 ; Acts 5:17-19 ,40 ; Acts 6:12-15 ; Acts 7:57-60 ; Acts 8:3 ; Acts 21:30 ,31 ; Acts 22:30 ; Acts 24:1-9 ; Acts 25:1 ,2 ,11 ,12 ,22-25 ; Acts 26:2-11 Luke is also the writer of Acts lay their hands on Acts 4:3; 5:18; 12:1; 21:27 Persecute to press hard after; to chase, to pursue Dioko speaks of an intensity of effort leading to a pursue with earnestness and diligence in order to obtain. - This promise still holds true for all followers of Jesus The key is at the end of v. 12 for His name s sake 11:49 has a similar prediction 3x in the Sermon on the Mt. Matt. 5:10-12 Romans 12:14 bless and curse not That is one of the most unnatural responses asked of men How can we do that? Supernatural power of the Holy Spirit Acts 7:60 Stephen s example How could he say that? 7:55 Delivered - NLT: dragged into synagogues and prisons Synagogues were the courts of the day It was considered to be a humiliating and degrading experience Christians were flogged Acts 5:40; 2 Cor. 11:24 Imprisoned Acts 5:18; 8:3 Mark 13:9 adds don t be caught off guard Matthew 24:9 adds Then they will deliver you to tribulation (thlipsis - same word used of "Great Tribulation " in Mt 24:21 , but not that specific time), and will kill you, and you will be hated by all nations because of My name Speaks to the intensity of the persecution Also brought before kings and governors So Not just Jewish courts but also Gentile Acts 12:1; 16:19-26; 25:12 Persecution because they hate the name of Jesus My name s sake Did you see some are all upset with Speaker Johnson because he dared to put out a statement about immigration from a Biblical framework. One article: House Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) was mockingly accused by critics on social media of trying to out-Bible the Pope after he attempted to use scripture to defend the Trump administration s hardline immigration agenda. I wanted to respond it does not take much to out-Bible the Pope 2 Corinthians 1:14-16 DIVINE ASSISTANCE 21:13-15 an opportunity for testimony How many people have martyr on their bucket list? Here is your privilege to proclaim the name of Jesus Paul s appeal to Caesar provided the opportunity to present Christ where he had never been before Chained to a Roman guard and you think Paul never mentioned Jesus Philippians 4:22 Acts 4:1-13; summary 4:33 Consider the disciples: persecution never entered the mind of one looking for a kingdom Testimony: witness; English word martyr "A word originally referred to one who was a legal witness but came to refer to one whose testimony for Jesus ends in death." MacArthur: The persecution of the church always brings gospel opportunity. Persecution of the church always purifies the church. The persecution of the church always makes the church strong, it makes the church bold. Put aside fear of what you will say 21:14 God s grace will provide in the hour of testing defend answer; apologia = apologetics; a speech given in defense The promise of verbal inspiration 21:15 No need to memorize a speech I myself will take care of you You are there because of Jesus name and Jesus will take it from there Evidenced throughout the book of Acts On Christ the Solid Rock I stand 2 Timothy 4:16, 17 Lenski: this means only that the testimony of the disciples which will be placed on their tongue by Jesus will be so strong and not that the disciples will in every case be acquitted and set free Exodus 4:11-15 Acts 6:9, 10 Those who suffer for Christ will be defended by Christ DIVIDED ASSOCIATIONS 21:16, 17 Heartbreak Betrayal Reflect back 12:51-53 Some will die; some; not the norm Hated by all To be detested is the general reaction of the ungodly to a Bible believer Some Christian testimonies are so weak they go unnoticed friendship with Jesus means the enmity of the world (Lenski) DESIRED ARRIVAL 21:18. 19 Put yourself in the sandals of a disciple This is new material. You are thinking glorious Kingdom; the King is talking some will be killed Now you hear a comment that makes littles sense not a hair will be destroyed The ESV Study Bible adds that "This was not a promise for the preservation of their physical lives, but a guarantee that they would suffer no eternal loss. God Himself sovereignly preserves His own." For me to live is Christ, to die is gain Phil. 1:21 though the outward man will perish, the inward man is being renewed day by day A Christian may die but will not suffer irreparable loss Throughout times of betrayal, God would be faithful; He would be their refuge and strength. When testimony was needed, the Holy Spirit would suggest the words to be uttered, and if they were called to pass through the valley of the shadow of death, they would not be alone, for God would lead them out of the shadows into the eternal sunlight of a new world. - Powell Luke 12:4, 5 Call to Endurance 21:19 Patience: remain under; endurance Endurance will prove one is genuinely saved Endurance will prove they have a supernatural force Endurance will be necessary as the heavy load of persecution is upon the lives of the disciples Endurance means one will not surrender to the circumstances under trial Hupomone does not describe a grim resignation or a "grin and bear" attitude but a triumphant facing of difficult circumstances knowing that even out of evil God guarantees good. Endurance is not about producing salvation; salvation is wholly of grace Endurance proves my salvation is real Darrell Bock - Saving faith does not renounce Jesus; it holds onto him even in the face of persecution. To cease to trust Jesus is to never have trusted him. Judas pictures one who failed. Peter pictures one who lapsed but whose commitment was real. To cling to Jesus is to have life even in the face of death. The opposite of enduring is denying This is not about loss of reward but the loss of eternal life THE PLAN OF THE AGES Luke 21:5-28 PERSECUTION SIGNS 21:12-19 In the short term, this is what life holds for you DEROGOTORY ACCUSATIONS What Jesus is describing is recorded in the book of Acts John 15:20 Persecute pursue with earnestness and diligence Delivered - dragged into synagogues and prisons Not just Jewish courts but also Gentile Persecution because they hate the name of Jesus 2 Corinthians 1:14-16 DIVINE ASSISTANCE 21:13-15 Testimony: witness; English word martyr God s grace will provide in the hour of testing defend a speech given in defense The promise of verbal inspiration 2 Timothy 4:16, 17 Those who suffer for Christ will be defended by Christ DIVIDED ASSOCIATIONS 21:16, 17 To be detested is the general reaction of the ungodly to a Bible believer DESIRED ARRIVAL 21:18. 19 A Christian may die but will not suffer irreparable loss Luke 12:4, 5 Patience: endurance Endurance proves my salvation is real This is not about loss of reward but the loss of eternal life ============================================= === SERMON: message(185).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 2/28/2026 10:00:11 AM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message FLEE WHEN YOU SEE Luke 21:20-24 INTRODUCTION Jesus has concluded His teaching ministry to the masses He has pronounced judgment on the religious system The stage was set in 19:41-44; please note vs. 43, 44 He wreaks havoc on the temple grounds; condemns the chief priests and scribes, 20:19; 46; addresses their refusal to recognized His Lordship, 20:41-44; rejects a religious system that steals from widows, 21:1-4; and responds to the disciples admiration of the temple, 21:5-19 Telling the future is still big business today What is the economic forecast: is this a good time to buy stocks, a house What is the weather forecast: rain, snow, freezing rain; March has it all While sitting in a restaurant Tuesday, the TV was doing a mock football draft Now, predictions are flowing about the upcoming baseball season What is of most interest to some, is what the Bible predicts about the future What is the schedule of events? When will these things take place? For what should we be looking? What are the signs of the times? The disciples asked the same questions 21:7 “when” “what” Acts 1:6 “will you at this time restore the kingdom to Israel?” We highlighted a couple of weeks ago the excitement of many when a 10th nation was added to the European union. People are always trying to identify something that indicates the end is near Books: ‘The Late Great Planet Earth’ Church ‘Prophecy Conferences’ were a big thing at one time Not sure why they died off, other than nothing happened Y2K elevated an awareness of prophecy Maybe somebody decided to take the words of Jesus to heart: Acts 1:7 “It is not for you to know the signs or seasons which the Father has put in His own authority” As they say, it is only the weather man who can maintain a job and be consistently wrong PRESERVATION Jesus has told them how to preserve their souls as the birth pangs prior to the return of Jesus increase: 21:12-19 v. 16 some of you will die but your soul will never be lost beginning in verse 20, Jesus tells them about personal, bodily preservation Jesus says there is a time to face the possibility of death There is also a time when it is best to put on your running shoes What Luke records here is unique to his gospel We will make a comparison with Matthew and Mark in a few minutes Luke writes for us what took place in 70 A.D. Titus and the Roman army thoroughly destroying the city of Jerusalem in the loss of life, burning down the city, destroying the temple, and fulfilling verse 6; “not one stone shall be left upon another” The word is ‘Desolation’ – laid waste, to make inhabitable (19:44) Three key words: ‘When’, ‘Know’, and ‘Flee’ “When you” this is only 40 years after the Resurrection Quite possible for some of the disciples to be alive; might only be in their 60’s “When” – the city surrounded by armies “Then” – an expression of time; then you know by experience Your life depends on your recognition; the sign will not be ambiguous Jesus already predicted it would happen 19:41-44 Now, they have a second warning and a specific sign When you see, then flee. The people are told to do the exact opposite of what one would typically do The instinct would be to seek the safety of a fortified city The question should be asked, if the army is surrounding the city, how were the people to escape? Good question. This prophecy was fulfilled in a very marvelous way. In the year A.D. 66, the first Jewish revolt broke out against the Romans. When the revolt first began, the Roman general in the Land, Cestius Gallus, came with his armies from Caesarea and surrounded Jerusalem. The surrounding of the city marked the sign that Jesus had promised, and the Jewish believers knew that Jerusalem would soon be destroyed. Jesus had commanded the Jewish believers to desert the city when they saw this happening (Lk 21:21). However, it was impossible to do so while the Romans were surrounding the city. Then Cestius Gallus noticed that his supply lines were not secure. He did not have enough supplies to maintain an extended siege, so he lifted the siege of Jerusalem in order to go back to Caesarea. On the way, he was attacked by Jewish forces and killed (AD 67). Temporarily, the city was no longer surrounded by the armies, so every single Jewish believer was able to leave Jerusalem. They crossed the Jordan River and set up a new community of Jewish believers in the town of Pella in the Transjordan. [Pella is about 16 miles south of the Sea of Galilee]They were joined by Jewish believers from Judea, Galilee, and the Golan. There, they waited for the prophecy of Jesus to be fulfilled. - Arnold Fruchtenbaum (born Jewish, converted, kicked out home at 18, 1966 Cedarville grad) When the Roman army returned, Titus had a wall built around the city which prevented anyone else from escaping. OBSERVATION We have an historical account of the fall of Jerusalem in 70 AD by a Jewish observer: Josephus My question is ‘with the utter destruction of the city, how did he survive to write it all down?’ Josephus was born in 37 AD; just 7 years after the Jesus’ resurrection He was born into a wealthy family, was educated well and decided to become a Pharisee We are told he excelled early and was only in his mid-twenties when he was sent to Rome and Nero to seek the release of a few priests He was successful in his appeal but also became quite enamored with the Roman way of life After returning to Jerusalem, he was put in charge of an army division in Galilee. After some initial success against Vespasian, a small group of about 40 retreated to a cave. They refused the idea of surrender and to commit suicide was against Jewish Law. So, Josephus proposed a process where they killed each other. Josephus and one other remained and they decided to surrender. Josephus eventually became an interpreter and intercessor between Rome and the Jews. After the two outer walls of Jerusalem were compromised and knowing the city inhabitants were starving, Titus sent Josephus to encourage surrender. The people refused and the final destruction took place. His parents and wife lost their lives. DISCRIMINATION I need to address the differences in how the gospel writers recorded this part of the Olivet Discourse There is no doubt that Luke uses similar language to Matthew and Mark, but he is clearly speaking of another event Different Wording: desolation of city vs abomination that brings desolation Different Event: focus on the city or the temple Different Warning: flee when you see the army or those in Judea (too late for the city to flee when you see the temple’s desolation ‘great tribulation’ was about to fall Wiersbe: 70 AD was a “dress rehearsal for what will happen when Satan vents his anger on Israel and the believing Gentiles during the last half of the Tribulation” (Rev. 12:7-17) To make Luke’s account equal with Matthew and Mark, it seems to me 3 things must be considered: *Jesus was confused in His teaching as differences are clearly evident *the Holy Spirit was confused when He directed the writing of the gospels *we must twist the accounts to make it fit our preconceived mindset Many commentators admit to the differences in the writings but then try to make them parallel Luke does address some future events beginning in v. 25 FLEE WHEN YOU SEE Luke 21:20-24 PRESERVATION What Luke records here is unique to his gospel Desolation’ – laid waste, to make inhabitable Jesus already predicted it would happen 19:41-44 When you see, then flee. Titus had a wall built around the city OBSERVATION Josephus was born in 37 AD Josephus eventually became an interpreter and intercessor between Rome and the Jews His parents and wife lost their lives DISCRIMINATION Luke uses similar language to Matthew and Mark, but he is clearly speaking of another event Different Wording: desolation of city vs abomination that brings desolation Different Event: focus on the city or the temple Different Warning: when you see the army or when you see the temple’s desolation Wiersbe: 70 AD was a “dress rehearsal for what will happen Rev. 12:7-17 Luke does address some future events beginning in v. 25 ============================================= === SERMON: message(186).txt ============================================= From: "Dwain Hill" Sent: 3/7/2026 10:00:11 AM To: "Jonathan Hill" Subject: message STRAIGHTEN UP Luke 21:22-28 INTRODUCTION It is the last week of the earthly life of Jesus. The dye has been cast. The Jewish leaders are seeking the opportune time for His death. Jesus has condemned them and the religious system they operate. They have been exposed as frauds. Their pride has taken a serious hit. They will soon hit back. Jesus is done talking to them, even to the point of declaring judgement. The day is coming when their city will be thoroughly destroyed. 19:43,44 They have rejected their Messiah. They have been told the vineyard will be taken from them and given to others who will give proper honor to the landowner. There are no more negotiations. It is most interesting that there is never a hint of remorse or repentance We know some among the leaders believed in Jesus but were afraid to openly acknowledge. John 12:42, 19:38 Their hatred blinded them to truth and then a judicial blindness settled upon They were willing to pay 30 pieces of silver to eliminate Jesus from their world The world has not changed much. Sure, technology has greatly altered our lives, but man’s heart remains the same. Deceitful, lost, dark. A heart that would rather go and believe a lie of self-rule, than yield allegiance to another. Who is sovereign is a big deal and one in which we must wrestle with every day. The battle between flesh and spirit continues. And then there are the “principalities and powers in the heavenlies” seeking to rob us of victories and other spiritual advances. Jesus is sitting on a hillside outside of the city of Jerusalem This section of teaching is typically called the Olivet Discourse Only the disciples are part of the lesson Jesus is preparing them for what lies ahead trying to get them to see beyond the immediate. The Kingdom is not coming yet. There are things that must happen before that time. Life will get hard. There will be wars, natural disasters, signs in the heavens, persecutions. They will be hauled in before rulers and kings. They will be betrayed by family members. They will be hated. All because of the name of Jesus. But . . Their souls are safe in the arms of Jesus. 21:19 There will be opportunities to escape physical hardships and death 21:21 The Olivet Discourse Continues THE REASON FOR SUFFERINGS 21:22, 23 “days of vengeance” 21:22 “because” ὅτι Vindication, retribution, punishment This time around it is not about experiencing victory for Israel but vengeance The Instrument of Vengeance : The Roman Army Their participation is not by chance or fate but an act of a faithful God God is faithful to His promise of punishment for Israel’s rejection of Himself God sent His only Son, the anointed One, God came to earth God has used a number of characters throughout history Jeremiah 25:9 Nebuchadnezzar "My servant" Isaiah 44:28 Cyrus: “My shepherd” Think of some of the Judges used by God: Samson particularly comes mind Barak, hiding behind the skirts of Deborah God can and will use who He will Psalm 94:1 “God of vengeance” Isaiah 34:8 "the LORD has a day of vengeance, a year of recompense for the cause of Zion." Here is the fulfillment of what was recently spoken in 19:41-44; 21:5, 6 The judgments of God are holy and right; He makes not mistakes Nothing here is to be seen as self-gratification There is no “got cha” God was wronged and retribution is called for It was written. Vengeance was written in the past and still in effect No specific passage of Scripture is referenced; there are several Leviticus 26:14-16, 31, 32 Hosea 9:7 The days of visitation have come. The days of reckoning have come. Israel will consider the prophet to be a fool, And the man who is inspired to be insane, Because of the abundance of your sins, And because your hostility is great. Difficulty of Travel 21:23 Time of distress: another difference from Matthew’s account Great distress vs. great tribulation Trouble, hardship vs. “such as has not occurred since the beginning of the world” Hardship vs. unprecedented proportions “this land” and “this people” the inhabitants of Judea and Jerusalem The judgment is so thorough that even women would suffer Distress and wrath = judgment THE DURATION OF SUFFERINGS 21:24-26 “times of the Gentiles” occurs only here in the NT Romans 11:25 is similar “the fullness of the Gentiles” “partial hardness” – blindness, callousness, stupidity When is “until”? When is this "partial hardening" no longer "partial" but in fact lifted by God? “when all Israel will be saved” Messiah’s second coming Revelation 11:2 the Gentiles will "tread under foot the holy city for 42 months." 3.5 years; 1,260 days; time, times, and half a time Eric Cline gives us a sense of the Gentiles trampling on Jerusalem writing that "There have been at least 118 separate conflicts in and for Jerusalem during the past four millennia—conflicts that ranged from local religious struggles to strategic military campaigns and that embraced everything in between. Jerusalem has been destroyed completely at least twice, besieged twenty-three times, attacked an additional fifty-two times, and captured and recaptured forty-four times. It has been the scene of twenty revolts and innumerable riots, has had at least five separate periods of violent terrorist attacks during the past century, and has only changed hands completely peacefully twice in the past four thousand years.” Twice the temple was destroyed: 586 B.C. and 70 A.D. Luke does not tell us when the ‘times of the Gentiles’ begins, only when it ends Most conservative commentaries date the beginning at 586 B.C. Jerusalem has never been totally free of Gentile domination to some degree “until” there is coming a day when the Gentiles will no longer trample the city “until” the time is finite, it will come to an end The ‘times of the Gentiles’ refers to the entire time period between the destruction of Jerusalem and the temple (586) and the beginning of the times of the end when the Lord will return. These words separate the prophecy of Jesus concerning ruin of the city and the temple from the eschatological portion of the prophecy The end of the Gentile age will mark the beginning of the Messianic age Acts 1:6, 7 Disciples asked ‘restoring the kingdom now” Jesus did not correct them by saying ‘there will be no kingdom’, but here is your assignment and leave the timing to the Father The partial hardening of Israel refers the nation as a whole, not individuals The partial hardening will continue until the set number of Gentiles are saved In so doing, God will honor the promise to Abraham, “that in Abraham’s seed all the peoples of the earth will be blessed” (Genesis 12:3b) One day the fullness of the Gentiles will be complete, and God will deliver the nation of Israel. Anyone who might have doubts about God’s faithfulness in keeping His promises can consider these passages as important reminders that God has a detailed plan that He is fulfilling patiently and carefully TRANSITION: THE REASON FOR SUFFERINGS; THE DURATION OF SUFFERINGS Jesus has been speaking of the fall of Jerusalem Next, Luke records a short prophetic description of the end times before the return of Jesus THE REDEMPTION FROM SUFFERINGS 21:27, 28 After the signs which are mentioned in v. 25, 26 Remember: Jesus described all the preliminary signs as ‘birth pangs’ The signs will be there, perhaps somewhat separated at first, but will gradually increase in number and intensity Signs in the heavens and on earth This will be a once in a life-time event Mark 13:19 “For those days will be a time of tribulation such as has not occurred since the beginning of the creation which God created until now, and never will. People will know judgment is coming “men’s hearts will fail them” literally: to breath out; faint, die Why? Because of ‘fear’ terror, dread, scared The Romans soldiers at the tomb of Jesus; fell as dead men This will be a great time of distress Daniel 12:1- v. 25 earth personified; distress – gripped by severe emotional distress a holy terror will accompany the return of Christ remember “Chicken Little” Nothing like this was experienced in 70 A.D. This is end-time stuff; this is Jesus’ answer to the second part of the question of the disciples; the when and the what “And then” Jesus is clearing linking His return to the cataclysmic cosmic signs “they shall see the Son of man coming” “The sign of signs will be the Son of Man Himself” Who are ‘they’? the people of the nations just mentioned The fainters, the unbelievers, the believers This is Jesus returning to the Mount of Olives; Zechariah 14 This is Jesus of Revelation 19:15, 16 "Out of His mouth goes a sharp sword, that with it He should smite the nations: and He shall rule them with a rod of iron: and He treads the winepress of the fierceness and wrath of Almighty God. And He has on his vesture and on His thigh a name written, KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS" “O Glorious Day” “Look up” unbend, straighten up; stand tall “Lift up” expectation “because” explanation Redemption: only gospel occurrence; pay in full Redemption requires a Redeemer His is here Your redemption: Jews will be there 1/3 of the nation will be redeemed (Zechariah 13:8, 9) Gentiles will be there; saved in the last days through the 144,000, the two witnesses, the angel in the air "The central theme of redemption in Scripture is that God has taken the initiative to act compassionately on behalf of those who are powerless to help themselves. The New Testament makes clear that divine redemption includes God's identification with humanity in its plight, and the securing of liberation of humankind through the obedience, suffering, death, and resurrection of the incarnate Son.” - Rightemire We were on the slave block and Christ paid the price for our freedom The signs mentioned will be a terror to unbelievers The same signs to the believer can fill him with joy his deliverance from sin, the flesh, and the devil is soon. He will soon bid farewell to sickness, sorrow, death and temptation. The very day when the unconverted man shall lose everything — shall be the day when the believer shall enter on his eternal reward. The very hour when the worldly man's hopes shall perish — shall be the hour when the believer's hope shall be exchanged for joyful certainty and full possession. J. C. Ryle CONLUSION When learning to play defense in basketball, I was told to not watch the opponent eyes or his feet. I was told to watch his navel because his head and feet can make some fakes, but he is going nowhere until his belly button moves. One writer called Jerusalem the political and spiritual ‘navel’ of the world. Until things begin to move there, we ain’t goin’ anywhere. D Jeremiah: “Making predictions is risky business unless you are God. In 1949, the magazine Popular Science predicted, “Computers in the future may weigh no more than 1.5 tons.” In 1977, Ken Olsen, founder of Digital Equipment Corporation, said, “There is no reason anyone would want a computer in their home.” Bill Gates said in 1981, “640K ought to be enough for anybody. According to John Wesley White, the coming again of Christ and the end of the age occupies some 1,845 scriptural verses, and each one offers sure and certain hope for the Christian.” Vance Havner: “We are not just looking for something to happen, we are looking for Someone to come! And when these things begin to come to pass, we are not to drop our heads in discouragement or shake our heads in despair, but rather lift up our heads in delight.” STRAIGHTEN UP Luke 21:22-28 THE REASON FOR SUFFERINGS 21:22, 23 It is not about experiencing victory for Israel but vengeance The Instrument of Vengeance : The Roman Army Psalm 94:1 Isaiah 34:8 The judgments of God are holy and right Vengeance was written in the past and still in effect Leviticus 26:14-16, 31, 32 Hosea 9:7 Difficulty of Travel 21:23 THE DURATION OF SUFFERINGS 21:24-26 Romans 11:25 “partial hardness” – blindness, callousness Revelation 11:2 Luke does not tell us when the ‘times of the Gentiles’ begins, only when it ends The end of the Gentile age will mark the beginning of the Messianic age Acts 1:6, 7 THE REDEMPTION FROM SUFFERINGS 21:27, 28 Jesus described all the preliminary signs as ‘birth pangs’ Signs in the heavens and on earth Mark 13:19 People will know judgment is coming “men’s hearts will fail them” literally: to breath out; faint, die a holy terror will accompany the return of Christ “The sign of signs will be the Son of Man Himself” “We are not just looking for something to happen, we are looking for Someone to come! - Vance Havner
